Part 1 — Section 1

Foundations and Motivation

Why Druid exists, what problems it solves, and the design philosophy behind it.

1 — Why Apache Druid Exists

1.1 The OLAP Problem Space

Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) refers to a class of database workloads characterized by complex aggregation queries across large datasets — GROUP BY, SUM, COUNT, AVG, and filtering across multiple dimensions simultaneously. Unlike OLTP which handles individual row-level reads and writes, OLAP scans millions or billions of rows to produce a single analytical result.

The fundamental tension in analytics systems is between high-volume writes (ingesting millions of events per second from Kafka, log pipelines, or IoT streams) and sub-second reads (powering interactive dashboards where users expect instantaneous drill-downs). Traditional systems force a choice. Druid was built to eliminate that choice.

Why Traditional RDBMS Fail at Analytical Scale

PostgreSQL and MySQL are row-oriented, OLTP-optimized. Running SELECT country, SUM(revenue) FROM events GROUP BY country on 1 billion rows means:

  • Reading every column in every row — row storage reads entire rows even if you need only 2 columns
  • Scanning through B-tree indexes not designed for multi-dimensional filtering
  • Operating on a single machine — read replicas still have single-node scan limits
  • Maintaining MVCC overhead — transaction isolation machinery that OLAP queries don't need

At 100M rows → multi-second latency. At 1B → minutes. At 10B → timeout. The architecture isn't wrong — it's designed for a fundamentally different workload.

Why Data Warehouses Have High Latency

  • Cold starts: BigQuery / Snowflake dynamically allocate compute → 1–5 second overhead
  • Disaggregated storage: data on S3/GCS → network I/O per query
  • Batch-oriented design: Hive and Spark SQL are MapReduce-derived
  • Concurrency limits: most handle 50–100 concurrent queries before queuing

The Need for Purpose-Built Real-Time OLAP

The gap was clear: teams needed a system that could ingest millions of events/sec from Kafka, store TB–PB of time-series data, and serve hundreds of concurrent queries with <1 second P99 latency. That's what "interactive analytics" means in production — a user clicks a dashboard filter and the chart updates in under a second across billions of rows while 200 other users do the same simultaneously.

Figure 1.1 — Where Druid sits in the OLAP landscape: large scale, sub-second latency

1.2 History and Origin of Druid

Apache Druid was born in 2011 at Metamarkets, an ad-tech analytics company. The founding engineers — Eric Tschetter, Fangjin Yang, and Gian Merlino — needed real-time, interactive analytics over billions of ad impression events per day with sub-second latency.

The core insight: trade general-purpose flexibility for extreme query speed on time-series event data. This led to immutable segments, bitmap indexes per dimension value, rollup at ingestion, columnar storage, and separated write/read paths.

YearMilestoneSignificance
2011Created at MetamarketsPurpose-built for ad-tech real-time analytics
2012Open-sourced (Apache License)Community growth begins
2015Apache incubation; Imply.io foundedOriginal creators form commercial company
2018Apache top-level projectProduction-ready at scale
2019GroupBy v2, vectorized executionMajor query performance improvements
2021SQL-based ingestion (MSQ)INSERT/REPLACE via SQL
2022K8s-native discovery, ZK-free modeCloud-native deployment
2023–24Nested JSON columns, MSE maturedSchema flexibility + complex analytics
2025ZooKeeper-free GAFull cloud-native architecture

Key Production Adopters

CompanyUse CaseScale
NetflixViewing analytics, A/B testing10B+ events/day
AirbnbMetrics platform, pricing analyticsMulti-TB daily
LyftOperational dashboards, rider/driver metricsBillions of events/day
PinterestAds analytics, advertiser reportingMulti-PB data
WalmartInventory & pricing optimizationEnterprise-scale
ConfluentKafka monitoring (Confluent Cloud)Cluster metrics at scale

1.3 What Problems Druid Solves Exceptionally Well

  • Sub-second aggregation on billions of rows — columnar storage + bitmap indexes + rollup + time pruning
  • Unified real-time + historical — single query spans 2-second-old and 6-month-old data transparently
  • 200–500 concurrent dashboards — stateless Brokers, independent Historicals, zero global locks
  • Power user exploration — any dimension filter combination without pre-materialized views
  • Replaces Lambda architecture — one ingestion pipeline, one store, one query interface

1.4 What Druid Is NOT Good For

Use CaseWhy Druid StrugglesBetter Alternative
Row-level lookups by PKNo primary key index — scans segmentsCassandra, Redis, DynamoDB
Heavy JOINs across large tablesBroadcast only (<100MB right side)Spark, Trino, StarRocks
OLTP write workloadsImmutable segments — no in-place mutationPostgreSQL, CockroachDB
Full-text searchNo inverted text indexElasticsearch, OpenSearch
Small data (<100GB)Operational overhead not justifiedDuckDB, ClickHouse single-node
⚠️ Common Pitfall Teams often choose Druid when ClickHouse or PostgreSQL would suffice. Druid's operational overhead (ZooKeeper, metadata store, 5+ service types, deep storage) only pays off at terabyte+ scale with real-time ingestion and high concurrency. Under 100GB → DuckDB or single-node ClickHouse gives 90% of the performance with 1% of the ops complexity.
Figure 1.2 — Druid dominates latency, concurrency, ingestion; weak at JOINs and updates
🎯 Interview Tip "When would you choose Druid over ClickHouse?" — Druid when you need: (1) native Kafka streaming with exactly-once, (2) 200+ concurrent sub-second dashboards, (3) rollup at ingestion, (4) S3 deep storage tier. ClickHouse when: stronger JOINs, UPDATE/DELETE, simpler ops, or batch-heavy data.

Section 1 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaDruid's Implementation
OLAP GapHigh writes + sub-second reads simultaneouslyColumnar segments + bitmap indexes + rollup
Sweet SpotTB+ scale, real-time, high concurrencyKafka ingestion, 200+ concurrent dashboards
Anti-patternsJOINs, row lookups, OLTP, small dataUse Spark/Trino for JOINs; Redis for lookups
Part 1 — Section 2

CAP Theorem, Immutability, and Design Trade-offs

Understanding Druid's consistency model, immutability philosophy, and write/read separation.

2 — CAP Theorem, Immutability, and Design Trade-offs

2.1 Druid in the CAP Spectrum

Druid is AP-leaning — it prioritizes Availability and Partition Tolerance over strong Consistency. A newly ingested segment is not immediately visible on all Historicals; the handoff delay is typically 1–60 seconds. But read availability is extremely high due to several architectural choices:

  • Segment replication: each segment on 2+ Historical nodes — losing one doesn't affect reads
  • Stateless Brokers: any Broker serves any query — put behind a load balancer for HA
  • ZooKeeper failure isolation: if ZK fails, in-flight queries still complete; only new segment assignments stall
  • Deep storage durability: S3/GCS = 99.999999999% durability — data survives total cluster loss

The practical trade-off: Druid sacrifices strong consistency and mutability for extreme read performance and availability. You cannot UPDATE a row in place — you overwrite an entire segment's time interval via reindexing or compaction.

Figure 2.1 — Druid sits near the AP edge: eventual consistency, high read availability

2.2 Append-Only Immutability — Druid's Core Philosophy

Every segment in Druid is immutable once published. No ALTER TABLE UPDATE, no row-level delete, no in-place mutation. This is a deliberate engineering choice that enables critical properties:

PropertyHow Immutability Enables It
Lock-free concurrent readsNo writer modifies data being read — zero contention, unlimited concurrency on same segment
Trivial cachingCached result valid forever (until segment replaced by newer version) — no invalidation logic
Safe replicationSimple file copy to another Historical — no replication protocol or conflict resolution needed
Reliable compactionOld segments intact if compaction fails — atomic segment versioning ensures consistency
Memory-mapped I/OOS page cache perfectly utilized — no stale pages since content never changes
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid handle data corrections?" — Cannot update rows in place. Reingest corrected data for the same time interval → creates new segment version → Coordinator overshadows old version → queries see corrected data. REPLACE INTO makes this atomic — old data replaced only after all new segments are published.

2.3 Eventual Consistency — Visibility Timeline

Understanding exactly when ingested data becomes visible to queries is critical for production systems. Druid has several distinct visibility stages:

Figure 2.2 — Handoff gap: 1–60 seconds between task commit and Historical serving
⚙️ Engineering Insight Minimize the handoff gap: (1) reduce druid.coordinator.period from 60s → 10s, (2) use druid.coordinator.loadqueuepeon.type=http for faster segment assignment, (3) ensure fast network to S3 for quick downloads, (4) use druid.serverview.type=http for faster segment discovery.

2.4 Write Path vs Read Path Separation

Druid's most important design decision: complete separation of write and read paths. They share only one artifact — the immutable segment. No write locks, no read contention, no shared mutable state. This allows millions of writes/sec alongside hundreds of sub-second queries.

Figure 2.3 — Write and read paths share only the immutable segment — zero contention
⚙️ Engineering Insight Write/read separation enables independent scaling: add MiddleManagers for ingestion throughput (zero impact on query latency). Add Historicals/Brokers for query capacity (zero impact on ingestion). They run on separate hardware — no resource competition.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid achieve sub-second queries while ingesting millions of events/sec?" — Complete write/read separation. They share only the immutable segment as their interface. No write locks, no read contention. Unlike PostgreSQL where reads and writes compete for the same buffer pool and lock manager.

2.5 Segment Versioning and Atomic Overshadowing

Every segment has a version string (typically a timestamp). New segments for the same interval get a newer version. The Coordinator ensures only the newest version is active — older ones are overshadowed and dropped. This is atomic at the interval level: queries never see a mix of old and new versions.

⚠️ Common Pitfall Segment version explosion: Every REPLACE INTO or compaction creates new versions. Thousands of overshadowed entries bloat the metadata store and slow Coordinator polls. Solution: schedule regular kill tasks to permanently delete overshadowed segment metadata and deep storage files.

Section 2 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaDruid's Implementation
CAP PositionAP-leaning, eventual consistencyHandoff gap 1–60s; extreme read availability
ImmutabilitySegments never change once publishedLock-free reads, trivial caching, safe replication
Data CorrectionOverwrite entire time intervalREPLACE INTO + versioning + overshadowing
Write/Read SplitIndependent paths sharing only segmentsMiddleManagers write, Historicals read, zero contention
Part 2 — Section 3

Core Architecture Overview

Every component in the Druid cluster, how they communicate, and the role each plays.

3 — Core Architecture Overview

3.1 Druid's Distributed Architecture Philosophy

Apache Druid is a distributed, columnar, append-only, time-partitioned OLAP database. Unlike monolithic databases (PostgreSQL) or general compute engines (Spark), Druid decomposes its responsibilities into purpose-built services, each designed for a specific role in the data lifecycle. This decomposition is what enables Druid to achieve sub-second queries on terabyte-scale datasets while simultaneously ingesting millions of events per second.

The core philosophy is separation of concerns:

  • Ingestion (MiddleManager / Indexer) — converting raw events into optimized segments
  • Storage (Deep Storage + Historical) — durable persistence and fast serving
  • Querying (Broker + Router) — SQL parsing, routing, and result merging
  • Coordination (Coordinator + Overlord) — segment management and task scheduling

Horizontal scaling is natural: add more Historical nodes for query capacity, add more MiddleManagers for ingestion throughput, add more Brokers for concurrency. Each component scales independently — there is no single bottleneck.

Figure 3.1 — All Druid components and their communication paths across 4 layers

3.2 The Broker

The Broker is the entry point for all queries — the query router and result aggregator. It is a stateless JVM process that holds no data itself. Its responsibilities form a clear pipeline:

  1. Receive SQL or native JSON queries from clients via HTTP POST
  2. Parse SQL via Apache Calcite → translate to native Druid JSON query
  3. Consult segment metadata (via ZooKeeper / HTTP) to identify which Historicals hold relevant segments
  4. Prune segments by time interval, partition, and min/max metadata
  5. Fan out sub-queries to all relevant Historicals in parallel
  6. Merge partial results from all Historicals into a single final response
  7. Cache the result (L1 in-memory or L3 shared Memcached/Redis)
  8. Return JSON response to client

Because Brokers are stateless, they can be scaled horizontally behind a load balancer. Losing a Broker loses query capacity but no data. A production cluster typically runs 2–8 Brokers depending on concurrent query volume.

⚙️ Engineering Insight The Broker's merge step is where GroupBy queries can become expensive. If a query returns millions of groups (e.g., GROUP BY user_id without LIMIT), the Broker must hold all partial groups in a hash table in heap memory. This is the most common cause of Broker OOM. Always add LIMIT to GroupBy queries.

3.3 Historical Nodes

Historical nodes are the query workhorse — they store and serve immutable segment data. They are the most resource-intensive component and typically comprise 60–80% of the cluster's hardware cost.

  • Downloads segments from Deep Storage to local disk when assigned by the Coordinator
  • Memory-maps segments using mmap — the OS page cache handles hot/cold data automatically
  • Serves queries from Broker fan-outs: scan segments, apply bitmap filters, compute partial aggregations
  • Maintains local segment cache (druid.segmentCache.locations) — segments survive restarts
  • Tiered: define hot/warm/cold tiers with different hardware — SSD for hot (recent data), HDD for warm (older data)

Historicals are stateless with respect to cluster membership: losing a Historical loses query capacity, not data. The Coordinator will reassign its segments to surviving Historicals (or new ones as they start).

Historical Configurationproperties
druid.server.type=historical
druid.server.tier=_default_tier   # or "hot", "warm", "cold"

# Segment cache on local disk — survives restarts
druid.segmentCache.locations=[{"path":"/mnt/ssd/druid/segment-cache","maxSize":"500g"}]

# Processing threads — typically = num_cores - 1
druid.processing.numThreads=15
druid.processing.numMergeBuffers=4
druid.processing.buffer.sizeBytes=536870912  # 512MB per buffer

# JVM — allocate most RAM to page cache, not heap
# 128GB node: 25GB heap, 12GB direct, ~90GB OS page cache
-Xms25g -Xmx25g
-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g
-XX:+UseZGC
🎯 Interview Tip "Why does Druid use memory-mapped files instead of heap-based caches?" — mmap lets the OS manage hot/cold data automatically via its page cache. Hot segments stay in RAM; cold segments are evicted to disk without any GC impact. This avoids GC pauses that would occur if terabytes of segment data lived on the JVM heap. The Historical's heap is small (~20–30GB) relative to total RAM, keeping GC pauses under control.

3.4 MiddleManager and Peons

MiddleManagers are responsible for ingestion tasks — converting raw data into Druid segments. The architecture uses process isolation for reliability:

  • MiddleManager is a worker process that receives task assignments from the Overlord
  • For each task, it forks a Peon JVM process — an isolated JVM running one indexing task
  • The Peon lifecycle: start → ingest → build segment → push to deep storage → publish metadata → exit
  • If a Peon crashes, only that one task fails — other tasks on the same MiddleManager continue unaffected

The Indexer (Druid 0.17+) is an alternative that runs tasks in-process — no Peon JVM forking. This reduces startup overhead and memory waste from JVM duplication, but provides less isolation. Production clusters increasingly use Indexers for their simplicity.

Figure 3.2 — MiddleManager forks isolated Peon JVMs per task, each pushing segments to deep storage

3.5 Coordinator

The Coordinator manages segment distribution across Historical nodes. It is the brain behind segment placement, replication, and lifecycle management:

  • Polls the Metadata Store for published segments on a configurable period (druid.coordinator.period, default 60s)
  • Polls ZooKeeper for alive Historicals and their current segment inventory
  • Computes segment placement based on replication factor, tier rules, and load balancing (cost-based balancing)
  • Sends load/drop instructions to Historicals via ZooKeeper (or HTTP in newer versions)
  • Runs compaction tasks — scheduling automatic compaction of small segments
  • Enforces retention rules — marking segments as unused when they exceed retention policy

The Coordinator is a leader-elected service (via ZK). Two instances run in active/standby — failover takes 5–30 seconds. Loss of the Coordinator doesn't affect running queries; it stalls new segment assignments and compaction.

3.6 Overlord

The Overlord manages ingestion task lifecycle:

  • Accepts task submissions via HTTP API or from Kafka Supervisors
  • Maintains the task queue in the Metadata Store
  • Assigns tasks to MiddleManagers based on available capacity
  • Tracks task status: PENDING → RUNNING → SUCCESS | FAILED
  • In remote task mode (production default): Overlord runs separate from MiddleManagers
⚙️ Engineering Insight Co-locating Coordinator and Overlord is the most common production pattern. Druid 0.22+ supports druid.coordinator.asOverlord.enabled=true — both services share one JVM. This reduces operational complexity without measurable performance impact. Only separate them if you need independent scaling of coordination vs task management (rare, >100 node clusters).

3.7 ZooKeeper — The Coordination Backbone

ZooKeeper provides three critical services to Druid:

  • Service discovery: Brokers discover Historicals and their segment inventories via ZK watches
  • Segment load queue: Coordinator places load/drop instructions in ZK for Historicals to consume
  • Leader election: Coordinator and Overlord use ZK for active/standby leader election

ZK failure impact: Existing queries continue (Brokers have cached segment topology). New segment assignments stall. Leader election stalls. In-flight ingestion tasks continue (they don't depend on ZK for data flow).

Druid 0.22+ supports Kubernetes-native service discovery as a ZK replacement. Set druid.zk.service.enabled=false and druid.discovery.type=k8s. This eliminates ZK as a dependency in cloud-native deployments.

3.8 Metadata Store

An external relational database — MySQL or PostgreSQL (required) — storing:

  • Segment metadata: datasource, interval, version, partition, dimensions, metrics, size, deep storage location
  • Task metadata: task spec, status, timestamps, error messages
  • Supervisor state: Kafka supervisor offsets, configuration
  • Audit logs: who changed what rules, when

Critical: metadata store failure blocks new segment publication and task submission. Use managed services (RDS, Cloud SQL) with automated backups and point-in-time recovery. The metadata store is the single most important backup in a Druid cluster.

⚠️ Common Pitfall Running the metadata store on Kubernetes or unmanaged EC2 instances. If the metadata store is lost without backup, you cannot rediscover which segments exist in deep storage — data recovery becomes extremely expensive (listing all S3 objects and reconstructing metadata). Always use managed RDS with multi-AZ and automated daily snapshots.

3.9 Deep Storage

Deep Storage is the permanent, durable storage layer for all segments — the source of truth. Druid supports S3, GCS, Azure Blob Storage, HDFS, and local disk (dev only).

  • Segments are pushed here by MiddleManagers after ingestion
  • Historicals download segments from deep storage when assigned by the Coordinator
  • Never queried directly — only used for segment transfer between services
  • Cost optimization: use S3 lifecycle rules (Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier) for old segments
  • Segment format: ZIP file containing columnar data files + metadata
Figure 3.3 — How data moves through the Druid cluster from ingestion to query

Section 3 — Key Takeaways

ComponentRoleStateful?Scale Pattern
BrokerQuery routing + mergeNoHorizontal behind LB
HistoricalSegment servingLocal cacheHorizontal — most nodes
MiddleManagerIngestion tasksIntermediate dataHorizontal by task count
CoordinatorSegment assignmentNo (reads metadata)Active/standby
OverlordTask managementNo (reads metadata)Active/standby
Deep StorageDurable segmentsYes — source of truthInfinite (S3)
Metadata StoreSegment + task metadataYes — criticalManaged RDS
ZooKeeperDiscovery + electionYes3–5 node ensemble
Part 2 — Section 4

The Router, Process Co-location, and Deployment Topologies

How to deploy Druid — from single-node dev to 500+ node production clusters.

4 — The Router, Co-location, and Deployment Topologies

4.1 The Router — Query Routing and API Gateway

The Router is an optional component that acts as a unified API gateway. In production, it sits behind an ALB/NLB as the single entry point for all Druid traffic:

  • Routes SQL queries to the Broker pool
  • Routes management API requests to the Coordinator (segment management, rules) or Overlord (task submission)
  • Hosts the Druid Console — the built-in web UI for monitoring and management
  • Supports query prioritization via routing strategies: different query patterns to different Broker tiers
Figure 4.1 — Router distributes traffic to Broker tiers, Coordinator, and Overlord based on request type
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you prevent ad-hoc queries from slowing down dashboards?" — Deploy two Broker tiers: Tier 1 for dashboards (low timeout, high priority) and Tier 2 for ad-hoc (high timeout, lower priority). Route via the Router's managementProxy strategy or use query context "lane": "dashboard" vs "lane": "adhoc".

4.2 Process Co-location Patterns

Druid services can be co-located on the same JVM or separated onto dedicated hardware. The right pattern depends on cluster scale:

PatternCo-locationWhen to Use
DevelopmentAll services in single JVM (start-druid)Local dev, CI testing, learning
Small productionCoordinator + Overlord (one "master" JVM); Broker + Router (one "query" JVM)3–10 nodes, ≤1TB data
Standard productionEvery service type on dedicated hardware10–50 nodes, TB-scale
Large productionMultiple Broker pools, tiered Historicals, dedicated compaction MMs50–500+ nodes, multi-PB
Co-located Master Configurationproperties
# Coordinator + Overlord in one JVM (most common production pattern)
druid.coordinator.asOverlord.enabled=true
druid.coordinator.asOverlord.overlordService=druid/overlord

# This single "master" JVM handles both segment management and task scheduling
# Deploy 2 instances for HA — ZK leader election handles failover

4.3 Deployment Topologies

Figure 4.2 — Four deployment topologies from single-node development to 500+ node production

4.4 Tiered Historical Architecture

In large deployments, Historicals are organized into tiers based on the age and access frequency of the data they serve:

TierHardwareData AgeReplicationPurpose
HotHigh-CPU, SSD, 128–256GB RAMLast 7–30 days2 replicasDashboard queries — sub-200ms P99
WarmStandard CPU, HDD/EBS, 64–128GB RAM30 days – 1 year1–2 replicasAd-hoc exploration — 1–5s acceptable
ColdMinimal CPU, large HDD, 32–64GB RAM1+ years1 replicaRegulatory/audit — 5–30s acceptable
Tiered Historical Configurationproperties
# Hot tier Historical
druid.server.tier=hot
druid.server.priority=10
druid.segmentCache.locations=[{"path":"/mnt/nvme/cache","maxSize":"1t"}]

# Warm tier Historical
druid.server.tier=warm
druid.server.priority=5
druid.segmentCache.locations=[{"path":"/mnt/ebs/cache","maxSize":"2t"}]
Coordinator Tiering RulesJSON
{
  "rules": [
    {
      "type": "loadByInterval",
      "interval": "P30D/now",
      "tieredReplicants": { "hot": 2 }
    },
    {
      "type": "loadByInterval",
      "interval": "P1Y/now",
      "tieredReplicants": { "warm": 1 }
    },
    { "type": "dropForever" }
  ]
}
⚠️ Common Pitfall Setting all data to hot tier "just in case." A 1-year dataset at 50GB/day = 18TB. With 2 replicas on SSD Historicals, that's 36TB of premium SSD storage. Moving data older than 30 days to warm tier (HDD, 1 replica) reduces cost by 70%+ while only affecting query latency for historical data that's rarely accessed.

4.5 Network Communication Patterns

Understanding Druid's network communication patterns is critical for firewall rules, security groups, and network topology design:

SourceDestinationProtocolPurposeLatency Sensitivity
ClientRouter/BrokerHTTP :8888/:8082SQL queriesHigh — user-facing
BrokerHistoricalHTTP :8083Query fan-outVery high — on critical path
BrokerMiddleManagerHTTP :8091Real-time queryHigh
MM/PeonDeep Storage (S3)HTTPS :443Segment pushMedium — async
HistoricalDeep Storage (S3)HTTPS :443Segment downloadMedium — on startup
AllZooKeeperTCP :2181Discovery, electionLow — background
CoordinatorMetadata StoreTCP :3306/5432Segment metadataLow — periodic polls
MM/PeonKafkaTCP :9092Event consumptionHigh — ingestion
⚙️ Engineering Insight The Broker → Historical link is the most latency-sensitive path in the entire system. Every millisecond of added latency here directly impacts query P99. Ensure Brokers and Historicals are in the same availability zone or at minimum the same region. Cross-AZ latency (0.5–2ms per hop) can add 5–20ms to every query when fanning out to 10+ Historicals.

Section 4 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
RouterAPI gateway + query routingSingle entry point behind ALB; route by query type
Co-locationCombine services for simplicityCoord+Overlord in one JVM; separate everything else
TopologiesScale complexity with data volumeStart micro, graduate to standard when needed
TieringMatch hardware cost to data access patternsHot (SSD, 30d), Warm (HDD, 1yr), Cold or drop
NetworkBroker→Historical is critical pathSame AZ, minimal hops, dedicated subnet
Part 3 — Section 5

Segments: Architecture and Internals

The fundamental unit of everything in Druid — storage, query, replication, and lifecycle.

5 — Segments: Architecture and Internals

5.1 What Is a Druid Segment?

The segment is the fundamental unit of data storage and query in Druid. Every architectural decision in Druid revolves around the segment: ingestion builds them, deep storage persists them, Historicals serve them, Coordinators manage them, and Brokers query them.

A segment is an immutable, compressed, columnar file containing a chunk of a datasource's data for a specific time interval. Each segment is uniquely identified by four components:

  • Datasource name — the logical table (e.g., user_events)
  • Time interval — the time range covered (e.g., 2024-03-15T00:00:00/2024-03-16T00:00:00)
  • Version — a timestamp string; newer versions overshadow older ones for the same interval
  • Partition number — when an interval has multiple shards (0, 1, 2, ...)

The full segment identifier looks like: user_events_2024-03-15T00:00:00.000Z_2024-03-16T00:00:00.000Z_2024-03-15T10:30:00.000Z_0

Segments are stored as ZIP files in deep storage and memory-mapped by Historicals for serving. The target segment size in production is 300–700 MB (compressed). Too small → many file opens per query → metadata overhead. Too large → fewer parallelism options → long download times on failover.

⚙️ Engineering Insight The ideal segment size is the sweet spot between parallelism and overhead. At Netflix (10B+ events/day), the standard target is ~500MB per segment. This gives enough segments for parallel scanning across Historicals while keeping per-segment metadata overhead low. Use maxRowsPerSegment (default 5M) and maxTotalRows to control segment size at ingestion.

5.2 Segment Internal Structure

Inside every segment ZIP file is a carefully organized set of columnar data files and index metadata. Understanding this structure is essential for performance tuning and debugging.

Figure 5.1 — Inside a Druid segment: columnar storage, dictionary encoding, and per-value bitmap indexes

5.3 Columnar Storage in Segments

Each column is stored as a separate compressed byte array — this is the foundation of Druid's read performance. A query that references only country and revenue_sum reads only those two columns, skipping all others entirely.

Column TypeStorage FormatCompressionIndex Support
__timeDelta-encoded LONG array (sorted)LZ4Used for time pruning
String dimensionDictionary (string→int) + encoded int arrayLZ4 + front codingBitmap per unique value
Long/Double dimensionCompressed numeric arraysLZ4Optional bitmap
Metric (numeric)Compressed LONG/DOUBLE/FLOAT arraysLZ4 or ZstdNo bitmap (aggregation only)
Complex (sketch)Serialized binary blobsNone (already compact)No bitmap
Nested JSONAuto-flattened sub-columns per JSON pathLZ4 + dictionaryBitmap per path value

5.4 Segment Granularity

Segments are time-partitioned by segmentGranularity — the coarseness of the time intervals each segment covers. This is one of the most impactful configuration decisions:

GranularitySegments/YearBest ForTrade-off
HOUR8,760High-volume streaming (>1M events/sec)Many segments — high metadata overhead
DAY365Most production workloads — default recommendationGood balance of parallelism and overhead
WEEK52Moderate volume, queries span weeksLarge segments — less parallelism
MONTH12Low volume, long retentionVery large segments — slow compaction
⚙️ Engineering Insight Choose granularity matching your most common query time range. If dashboards typically query "last 7 days," use DAY granularity — this means 7 segments to scan, each optimally sized. If you use HOUR, you'd scan 168 segments — more parallelism but more file-open overhead. If dashboards query "last 1 hour," then HOUR is optimal.

5.5 Segment Partitioning (Sharding)

When data for a single time interval exceeds the target segment size, Druid splits it into multiple partitions (shards). The partitioning strategy determines how rows are distributed across shards — and directly impacts query performance:

Shard TypeHow Rows Are AssignedUsed ByQuery Benefit
NumberedShardSpecSequential assignment as rows arriveStreaming ingestion (default)None — all shards scanned
HashBasedNumberedHash of selected dimension(s)Batch ingestion, compactionPartition pruning on hash dimension
SingleDimensionRange partitioning on one dimensionBatch ingestion (CLUSTERED BY)Strong pruning for range/equality filters
DimensionRangeMulti-dimension range partitioningBatch ingestion (advanced)Pruning on multiple dimensions
SQL-Based Ingestion with CLUSTERED BYSQL
INSERT INTO "user_events"
SELECT
    TIME_PARSE("event_time") AS __time,
    "country", "platform", "event_type",
    COUNT(*) AS event_count,
    SUM("revenue") AS revenue_sum
FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...))
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
PARTITIONED BY DAY          -- segmentGranularity = DAY
CLUSTERED BY "country"      -- range-partition shards by country
                              -- WHERE country='US' skips non-US shards entirely
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you optimize a Druid query that filters on country?" — Use CLUSTERED BY country at ingestion time. This creates range-partitioned shards where all "US" data is in shard 0, "UK" in shard 1, etc. When the Broker sees WHERE country='US', it prunes all non-US shards — potentially eliminating 90%+ of segments before any data is read.

5.6 Front Coding — String Dictionary Compression

Druid 25+ supports front coding for string dimension dictionaries. Strings sharing a common prefix (URLs, file paths, geo names) store the prefix once and only the suffix per entry. This achieves 30–70% dictionary size reduction.

Figure 5.2 — Front coding reduces dictionary size by 60% for URL-like string dimensions
Enable Front CodingJSON
// In ingestion spec → tuningConfig or datasource-level default
"stringDictionaryEncoding": {
    "type": "frontCoded",
    "bucketSize": 4    // Group 4 entries per bucket; 4 is a good default
}
// Trade-off: slightly slower dictionary lookups (~5% on exact match)
// but 30–70% smaller dictionaries for URL/path-heavy dimensions

Section 5 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
SegmentImmutable columnar file, 300–700MBTarget 5M rows, ~500MB per segment
Columnar storageRead only columns needed by queryMinimize dimensions to what's queried
GranularityTime partition per segmentDAY for most; HOUR for high-volume streaming
PartitioningSharding within a time intervalCLUSTERED BY your most-filtered dimension
Front codingPrefix dedup in string dictionariesEnable for URL/path dimensions → 30–70% savings
Part 3 — Section 6

Bitmap Indexes — The Secret to Druid's Speed

How Druid evaluates WHERE clauses in nanoseconds — without scanning a single row.

6 — Bitmap Indexes — The Secret to Druid's Speed

6.1 Roaring Bitmap Architecture

For each unique value in each string dimension column, Druid maintains a Roaring Bitmap — a compressed bitset where bit i = 1 means row i contains that value. This is the single most important data structure in Druid and the primary reason it achieves sub-second query latency.

Roaring Bitmaps are a hybrid compressed bitmap format that uses three container types internally:

  • Array containers — for sparse data (few set bits): stores a sorted list of row indices. Space: 2 bytes per set bit.
  • Bitmap containers — for dense data (many set bits): stores a raw 8KB bitset for a 64K-row block. Space: fixed 8KB regardless of density.
  • Run-length containers — for runs of consecutive set bits: stores start + length pairs. Space: 4 bytes per run.

The hybrid approach gives 10–100× compression over raw bitmaps for typical dimension cardinalities. A dimension with 1000 unique values on a 5M-row segment might use only 5–50MB of bitmap space total.

6.2 Filter Evaluation via Bitmaps

This is where the magic happens. When Druid evaluates a WHERE clause, it doesn't scan rows — it performs bitwise operations on bitmaps:

2 rows read — 66% eliminated At scale: 5,000,000 rows → ~100,000 read — 98% eliminated Filter resolved in nanoseconds via bitwise AND — no row scanning Traditional DB approach: Scan all 6 rows, evaluate each WHERE → O(N) per filter, sequential Druid bitmap approach: Lookup 2 bitmaps, AND them, read matches → O(1) lookup + O(bitmap_size) AND
Figure 6.1 — Bitmap AND eliminates 98%+ of rows before any data is read at scale

The key insight: filter evaluation cost is independent of table size. Whether the segment has 1 million or 100 million rows, the bitmap AND takes the same number of operations (proportional to bitmap size, not row count). This is fundamentally different from B-tree index lookups which scale with data size.

Multi-filter combinations work naturally:

  • WHERE A AND B → bitwise AND of two bitmaps
  • WHERE A OR B → bitwise OR
  • WHERE NOT A → bitwise complement
  • WHERE (A AND B) OR (C AND NOT D) → nested bitwise operations

These operations are SIMD-accelerated on modern CPUs — processing 64+ rows per CPU instruction via AVX2/SSE instructions.

6.3 Bitmap Index Memory and Cardinality Impact

Each unique value = one bitmap. This creates a direct relationship between dimension cardinality and segment memory footprint:

CardinalityBitmap Memory (5M rows)ImpactRecommendation
<100~50 KBNegligible✅ Ideal — bitmap at its best
1,000~500 KBMinor✅ Standard — works great
100,000~50 MBModerate⚠️ Consider if needed in WHERE
1,000,000~500 MBSignificant⚠️ Disable bitmap if not filtered
>10,000,000~5+ GBSevere❌ Anti-pattern — use sketches instead
Disable Bitmap for High-Cardinality DimensionJSON
// In dimensionsSpec — skip bitmap for user_id (100M+ unique)
"dimensions": [
    "country",        // low cardinality — keep bitmap
    "platform",       // low cardinality — keep bitmap
    {
        "type": "string",
        "name": "user_id",
        "createBitmapIndex": false   // 100M+ unique → skip bitmap
    }                            // Use HLLSketch for distinct count instead
]
⚠️ Common Pitfall Including user_id, session_id, or request_id as normal string dimensions. With 100M+ unique values, each segment's bitmap set can exceed 5GB — larger than the actual data. These columns should either have "createBitmapIndex": false or (better) be ingested as HLLSketch/ThetaSketch metrics for approximate distinct counting.

6.4 Bitmap vs B-Tree — Why Druid Chose Bitmaps

This is a foundational architectural decision worth understanding deeply:

PropertyBitmap Index (Druid)B-Tree Index (PostgreSQL)
Multi-column filterO(bitmap_size) — AND/OR bitmaps in nanosecondsO(N × log N) — index intersection, expensive
Single-column equalityO(1) bitmap lookupO(log N) tree traversal
Range filter (numeric)Bounds-check on compressed arrays (OK, not ideal)O(log N + K) — excellent with sorted data
Insert performanceN/A — bitmaps built at segment creationO(log N) per insert — requires balancing
Update supportN/A — immutableFull support — MVCC overhead
Memory efficiencyRoaring compression — 10–100× vs rawFixed tree structure — less compressible
Best forMulti-dim analytics with AND/OR filtersOLTP point lookups and range scans
🎯 Interview Tip "Why does Druid use bitmap indexes instead of B-trees?" — Druid queries typically filter on multiple dimension combinations simultaneously (country + platform + device_type). Bitmaps combine N filters in O(1) per row via bitwise operations. A B-tree would require N separate index lookups and a set intersection — orders of magnitude slower for this workload. Additionally, bitmaps are built once at segment creation and never updated, which aligns perfectly with Druid's immutable segment design.

6.5 Bitmap Performance in Practice

To illustrate the real-world impact, consider a production query on a segment with 5 million rows:

Figure 6.2 — Three bitmap ANDs reduce 5M rows to 45K (99.1% eliminated) before any metric data is read

6.6 Advanced: Concise vs Roaring Bitmaps

Druid historically supported two bitmap implementations:

  • Concise bitmaps (legacy default): Word-aligned hybrid — good compression but slower operations
  • Roaring bitmaps (current default, Druid 0.16+): hybrid array/bitmap/run containers — faster operations AND often better compression

Roaring bitmaps outperform Concise in virtually all workloads. If you're running an older cluster with Concise bitmaps, switching to Roaring via reindexing is one of the easiest performance wins available.

Force Roaring Bitmaps (if running legacy segments)properties
# Global default — new segments will use Roaring
druid.processing.bitmap.type=roaring

# To convert existing Concise segments → Roaring:
# Run compaction on the datasource — new compacted segments use Roaring
⚙️ Engineering Insight You can inspect bitmap statistics per segment via the /druid/v2/datasources/{name}/segments/{segmentId} API. Key metrics: bitmapSerdeFactory (Roaring vs Concise), per-column bitmap sizes, and dictionary sizes. This data is invaluable for identifying high-cardinality dimensions inflating segment sizes.

Section 6 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
Roaring BitmapsCompressed bitset per unique value per dimensionEnsure Roaring (not Concise) on all segments
Filter evaluationBitwise AND/OR — O(1) per row, SIMD-acceleratedMulti-column filters are effectively free
Cardinality impactEach unique value = one bitmapDisable bitmaps for >1M cardinality dims
vs B-treeBitmaps excel at multi-dim AND/OR combosPerfect for dashboard "filter by everything" pattern
99%+ eliminationMost rows skipped before any metric data is readMore selective filters = faster queries
Part 4 — Section 7

Ingestion Architecture — Streaming and Batch

The three roads into Druid: Kafka streaming, native batch, and SQL-based ingestion.

7 — Ingestion Architecture — Streaming and Batch

7.1 Ingestion Overview

Druid provides three distinct ingestion paths, each optimized for different use cases. Understanding which to use — and when — is a critical production decision:

  • Streaming (Kafka / Kinesis): Continuous real-time ingestion — data queryable within seconds of arrival. The most common production pattern.
  • Native batch (index_parallel): Read from S3/HDFS/GCS files. Used for historical backfills, data corrections, and sources without Kafka.
  • SQL-based (MSQ — INSERT/REPLACE): Write SQL that reads from external sources and creates segments. The modern, preferred approach for batch operations.

All three paths converge at the same destination: immutable segments in deep storage. After ingestion, segments are published to the metadata store, discovered by the Coordinator, assigned to Historicals, and become queryable.

Figure 7.1 — All three ingestion paths converge at immutable segments in deep storage

7.2 Streaming Ingestion — Kafka Supervisor

Native Kafka ingestion is the most common production pattern. A Kafka Supervisor is a persistent process on the Overlord that manages a set of indexing tasks consuming from Kafka topic partitions.

Key behavior:

  • The supervisor creates taskCount tasks, each reading from a subset of Kafka partitions
  • Each task builds segments in memory, persisting to local disk periodically (intermediatePersistPeriod)
  • After taskDuration elapses (default 1 hour), the task commits: merges persists → pushes to S3 → publishes metadata → exits
  • The supervisor immediately starts new replacement tasks from the committed Kafka offsets
  • replicas parameter creates duplicate tasks for ingestion HA — if one replica fails, the other continues
Kafka Supervisor Spec — Production ExampleJSON
{
  "type": "kafka",
  "spec": {
    "dataSchema": {
      "dataSource": "user_events",
      "timestampSpec": { "column": "event_time", "format": "iso" },
      "dimensionsSpec": {
        "dimensions": [
          "user_id", "event_type", "platform", "country",
          { "type": "long", "name": "session_id" }
        ]
      },
      "metricsSpec": [
        { "type": "count", "name": "event_count" },
        { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "revenue_sum", "fieldName": "revenue" },
        { "type": "HLLSketchBuild", "name": "unique_users", "fieldName": "user_id" }
      ],
      "granularitySpec": {
        "segmentGranularity": "HOUR",
        "queryGranularity": "MINUTE",
        "rollup": true
      }
    },
    "ioConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "consumerProperties": { "bootstrap.servers": "kafka-1:9092,kafka-2:9092" },
      "topic": "user_events_prod",
      "taskCount": 4,          // 4 tasks — match Kafka partition count / 4
      "replicas": 2,            // 2 replicas for ingestion HA
      "taskDuration": "PT1H"    // Tasks commit and restart every hour
    },
    "tuningConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "maxRowsPerSegment": 5000000,
      "maxRowsInMemory": 1000000,         // Buffer 1M rows before persist
      "intermediatePersistPeriod": "PT10M"  // Persist to disk every 10 min
    }
  }
}

7.3 Streaming Ingestion Internals — Task Handoff

Figure 7.2 — Task generations overlap: Gen 1 hands off while Gen 2 begins ingesting
⚙️ Engineering Insight Sizing taskCount: A common rule is taskCount = Kafka partitions / 4 (each task reads ~4 partitions). If you have 32 Kafka partitions, start with taskCount=8. More tasks = more parallelism but more MiddleManager resource usage. Monitor ingest/kafka/lag — if lag grows, increase taskCount or add MiddleManagers.

7.4 Batch Ingestion — Native Parallel Index Task

For historical data or sources without Kafka, Druid provides native batch ingestion. The index_parallel task type is the production-recommended approach — it launches multiple sub-tasks for parallel data reading and segment building.

Parallel Batch Ingestion from S3 ParquetJSON
{
  "type": "index_parallel",
  "spec": {
    "dataSchema": {
      "dataSource": "orders_historical",
      "timestampSpec": { "column": "order_ts", "format": "posix" },
      "dimensionsSpec": {
        "dimensions": ["order_id", "customer_id", "product_id", "region"]
      },
      "metricsSpec": [
        { "type": "count", "name": "order_count" },
        { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "amount_sum", "fieldName": "amount" }
      ],
      "granularitySpec": {
        "segmentGranularity": "DAY",
        "queryGranularity": "HOUR",
        "rollup": true,
        "intervals": ["2024-01-01/2024-12-31"]  // Required for batch
      }
    },
    "ioConfig": {
      "type": "index_parallel",
      "inputSource": { "type": "s3", "prefixes": ["s3://bucket/orders/year=2024/"] },
      "inputFormat": { "type": "parquet" }
    },
    "tuningConfig": {
      "type": "index_parallel",
      "maxNumConcurrentSubTasks": 8,   // Parallel reading threads
      "maxRowsPerSegment": 5000000,
      "partitionsSpec": { "type": "hashed", "numShards": 4 }
    }
  }
}

7.5 Choosing the Right Ingestion Method

CriteriaKafka StreamingNative BatchSQL (MSQ)
Data freshnessSecondsMinutes–hoursMinutes
Rollup qualityBest-effort (per task window)Perfect (all data available)Perfect
Exactly-onceYes (atomic offset + metadata)Yes (idempotent versions)Yes (atomic REPLACE)
Source typesKafka, KinesisS3, GCS, HDFS, HTTPAny EXTERN source
SchemaJSON ingestion specJSON ingestion specSQL (much simpler)
Use caseReal-time dashboardsBackfills, migrationsBackfills, corrections, ETL
RecommendedPrimary ingestion pathLegacy / specific sourcesModern batch (preferred)
⚠️ Common Pitfall Using native batch (index_parallel) for regular data corrections when SQL-based REPLACE INTO is available. MSQ is simpler to write, debug, and monitor. The JSON ingestion spec is verbose and error-prone. Modern Druid (25+) should use SQL-based ingestion for all batch operations.

Section 7 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
StreamingKafka Supervisor → continuous tasksPrimary path — data queryable in seconds
BatchRead files → build segmentsUse SQL (MSQ) instead for new deployments
Exactly-onceAtomic commit of offsets + metadataGuaranteed in all three paths
taskCountParallelism for Kafka ingestionKafka partitions / 4; scale by lag metric
Part 4 — Section 8

Rollup — Pre-Aggregation at Ingestion Time

How Druid collapses billions of raw events into millions of pre-aggregated rows — 100× storage reduction.

8 — Rollup — Pre-Aggregation at Ingestion Time

8.1 What Is Rollup?

Rollup is Druid's ability to pre-aggregate rows with identical dimension values and timestamp (rounded to queryGranularity) at ingestion time. Instead of storing 1 billion raw events, Druid can store N million pre-aggregated rows — dramatically reducing both storage and query cost.

50× reduction
Figure 8.1 — Rollup collapses rows with identical dimensions + time bucket into pre-aggregated rows

8.2 Rollup Ratio — The Key Efficiency Metric

The rollup ratio (raw_event_count / rolled_up_row_count) is the single best indicator of how efficiently Druid is storing your data. Higher ratios mean smaller segments, faster queries, and lower costs.

queryGranularityTypical Row Count (1B raw/day)Rollup RatioStorage Impact
NONE (no rollup)1,000,000,0001×Baseline — maximum storage
SECOND~800,000,000~1.25×Minimal improvement
MINUTE~20,000,000~50×Excellent for most use cases
HOUR~330,000~3,000×Massive reduction — common for dashboards
DAY~14,000~71,000×Maximum rollup — aggregated metrics only

How to improve rollup ratio:

  • Coarsen queryGranularity: MINUTE → HOUR gives 10–100× improvement
  • Remove high-cardinality dimensions: dropping user_id from dimensions collapses millions of unique combos
  • Use sketches instead of raw values: replace user_id dimension with HLLSketchBuild metric
  • Reduce dimension cardinality: map browser_version (10K+ values) to browser_family (5 values) at ingestion
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid maintain analytics accuracy with pre-aggregation?" — After rollup, exact values are lost — you can't compute exact COUNT DISTINCT from pre-summed rows. The solution is probabilistic sketches (HLL, Theta, Quantile) ingested as metrics. Sketches are mergeable: the sketch from rolled-up group A + group B produces the same approximate result as computing from raw events. This is how Druid delivers both pre-aggregation AND cardinality/percentile analytics.

8.3 Perfect Rollup vs Best-Effort Rollup

PropertyPerfect RollupBest-Effort Rollup
WhenBatch ingestion (all data available)Streaming (data arrives over time)
GuaranteeAll rows with identical dims+time are collapsedOnly within single task's memory window
Fix for streamingRun compaction after streamingCompaction achieves perfect rollup
Example ratio50× (all data seen at once)5–10× (partial windows)
⚙️ Engineering Insight The typical production pattern is: Kafka streaming with best-effort rollup → automatic compaction achieves perfect rollup. Streaming ingestion creates many small segments with imperfect rollup. The Coordinator's auto-compaction merges these into fewer, optimally rolled-up segments. This two-phase approach gives you real-time freshness AND optimal storage efficiency.

Section 8 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
RollupCollapse identical dimension+time rowsAlways enable — 10–1000× storage reduction
Rollup ratioraw_count / rolled_up_countMonitor this metric per datasource
queryGranularityTime bucket for rollupMINUTE for most; HOUR for dashboards
SketchesMergeable approximate metricsAlways use HLL for distinct counts
CompactionPerfect rollup after streamingEnable auto-compaction on all datasources
Part 4 — Section 9

SQL-Based Ingestion (INSERT / REPLACE via MSQ)

The modern, SQL-native approach to loading data into Druid — atomic, debuggable, and powerful.

9 — SQL-Based Ingestion (INSERT / REPLACE via MSQ)

9.1 INSERT INTO — Append New Data

INSERT INTO appends new segments without affecting existing data. It's the SQL equivalent of batch ingestion — but vastly simpler to write and debug.

INSERT INTO — Append from S3 JSONSQL
INSERT INTO "user_events"
SELECT
    TIME_PARSE("event_time") AS __time,
    "user_id",
    "event_type",
    "platform",
    "country",
    COUNT(*) AS "event_count",
    SUM("revenue") AS "revenue_sum"
FROM TABLE(
    EXTERN(
        '{"type":"s3","prefixes":["s3://bucket/events/2024-01/"]}',
        '{"type":"json"}',
        '[{"name":"event_time","type":"string"},
          {"name":"user_id","type":"string"},
          {"name":"event_type","type":"string"},
          {"name":"platform","type":"string"},
          {"name":"country","type":"string"},
          {"name":"revenue","type":"double"}]'
    )
)
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4, 5     -- GROUP BY enables rollup
PARTITIONED BY DAY              -- segmentGranularity = DAY
CLUSTERED BY "country", "platform"  -- range-partition shards

9.2 REPLACE INTO — Atomic Interval Overwrite

REPLACE INTO atomically overwrites all data in the specified time interval. Old segments are only overshadowed after all new segments are successfully published. If the task fails midway, no data is lost or corrupted. This is the preferred method for:

  • Data corrections: reprocess and replace a day's data with corrected values
  • Backfills: load historical data with perfect rollup and optimal partitioning
  • Schema migration: reingest with new dimensions or changed types
REPLACE INTO — Atomic Overwrite for January 2024SQL
REPLACE INTO "user_events"
  OVERWRITE WHERE __time >= TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01'
                   AND __time < TIMESTAMP '2024-02-01'
SELECT
    TIME_PARSE("event_time") AS __time,
    "event_type", "platform", "country",
    COUNT(*) AS event_count,
    SUM("revenue") AS revenue_sum
FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...))
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
PARTITIONED BY DAY
CLUSTERED BY "country"

-- Atomic: old January segments ONLY replaced after ALL new ones are published
-- If task fails midway → old data still intact, zero downtime
⚙️ Engineering Insight REPLACE INTO is the safest data correction mechanism in Druid. Unlike manual segment deletion + reingestion (which has a visibility gap), REPLACE is atomic — queries always see either all-old or all-new data, never a mix. This is critical for production dashboards that cannot tolerate data gaps.

9.3 MSQ Ingestion Architecture

Figure 9.1 — MSQ Controller orchestrates parallel Workers that read external data, aggregate, and build segments

9.4 Exactly-Once Semantics Across All Paths

Ingestion PathExactly-Once MechanismFailure Behavior
Kafka streamingAtomic commit of Kafka offset + segment metadata in one transactionCrash before commit → offsets roll back → re-read
Native batchSegment version locking — same version can't be published twiceRetry produces same version → idempotent
SQL REPLACEAll-or-nothing: new segments published atomically, then old overshadowedPartial failure → old segments still active

9.5 MSQ Tuning Parameters

MSQ Context ParametersSQL
INSERT INTO "user_events"
SELECT ...
FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...))
PARTITIONED BY DAY
CLUSTERED BY "country"

-- Tuning via query context:
-- { "maxNumTasks": 10,             -- total tasks (controller + workers)
--   "rowsPerSegment": 5000000,     -- target rows per output segment
--   "taskAssignment": "auto",      -- let MSQ decide worker placement
--   "durableShuffleStorage": true   -- use S3 for shuffle (production) 
-- }
⚠️ Common Pitfall Running MSQ ingestion during peak query hours. MSQ workers run on MiddleManagers — the same infrastructure as streaming ingestion. A large REPLACE job with maxNumTasks=20 can starve Kafka ingestion tasks. Solution: schedule REPLACE jobs during off-peak hours, or use dedicated MiddleManagers for batch (workerCategory config in Druid 28+).
🎯 Interview Tip "When would you use INSERT vs REPLACE?" — INSERT appends new data (e.g., daily ETL adding yesterday's data). REPLACE overwrites existing data atomically (e.g., correcting a bug in last week's data, schema migration). REPLACE is always safe — it never leaves gaps because old segments persist until new ones are fully published.

Section 9 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
INSERT INTOAppend new segmentsDaily batch ETL, no existing data affected
REPLACE INTOAtomic overwrite of time intervalCorrections, backfills, migrations — zero downtime
MSQ EngineDistributed execution on MiddleManagersSet maxNumTasks to avoid starving streaming
CLUSTERED BYRange-partition output segmentsAlways use for your most-filtered dimension
Exactly-onceAtomic metadata publishSafe by default — no special config needed
Part 5 — Section 10

Druid Query Architecture and Execution

From SQL string to sub-second JSON response — every step in the query pipeline.

10 — Druid Query Architecture and Execution

10.1 Query Types — SQL and Native API Taxonomy

Druid exposes two query interfaces: a SQL endpoint (parsed by Apache Calcite) and a native JSON API. Internally, all SQL queries are translated to native JSON. Understanding the native types is critical because they determine which optimizations are applied:

Native Query TypeWhen UsedPerformanceKey Characteristics
TimeseriesGROUP BY only on TIME_FLOOR(__time)FastestNo dimension grouping — pure time-series aggregation. Single-pass scan.
TopNSingle dimension + ORDER BY metric + LIMITVery fastApproximate top-K algorithm — 3× faster than GroupBy for this pattern.
GroupBy (v2)Multi-dimension GROUP BYFastHash-based aggregation. Spills to disk for large cardinality. Most common type.
ScanSELECT * or SELECT without aggregationModerateReturns raw rows. Used for data exploration and debugging.
SearchDimension value search / autocompleteFastSearches dimension dictionaries directly — no data scan.
SegmentMetadataIntrospection of segment schemaInstantReturns column types, sizes, min/max — reads metadata only.
TimeBoundaryMin/max time of a datasourceInstantReads segment metadata — no data scan.
Figure 10.1 — All Druid query types and their relative performance. Calcite maps SQL to the optimal native type.
⚙️ Engineering Insight The query type Calcite selects has a 3–10× performance impact. A query like SELECT country, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY country ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10 maps to TopN (very fast). But if you add a second dimension — GROUP BY country, platform — it falls back to GroupBy (slower). Always check EXPLAIN PLAN to verify which native type Calcite chose.

10.2 Query Execution Flow — 8 Phases

Every Druid query follows the same 8-phase pipeline. Understanding this flow is essential for debugging slow queries and optimizing performance:

Figure 10.2 — The complete 8-phase query path. Step 5 (Historical scan) dominates latency — optimize there first.

Where time is spent: Steps 1–3 (parse, plan, prune) take ~5ms combined. Step 4 (fan-out) adds ~1ms of network overhead. Step 5 (Historical scan) consumes 80–95% of total query time — this is where bitmap filtering, column scanning, and partial aggregation happen. Step 7 (Broker merge) is typically <10ms unless the result set is very large (millions of groups).

The implication: all performance optimization should focus on reducing work in Step 5 — tighter time filters (fewer segments), better partitioning (fewer shards scanned), rollup (fewer rows per segment), and vectorized execution (faster per-row processing).

10.3 Scatter-Gather vs Multi-Stage Execution

Druid has two fundamentally different execution models. Understanding which one activates for a given query is critical for latency prediction:

PropertyScatter-Gather (Default)Multi-Stage (MSE)
How it worksBroker fans out to all Historicals in parallel, each returns partial results, Broker mergesController task creates a multi-stage DAG of worker tasks with shuffles between stages
Latency50–500ms (sub-second)5–60 seconds (batch-like)
TriggersSimple GROUP BY, TopN, Timeseries, ScanWindow functions, complex subqueries, JOINs, INSERT/REPLACE
Runs onBroker + HistoricalsMiddleManagers (task-based)
Use forReal-time dashboardsAnalytical reports, data engineering
⚠️ Common Pitfall Adding a window function like ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) to a dashboard query. This silently triggers MSE instead of scatter-gather — query latency jumps from 200ms to 15 seconds. Always check EXPLAIN PLAN: if you see "queryType": "query_controller", it's MSE. For real-time dashboards, pre-compute window results in ETL instead.

10.4 Query Context Parameters

Query context parameters control execution behavior per-query. They're sent alongside the SQL and override cluster defaults:

Key Query Context ParametersJSON
{
  "query": "SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) FROM user_events WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 100",
  "context": {
    "timeout": 30000,               // Max query time in ms (default 300000)
    "priority": 0,                  // Lower = higher priority (default 0)
    "lane": "interactive",           // Query lane for resource isolation
    "useCache": true,               // Read from segment-level cache
    "populateCache": true,          // Write results to cache
    "useResultLevelCache": true,    // Broker-level full result cache
    "vectorize": "force",            // Force vectorized execution (error if can't)
    "maxScatterGatherBytes": 157286400,  // 150MB max scatter-gather (OOM guard)
    "numMergeBuffers": 4            // Merge buffers for GroupBy (default 2)
  }
}

10.5 Understanding Query Latency Breakdown

PhaseTypical LatencyWhat Affects ItHow to Optimize
① Client → Broker1–3msNetwork, TLS handshakeConnection pooling, keep-alive
② Calcite parse + plan1–5msQuery complexityUse native JSON for ultra-low latency
③ Segment pruning0.1–2msSegment count, metadata sizeTight time filters, CLUSTERED BY
④ Fan-out0.5–2msNumber of HistoricalsSame-AZ deployment
⑤ Historical scan50–500msSegment count, rows, cardinalityRollup, bitmaps, vectorize, pruning
⑥ Result transfer1–10msResult size, networkLIMIT, fewer dimensions
⑦ Broker merge1–50msGroup count, sort complexityAdd LIMIT, use TopN pattern
⑧ Response0.5–2msSerialization, TLSReduce result size
🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through what happens when a user clicks a dashboard chart in a Druid-powered system." — The dashboard fires an HTTP POST with SQL to the Broker. Calcite parses the SQL into an AST, validates it, and translates it to a native Druid query (e.g., TopN for single-dim with LIMIT). The Broker prunes 99% of segments using the time filter, fans out the query in parallel to 3 Historicals. Each Historical memory-maps the relevant segments, evaluates bitmap filters in nanoseconds (eliminating 95%+ of rows), reads only the needed columns for matching rows, computes a partial aggregation, and returns the partial result. The Broker merges all partials, applies ORDER BY and LIMIT, caches the result, and returns JSON — all in 100–500ms.

10.6 Query Concurrency and Resource Model

Each Historical has a fixed pool of processing threads (druid.processing.numThreads, typically num_cores - 1). Each concurrent query segment-scan consumes one processing thread. When all threads are busy, new queries queue. This is the fundamental concurrency model:

  • Concurrency per Historical ≈ numThreads / segments_per_query
  • If a query touches 10 segments on one Historical and numThreads=15, that Historical can serve ~1.5 concurrent instances of that query
  • To increase concurrency: add more Historicals (distributes segments) or add more Brokers (handles more client connections)
  • Query lanes partition the thread pool: interactive: 70%, batch: 30% — prevents batch queries from starving dashboards
Query Lane Configurationproperties
# Define two lanes: interactive (dashboards) and batch (ad-hoc/reports)
druid.query.scheduler.laning.type=manual
druid.query.scheduler.laning.lanes={"interactive": 0.7, "batch": 0.3}

# Dashboard queries use: {"context": {"lane": "interactive", "timeout": 5000}}
# Ad-hoc queries use:    {"context": {"lane": "batch", "timeout": 60000}}

# Result: even if batch queries saturate their 30% of threads,
# dashboards still have 70% — no noisy-neighbor effect

Section 10 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
Query typesTimeseries > TopN > GroupBy > ScanCheck EXPLAIN PLAN — ensure optimal native type
8-phase pipelineParse → prune → fan-out → scan → merge95% of latency is in Historical scan (phase 5)
Scatter-gather vs MSESub-second vs seconds-minutesWindow functions → MSE. Never in dashboards.
Query contextPer-query execution tuningSet timeout, lane, vectorize for all dashboard queries
ConcurrencyFixed thread pool per HistoricalUse query lanes to isolate dashboard from batch
Part 5 — Section 11

Druid SQL, Calcite, and Query Planning

How Druid translates SQL into optimized native queries — and how to ensure Calcite picks the fastest path.

11 — Druid SQL, Calcite, and Query Planning

11.1 Apache Calcite Integration

Druid's SQL layer is built on Apache Calcite, a dynamic data management framework used by many query engines (Hive, Flink, Phoenix). Calcite handles SQL parsing, validation, and logical plan generation. Druid extends Calcite with custom optimization rules that translate logical plans into optimized native Druid JSON queries.

The translation pipeline:

  1. Lexing & Parsing: SQL string → parse tree (AST) via Calcite's SQL parser
  2. Validation: Check table/column names against Druid's metadata catalog, validate types
  3. Logical Plan: AST → relational algebra tree (LogicalProject, LogicalFilter, LogicalAggregate...)
  4. Druid Rule Matching: Calcite's rule-based optimizer applies Druid-specific rules that recognize patterns and rewrite them into native query operations
  5. Native Query Emission: The optimized plan is converted to a native Druid JSON query (Timeseries, TopN, GroupBy, etc.)
WHERE __time ≥ X to segment pruning 2. DruidRules.AGGREGATE converted GROUP BY to native aggregation 3. DruidRules.TOPN recognized single-dim + ORDER BY + LIMIT → TopN (3× faster) TopN is ~3× faster than GroupBy for this pattern — Calcite detected and optimized automatically
Figure 11.1 — Calcite translates SQL through 5 stages, applying Druid-specific optimization rules to select the fastest native query type

11.2 Calcite Rule-Based Optimization

Druid registers a set of custom Calcite rules that recognize SQL patterns and translate them into the most efficient native query type. Understanding these rules lets you write SQL that Calcite can optimize, rather than fighting it:

Calcite RuleSQL Pattern RecognizedNative Query EmittedPerformance Impact
DruidRules.TOPNGROUP BY single_dim ORDER BY metric LIMIT NTopN3× faster than GroupBy (approximate top-K)
DruidRules.TIMESERIESGROUP BY TIME_FLOOR(__time, ...) onlyTimeseriesFastest query type — single-pass scan
DruidRules.FILTERAny WHERE clauseFilter pushed to segment-level bitmap99%+ row elimination before scan
DruidRules.AGGREGATEAny GROUP BY + aggregation functionsNative aggregation (GroupBy v2)Hash-based, vectorizable
DruidRules.SORTORDER BY + LIMITNative sort pushed to HistoricalsReduces Broker merge work
DruidRules.PROJECTColumn references in SELECTProjection pruning — skip unused columnsFewer bytes read from mmap
Subquery flatteningNested SELECT ... FROM (SELECT ...)Flattened to single-stage when possibleAvoids MSE overhead

11.3 SQL → Native JSON Translation — Worked Example

Let's trace how a typical dashboard query is translated from SQL to native JSON, showing each optimization Calcite applies:

Input SQL — Dashboard QuerySQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H')  AS hour,
    country,
    platform,
    SUM(revenue_sum)            AS total_revenue,
    APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users) AS unique_users
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND country IN ('US', 'UK', 'DE')
  AND platform = 'mobile'
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 100

Calcite analysis:

  • GROUP BY has 3 dimensions (hour, country, platform) → cannot use TopN (requires single dim) or Timeseries (requires time only). Falls to GroupBy v2.
  • WHERE __time ≥ ... → filter pushed to segment time pruning — only 7 days of segments considered
  • WHERE country IN (...) AND platform = ... → filter pushed to bitmap evaluation on each segment
  • ORDER BY + LIMIT → pushed to native sort — Historicals only send top-100 partial results each
  • APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users) → translated to HLL sketch merge aggregation (not exact count)
  • Projection: only columns __time, country, platform, revenue_sum, unique_users are read — all others skipped
Output — Native GroupBy v2 JSON (simplified)JSON
{
  "queryType": "groupBy",
  "dataSource": { "type": "table", "name": "user_events" },
  "intervals": ["2024-03-22T00:00:00.000Z/2024-03-29T00:00:00.000Z"],
  "granularity": "all",
  "dimensions": [
    { "type": "extraction", "dimension": "__time",
      "extractionFn": { "type": "timeFormat", "granularity": "PT1H" } },
    "country",
    "platform"
  ],
  "filter": {
    "type": "and",
    "fields": [
      { "type": "in", "dimension": "country", "values": ["US","UK","DE"] },
      { "type": "selector", "dimension": "platform", "value": "mobile" }
    ]
  },
  "aggregations": [
    { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "a0", "fieldName": "revenue_sum" },
    { "type": "HLLSketchMerge", "name": "a1", "fieldName": "unique_users" }
  ],
  "limitSpec": {
    "type": "default",
    "limit": 100,
    "columns": [{ "dimension": "a0", "direction": "descending" }]
  },
  "context": { "vectorize": "true" }
}

11.4 TopN vs GroupBy — When Each Is Selected

The TopN vs GroupBy decision is the most impactful automatic optimization Calcite makes. TopN uses an approximate top-K algorithm that avoids building a full hash table — it's ~3× faster than GroupBy for eligible queries:

SQL PatternCalcite EmitsWhy
SELECT country, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY country ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10TopNSingle dimension + ORDER BY metric + LIMIT → TopN eligible
SELECT country, platform, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC LIMIT 10GroupByTwo dimensions → TopN requires single dimension
SELECT country, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY country (no LIMIT)GroupByNo LIMIT → TopN requires LIMIT
SELECT country, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY country ORDER BY country LIMIT 10GroupByORDER BY dimension (not metric) → TopN requires ORDER BY metric
SELECT TIME_FLOOR(__time,'PT1H'), SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY 1TimeseriesOnly time-floor grouping, no dimension → fastest type
⚙️ Engineering Insight TopN is approximate: it computes top-K per Historical, then merges at the Broker. If a value is just barely in the top-K on one Historical but not another, it might be missed. In practice, for top-10 or top-100 with well-skewed data, accuracy is >99.9%. If you need exact results, set "useApproximateTopN": false in the query context — this forces GroupBy instead.

11.5 EXPLAIN PLAN — Seeing What Calcite Chose

EXPLAIN PLAN FOR is the most important debugging tool for Druid SQL. It reveals the native query type, filters, dimensions, aggregations, and vectorization status — without executing the query.

Using EXPLAIN PLANSQL
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY total_rev DESC
LIMIT 10

-- Output reveals:
-- {
--   "queryType": "topN",           ← Calcite chose TopN (fast path!)
--   "intervals": ["7-day range"],   ← Time pruning active
--   "filter": null,                 ← No extra filter (only time)
--   "dimension": "country",         ← Single dimension confirmed
--   "threshold": 10,                ← LIMIT pushed
--   "context": {"vectorize":"true"} ← Vectorized execution active
-- }

Key checks in EXPLAIN PLAN output:

  • Is it topN or groupBy? — TopN is 3× faster for single-dim + LIMIT queries
  • Is vectorize true? — Vectorized execution is 2–10× faster. If false, identify the non-vectorizable expression and refactor.
  • Are intervals tight? — Only 7 days, not all-time? Loose intervals = scanning unnecessary data.
  • Is the filter pushed down? — "filter" should appear in the native query, not as a post-aggregation step.
  • Are there unnecessary subqueries? — Look for "dataSource": {"type": "query"} — nested queries trigger slower execution.
  • Is it query_controller? — This means MSE was triggered (5–60s latency). Unexpected for dashboards.
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you optimize a Druid dashboard query?" — (1) Run EXPLAIN PLAN first. (2) Ensure a tight __time filter — prunes 90%+ of segments. (3) For single-dim GROUP BY + LIMIT, verify Calcite emits TopN (not GroupBy). (4) Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT instead of COUNT(DISTINCT) — 10–100× faster. (5) Verify vectorize=true. (6) Ensure segments are compacted and CLUSTERED BY the filter dimension. Each step gives a multiplicative improvement.

11.6 SQL Functions and Feature Support

Druid SQL supports a broad — but not complete — subset of ANSI SQL. Understanding the boundaries avoids runtime surprises:

FeatureSupport LevelNotes
SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING✅ FullCore analytics patterns fully supported
ORDER BY + LIMIT✅ FullPushed to native sort; triggers TopN when eligible
COUNT, SUM, MIN, MAX, AVG✅ FullStandard aggregations on metrics
APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT✅ FullHLL-based — use this instead of COUNT(DISTINCT)
TIME_FLOOR, TIME_SHIFT, TIME_EXTRACT✅ FullDruid-specific time functions — essential for time grouping
Subqueries✅ SupportedFlattened when possible; may trigger MSE for complex nesting
Window functions⚠️ MSE onlyROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, SUM OVER — 5–60s latency
JOINs⚠️ LimitedBroadcast join (<100MB right) or subquery join. No large-table JOIN.
COUNT(DISTINCT x)⚠️ Supported but slowExact — very expensive on high cardinality. Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT.
INSERT / REPLACE✅ FullSQL-based ingestion via MSQ engine
UPDATE / DELETE❌ Not supportedDruid is append-only. Use REPLACE for corrections.
CREATE TABLE❌ Not supportedDatasources created implicitly by ingestion

11.7 Common SQL Mistakes and Fixes

MistakeImpactFix
COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)Exact computation, extremely slow on high cardinalityAPPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users) — use HLL sketch column
No __time filterScans ALL segments in the datasourceAlways add WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY user_id without LIMITMillions of groups → Broker OOMAdd LIMIT 1000 or use TopN pattern with single dim
SELECT *Reads all columns including unusedList only needed columns — reduces I/O dramatically
CURRENT_TIMESTAMP in time filterShifts every query → zero cache hitsTIME_FLOOR(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, 'PT5M') - INTERVAL '7' DAY
Window function in dashboardTriggers MSE → 5–60s latencyPre-compute in ETL or use subquery pattern
Large subquery JOINRight side materialized in Broker memory → OOMDenormalize at ingestion time or use LOOKUP
⚠️ Common Pitfall Writing WHERE __time BETWEEN TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01' AND TIMESTAMP '2024-12-31' when you only need last 7 days. This scans 365 days of segments instead of 7. Every unnecessary day adds segments to scan. The difference: 7 segments (2 seconds of scan) vs 365 segments (45 seconds). Always use the tightest time filter possible.

Section 11 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
Calcite pipelineSQL → parse → validate → logical plan → Druid rules → native JSONEXPLAIN PLAN reveals the native type chosen
Rule-based optimizationDruid rules match patterns → emit optimal native queryWrite SQL that matches TopN/Timeseries patterns
TopN recognitionSingle dim + ORDER BY metric + LIMIT → 3× fasterDesign dashboards to use single-dim TopN queries
EXPLAIN PLANShows native type, vectorize status, filtersRun on every new query before deploying to dashboard
SQL pitfallsCOUNT(DISTINCT), no __time, no LIMIT → slow/OOMChecklist: time filter + LIMIT + APPROX + vectorize
Part 5 — Section 12

Segment Pruning — Multi-Layer Data Elimination

How Druid eliminates 99.9% of data before reading a single row — the foundation of sub-second latency.

12 — Segment Pruning — Multi-Layer Data Elimination

12.1 The Pruning Philosophy

Segment pruning is the single most important performance mechanism in Druid. Before any column data is read, before any bitmap filter is evaluated, the Broker eliminates entire segments from consideration through a cascade of progressively finer filters. The goal: of 10,000 segments in the cluster, scan only the 12 that could possibly contain matching data.

Pruning happens in four layers, each narrowing the set further. Each layer is a distinct mechanism with different inputs and trade-offs:

Figure 12.1 — Four pruning layers progressively eliminate 99.88% of segments before any data is scanned

12.2 Layer 1 — Time Interval Pruning

Every segment covers a specific time interval (e.g., 2024-03-15/2024-03-16 for DAY granularity). The Broker compares the query's WHERE __time filter against each segment's interval and eliminates all non-overlapping segments. This is always free — no configuration needed.

  • WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY with DAY granularity → only 7 segments per partition
  • WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' HOUR with HOUR granularity → only 1 segment per partition
  • No __time filter → ALL segments scanned — this is the #1 performance mistake in Druid
⚠️ Common Pitfall Forgetting the __time filter on a datasource with 2 years of hourly data: 2 × 365 × 24 × 4 shards = 70,000+ segments to scan. With a 7-day filter: 7 × 24 × 4 = 672 segments. That's a 100× reduction from a single WHERE clause. Every Druid query should have a time filter — no exceptions.

12.3 Layer 2 — Partition Pruning

When segments are created with CLUSTERED BY or hash/range partitioning, each shard contains a known subset of dimension values. The Broker reads partition metadata and skips shards that cannot contain the filtered value:

  • Range partitioning (CLUSTERED BY country): shard 0 has countries A–F, shard 1 has G–M, shard 2 has N–Z. WHERE country='US' → only shard 2 scanned.
  • Hash partitioning (partitionsSpec.type=hashed): hash("US") % 4 = 2 → only shard 2.
  • No partitioning (streaming default): all shards must be scanned — no pruning possible.
⚙️ Engineering Insight Partition pruning is the highest-ROI optimization after time filtering. If your dashboards always filter on country, using CLUSTERED BY country at ingestion eliminates 75–95% of shards. For streaming data, this requires compaction with hashed or range partitioning — streaming ingestion itself only produces numbered (non-prunable) shards.

12.4 Layer 3 — Min/Max Metadata Pruning

Each segment stores per-column min and max values in its metadata. The Broker compares filter predicates against these bounds:

  • WHERE revenue > 1000 → segment with max(revenue) = 500 → skip
  • WHERE country = 'ZZ' → segment with max(country) = 'US' → skip
  • Most effective on sorted/clustered dimensions where min/max ranges are narrow per segment

This pruning is automatic — Druid always collects min/max stats. However, its effectiveness depends on data distribution. If every segment contains values from 'A' to 'Z', min/max doesn't help — the ranges overlap completely.

12.5 Layer 4 — Bloom Filter Pruning (Druid 0.22+)

For high-cardinality dimensions where min/max ranges overlap, Druid supports optional per-segment Bloom filters. A Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure that answers "is value X in this segment?" with:

  • "Definitely not here" → segment safely skipped (true negative — 100% accurate)
  • "Possibly here" → segment must be scanned (may be false positive — configurable error rate)

Bloom filters are most useful for point lookups on high-cardinality dimensions (e.g., WHERE user_id = 'abc123') where min/max pruning is ineffective but most segments don't contain the specific value.

Enable Bloom Filter at Query TimeSQL
SELECT *
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
  AND bloom_filter_test("user_id", 'abc123')  -- Bloom filter check

-- Requires bloom filter index built at ingestion time:
-- "metricsSpec": [
--   { "type": "bloom", "name": "user_id_bloom", "fieldName": "user_id",
--     "maxNumEntries": 500000 }
-- ]

12.6 Pruning Effectiveness — Measuring Impact

You can measure pruning effectiveness through query metrics and the request log:

MetricWhere to FindHealthy ValueAction if Unhealthy
query/segment/countBroker request log<100 for dashboard queriesAdd tighter time filter, enable partition pruning
query/segment/timeBroker metrics<500ms total scan timeCompaction, rollup, or add Historicals
query/wait/timeBroker metrics<5ms (queuing time)Add processing threads or Historicals
Segment count per intervalDruid Console → Datasources<10 per DAY intervalEnable compaction — too many small segments
🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through how Druid handles a query that needs to scan 50TB of data." — It doesn't scan 50TB. Through multi-layer pruning, it scans maybe 50GB. Layer 1: time filter eliminates 95% of segments. Layer 2: partition pruning on the filter dimension eliminates another 80% of remaining shards.
