Layer 3: min/max metadata skips segments where column ranges don't match. Layer 4: optional Bloom filters for point lookups. After all layers, typically 0.1–1% of segments remain. Then bitmap indexes within each surviving segment eliminate 95%+ of rows. The actual data read is a tiny fraction of total cluster data.

Section 12 — Key Takeaways

Pruning LayerMechanismConfiguration RequiredTypical Elimination
Time intervalCompare query __time vs segment intervalNone — always free. Just add WHERE __time.90–99%
PartitionHash/range shard metadataCLUSTERED BY at ingestion or compaction50–95%
Min/MaxPer-column min/max boundsAutomatic — most effective with sorted data0–80%
Bloom filterProbabilistic membership testExplicit bloom metric at ingestion50–95% (point lookups)
Part 5 — Section 13

Query Patterns and Advanced SQL Examples

Battle-tested SQL patterns for dashboards, funnels, percentiles, set operations, and native JSON queries.

13 — Query Patterns and Advanced SQL Examples

13.1 Time-Series Aggregation

The bread-and-butter of Druid dashboards: aggregate metrics over time buckets. This pattern maps to the Timeseries native query (fastest type) when only time-floor grouping is used, or GroupBy when additional dimensions are included.

Hourly Revenue + Unique Users (last 30 days)SQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H') AS hour,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS hourly_revenue,
    APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users) AS unique_users
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
  AND event_type = 'purchase'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

-- Calcite emits: Timeseries (fastest — only time grouping)
-- Vectorized: yes | Segments: ~720 (30 days × 24 hours)
-- Typical latency: 80–200ms

13.2 TopN Pattern — Leaderboards and Rankings

The TopN pattern is critical for dashboards showing "top countries," "top products," or any ranked list. It activates when Calcite sees: single dimension + ORDER BY metric + LIMIT.

Top 100 Products by Revenue (last 7 days)SQL
SELECT
    product_id,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_revenue,
    SUM(event_count) AS total_orders
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND event_type = 'purchase'
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 100

-- Calcite emits: TopN (3× faster than GroupBy for this pattern)
-- Approximate top-K: accuracy >99.9% for top-100 with skewed data
-- Typical latency: 50–150ms
⚙️ Engineering Insight TopN's approximation means a product ranked #101 on one Historical might be promoted to #99 globally when combined — but a product ranked #500 won't mistakenly appear in the top 10. For dashboards showing top-10 or top-50, the error rate is effectively zero. For exact results: {"useApproximateTopN": false} in query context forces GroupBy.

13.3 Funnel Analysis

Druid handles funnel queries naturally using conditional aggregation. Each funnel step is a CASE WHEN expression filtering the appropriate event_type:

Daily Conversion Funnel: View → Cart → PurchaseSQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'P1D') AS day,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'view'
         THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)       AS views,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'add_to_cart'
         THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)       AS carts,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'purchase'
         THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)       AS purchases,
    ROUND(100.0 *
        SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'purchase'
             THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)
      / NULLIF(
        SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'view'
             THEN event_count ELSE 0 END), 0)
    , 2)                                     AS cvr_pct
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

-- Single-pass scan: all funnel steps computed in one query
-- No self-join needed — much faster than traditional funnel queries
-- Typical latency: 100–300ms
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you implement funnel analysis in Druid?" — Use conditional aggregation with CASE WHEN event_type = 'step_N' inside SUM(). All steps are computed in a single scan — no self-joins. For user-level funnels (did user X do step 1 then step 2), you'd need Theta Sketch set intersections or pre-compute user journeys at ingestion.

13.4 Approximate Percentiles

Percentile queries (P50, P95, P99) are critical for monitoring latency and performance. Druid uses quantile sketches ingested at ingestion time — they're mergeable across rollup and pre-aggregation:

Page Load Latency Percentiles by CountrySQL
SELECT
    country,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(page_load_sketch, 0.50) AS p50_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(page_load_sketch, 0.95) AS p95_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(page_load_sketch, 0.99) AS p99_ms
FROM "page_performance"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY p99_ms DESC

-- Requires: quantilesDoublesSketch column ingested at ingestion time
-- metricsSpec: { "type": "quantilesDoublesSketch",
--                "name": "page_load_sketch",
--                "fieldName": "page_load_ms", "k": 128 }

13.5 Theta Sketch Set Operations — User Overlap

Theta Sketches enable set algebra on approximate user sets: union (A∪B), intersection (A∩B), and difference (A\B). This powers "users who viewed product A AND product B" queries:

Users Who Viewed Both Product A and Product BSQL
SELECT
    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
      THETA_SKETCH_INTERSECT(
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A'),
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
      )
    ) AS users_viewed_both,

    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
      DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A')
    ) AS users_viewed_a,

    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
      DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
    ) AS users_viewed_b

FROM "product_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY

-- All operations are O(sketch_size), not O(user_count)
-- 100M users intersected in ~5ms
Figure 13.1 — Theta Sketch set operations enable user overlap analysis at O(sketch_size), not O(user_count)

13.6 Multi-Dimension Breakdown with Drill-Down

Dashboard drill-down pattern: start with a high-level view (by country), then drill into a specific country to see platform breakdown:

Level 1: Top CountriesSQL
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10
-- → TopN, ~100ms
Level 2: Drill into US by PlatformSQL
SELECT platform, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND country = 'US'                   -- Drill-down filter
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10
-- → TopN, ~80ms (country='US' uses bitmap + partition pruning)
Level 3: Drill into US + mobile by event type over timeSQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H') AS hour,
    event_type,
    SUM(event_count) AS events
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND country = 'US'
  AND platform = 'mobile'
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1
-- → GroupBy (2 dims), ~150ms (two bitmap ANDs + time grouping)

13.7 Native JSON Query Example

While SQL is preferred for readability, native JSON gives direct control and avoids Calcite translation overhead (~2–5ms). Some ultra-low-latency applications use native JSON directly:

Native TopN — Top 10 Countries by RevenueJSON
{
  "queryType": "topN",
  "dataSource": "user_events",
  "intervals": ["2024-01-01/2024-01-31"],
  "granularity": "all",
  "dimension": "country",
  "metric": { "type": "numeric", "metric": "total_revenue" },
  "threshold": 10,
  "aggregations": [
    { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "total_revenue",
      "fieldName": "revenue_sum" }
  ],
  "filter": {
    "type": "selector",
    "dimension": "platform",
    "value": "mobile"
  },
  "context": { "vectorize": "true" }
}

// POST to http://broker:8082/druid/v2
// Saves ~3ms of Calcite overhead vs SQL endpoint

13.8 Query Pattern Quick Reference

Use CaseSQL PatternNative TypeTypical Latency
Time-series chartGROUP BY TIME_FLOOR(__time, ...)Timeseries80–200ms
Top-N leaderboardGROUP BY dim ORDER BY metric LIMIT NTopN50–150ms
Multi-dim breakdownGROUP BY dim1, dim2GroupBy100–400ms
Funnel analysisSUM(CASE WHEN event_type=...)GroupBy/Timeseries100–300ms
Percentile monitoringAPPROX_QUANTILE_DS(sketch, 0.95)GroupBy100–300ms
User set overlapTHETA_SKETCH_INTERSECT(DS_THETA(...))GroupBy200–500ms
Data explorationSELECT * ... LIMIT 100Scan50–200ms
Cumulative / windowSUM(...) OVER (ORDER BY ...)MSE5–60s ⚠️
⚠️ Common Pitfall Combining patterns that look innocent but trigger slow execution: SELECT country, platform, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC LIMIT 10. This has 2 GROUP BY dimensions, so Calcite emits GroupBy (not TopN). If you can restructure to use a single dimension (e.g., CONCAT(country, ':', platform) as a derived dimension), it becomes TopN-eligible — 3× faster.
🎯 Interview Tip "Design a real-time dashboard with 5 panels using Druid." — Panel 1: Time-series revenue chart → Timeseries query (80ms). Panel 2: Top countries → TopN (50ms). Panel 3: Funnel view→cart→purchase → GroupBy with CASE (150ms). Panel 4: P50/P95/P99 latency → quantile sketch query (120ms). Panel 5: User overlap between products → Theta sketch intersection (200ms). Total: 5 parallel queries, wall clock ~200ms (slowest query dominates), all on rolled-up data with bitmap filtering.

Section 13 — Key Takeaways

PatternKey TechniqueProduction Tip
Time-seriesTIME_FLOOR + GROUP BY 1Fastest type — design dashboards around this
TopNSingle dim + ORDER + LIMIT3× faster than GroupBy — check EXPLAIN PLAN
FunnelCASE WHEN conditional aggregationSingle-pass — no self-join needed
PercentilesQuantile sketch at ingestionMust ingest as sketch column — cannot compute on raw
Set operationsTheta Sketch intersect/union/diffO(sketch_size) not O(user_count) — ~5ms for 100M users
Drill-downProgressive filter additionEach level adds a filter → bitmap AND → faster
Part 5 — Section 12

Segment Pruning — Multi-Layer Data Elimination

How Druid eliminates 99.9% of data before reading a single row — the foundation of sub-second latency.

12 — Segment Pruning — Multi-Layer Data Elimination

12.1 The Pruning Philosophy

Segment pruning is the single most important performance mechanism in Druid. Before any column data is read, before any bitmap filter is evaluated, the Broker eliminates entire segments from consideration through a cascade of progressively finer filters. The goal: of 10,000 segments in the cluster, scan only the 12 that could possibly contain matching data.

Pruning happens in four layers, each narrowing the set further. Each layer is a distinct mechanism with different inputs and trade-offs:

Figure 12.1 — Four pruning layers progressively eliminate 99.88% of segments before any data is scanned

12.2 Layer 1 — Time Interval Pruning

Every segment covers a specific time interval (e.g., 2024-03-15/2024-03-16 for DAY granularity). The Broker compares the query's WHERE __time filter against each segment's interval and eliminates all non-overlapping segments. This is always free — no configuration needed.

  • WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY with DAY granularity → only 7 segments per partition
  • WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' HOUR with HOUR granularity → only 1 segment per partition
  • No __time filter → ALL segments scanned — this is the #1 performance mistake in Druid
⚠️ Common Pitfall Forgetting the __time filter on a datasource with 2 years of hourly data: 2 × 365 × 24 × 4 shards = 70,000+ segments to scan. With a 7-day filter: 7 × 24 × 4 = 672 segments. That's a 100× reduction from a single WHERE clause. Every Druid query should have a time filter — no exceptions.

12.3 Layer 2 — Partition Pruning

When segments are created with CLUSTERED BY or hash/range partitioning, each shard contains a known subset of dimension values. The Broker reads partition metadata and skips shards that cannot contain the filtered value:

  • Range partitioning (CLUSTERED BY country): shard 0 has countries A–F, shard 1 has G–M, shard 2 has N–Z. WHERE country='US' → only shard 2 scanned.
  • Hash partitioning (partitionsSpec.type=hashed): hash("US") % 4 = 2 → only shard 2.
  • No partitioning (streaming default): all shards must be scanned — no pruning possible.
⚙️ Engineering Insight Partition pruning is the highest-ROI optimization after time filtering. If your dashboards always filter on country, using CLUSTERED BY country at ingestion eliminates 75–95% of shards. For streaming data, this requires compaction with hashed or range partitioning — streaming ingestion itself only produces numbered (non-prunable) shards.

12.4 Layer 3 — Min/Max Metadata Pruning

Each segment stores per-column min and max values in its metadata. The Broker compares filter predicates against these bounds:

  • WHERE revenue > 1000 → segment with max(revenue) = 500 → skip
  • WHERE country = 'ZZ' → segment with max(country) = 'US' → skip
  • Most effective on sorted/clustered dimensions where min/max ranges are narrow per segment

This pruning is automatic — Druid always collects min/max stats. However, its effectiveness depends on data distribution. If every segment contains values from 'A' to 'Z', min/max doesn't help — the ranges overlap completely.

12.5 Layer 4 — Bloom Filter Pruning (Druid 0.22+)

For high-cardinality dimensions where min/max ranges overlap, Druid supports optional per-segment Bloom filters. A Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure that answers "is value X in this segment?" with:

  • "Definitely not here" → segment safely skipped (true negative — 100% accurate)
  • "Possibly here" → segment must be scanned (may be false positive — configurable error rate)

Bloom filters are most useful for point lookups on high-cardinality dimensions (e.g., WHERE user_id = 'abc123') where min/max pruning is ineffective but most segments don't contain the specific value.

Enable Bloom Filter at Query TimeSQL
SELECT *
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
  AND bloom_filter_test("user_id", 'abc123')  -- Bloom filter check

-- Requires bloom filter index built at ingestion time:
-- "metricsSpec": [
--   { "type": "bloom", "name": "user_id_bloom", "fieldName": "user_id",
--     "maxNumEntries": 500000 }
-- ]

12.6 Pruning Effectiveness — Measuring Impact

You can measure pruning effectiveness through query metrics and the request log:

MetricWhere to FindHealthy ValueAction if Unhealthy
query/segment/countBroker request log<100 for dashboard queriesAdd tighter time filter, enable partition pruning
query/segment/timeBroker metrics<500ms total scan timeCompaction, rollup, or add Historicals
query/wait/timeBroker metrics<5ms (queuing time)Add processing threads or Historicals
Segment count per intervalDruid Console → Datasources<10 per DAY intervalEnable compaction — too many small segments
🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through how Druid handles a query that needs to scan 50TB of data." — It doesn't scan 50TB. Through multi-layer pruning, it scans maybe 50GB. Layer 1: time filter eliminates 95% of segments. Layer 2: partition pruning on the filter dimension eliminates another 80% of remaining shards. Layer 3: min/max metadata skips segments where column ranges don't match. Layer 4: optional Bloom filters for point lookups. After all layers, typically 0.1–1% of segments remain. Then bitmap indexes within each surviving segment eliminate 95%+ of rows. The actual data read is a tiny fraction of total cluster data.

Section 12 — Key Takeaways

Pruning LayerMechanismConfiguration RequiredTypical Elimination
Time intervalCompare query __time vs segment intervalNone — always free. Just add WHERE __time.90–99%
PartitionHash/range shard metadataCLUSTERED BY at ingestion or compaction50–95%
Min/MaxPer-column min/max boundsAutomatic — most effective with sorted data0–80%
Bloom filterProbabilistic membership testExplicit bloom metric at ingestion50–95% (point lookups)
Part 5 — Section 13

Query Patterns and Advanced SQL Examples

Battle-tested SQL patterns for dashboards, funnels, percentiles, set operations, and native JSON queries.

13 — Query Patterns and Advanced SQL Examples

13.1 Time-Series Aggregation

The bread-and-butter of Druid dashboards: aggregate metrics over time buckets. This pattern maps to the Timeseries native query (fastest type) when only time-floor grouping is used, or GroupBy when additional dimensions are included.

Hourly Revenue + Unique Users (last 30 days)SQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H') AS hour,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS hourly_revenue,
    APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users) AS unique_users
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
  AND event_type = 'purchase'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

-- Calcite emits: Timeseries (fastest — only time grouping)
-- Vectorized: yes | Segments: ~720 (30 days × 24 hours)
-- Typical latency: 80–200ms

13.2 TopN Pattern — Leaderboards and Rankings

The TopN pattern is critical for dashboards showing "top countries," "top products," or any ranked list. It activates when Calcite sees: single dimension + ORDER BY metric + LIMIT.

Top 100 Products by Revenue (last 7 days)SQL
SELECT
    product_id,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_revenue,
    SUM(event_count) AS total_orders
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND event_type = 'purchase'
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 100

-- Calcite emits: TopN (3× faster than GroupBy for this pattern)
-- Approximate top-K: accuracy >99.9% for top-100 with skewed data
-- Typical latency: 50–150ms
⚙️ Engineering Insight TopN's approximation means a product ranked #101 on one Historical might be promoted to #99 globally when combined — but a product ranked #500 won't mistakenly appear in the top 10. For dashboards showing top-10 or top-50, the error rate is effectively zero. For exact results: {"useApproximateTopN": false} in query context forces GroupBy.

13.3 Funnel Analysis

Druid handles funnel queries naturally using conditional aggregation. Each funnel step is a CASE WHEN expression filtering the appropriate event_type:

Daily Conversion Funnel: View → Cart → PurchaseSQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'P1D') AS day,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'view'
         THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)       AS views,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'add_to_cart'
         THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)       AS carts,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'purchase'
         THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)       AS purchases,
    ROUND(100.0 *
        SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'purchase'
             THEN event_count ELSE 0 END)
      / NULLIF(
        SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'view'
             THEN event_count ELSE 0 END), 0)
    , 2)                                     AS cvr_pct
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

-- Single-pass scan: all funnel steps computed in one query
-- No self-join needed — much faster than traditional funnel queries
-- Typical latency: 100–300ms
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you implement funnel analysis in Druid?" — Use conditional aggregation with CASE WHEN event_type = 'step_N' inside SUM(). All steps are computed in a single scan — no self-joins. For user-level funnels (did user X do step 1 then step 2), you'd need Theta Sketch set intersections or pre-compute user journeys at ingestion.

13.4 Approximate Percentiles

Percentile queries (P50, P95, P99) are critical for monitoring latency and performance. Druid uses quantile sketches ingested at ingestion time — they're mergeable across rollup and pre-aggregation:

Page Load Latency Percentiles by CountrySQL
SELECT
    country,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(page_load_sketch, 0.50) AS p50_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(page_load_sketch, 0.95) AS p95_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(page_load_sketch, 0.99) AS p99_ms
FROM "page_performance"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY p99_ms DESC

-- Requires: quantilesDoublesSketch column ingested at ingestion time
-- metricsSpec: { "type": "quantilesDoublesSketch",
--                "name": "page_load_sketch",
--                "fieldName": "page_load_ms", "k": 128 }

13.5 Theta Sketch Set Operations — User Overlap

Theta Sketches enable set algebra on approximate user sets: union (A∪B), intersection (A∩B), and difference (A\B). This powers "users who viewed product A AND product B" queries:

Users Who Viewed Both Product A and Product BSQL
SELECT
    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
      THETA_SKETCH_INTERSECT(
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A'),
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
      )
    ) AS users_viewed_both,

    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
      DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A')
    ) AS users_viewed_a,

    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
      DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
    ) AS users_viewed_b

FROM "product_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY

-- All operations are O(sketch_size), not O(user_count)
-- 100M users intersected in ~5ms
Figure 13.1 — Theta Sketch set operations enable user overlap analysis at O(sketch_size), not O(user_count)

13.6 Multi-Dimension Breakdown with Drill-Down

Dashboard drill-down pattern: start with a high-level view (by country), then drill into a specific country to see platform breakdown:

Level 1: Top CountriesSQL
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10
-- → TopN, ~100ms
Level 2: Drill into US by PlatformSQL
SELECT platform, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND country = 'US'                   -- Drill-down filter
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10
-- → TopN, ~80ms (country='US' uses bitmap + partition pruning)
Level 3: Drill into US + mobile by event type over timeSQL
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H') AS hour,
    event_type,
    SUM(event_count) AS events
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND country = 'US'
  AND platform = 'mobile'
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1
-- → GroupBy (2 dims), ~150ms (two bitmap ANDs + time grouping)

13.7 Native JSON Query Example

While SQL is preferred for readability, native JSON gives direct control and avoids Calcite translation overhead (~2–5ms). Some ultra-low-latency applications use native JSON directly:

Native TopN — Top 10 Countries by RevenueJSON
{
  "queryType": "topN",
  "dataSource": "user_events",
  "intervals": ["2024-01-01/2024-01-31"],
  "granularity": "all",
  "dimension": "country",
  "metric": { "type": "numeric", "metric": "total_revenue" },
  "threshold": 10,
  "aggregations": [
    { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "total_revenue",
      "fieldName": "revenue_sum" }
  ],
  "filter": {
    "type": "selector",
    "dimension": "platform",
    "value": "mobile"
  },
  "context": { "vectorize": "true" }
}

// POST to http://broker:8082/druid/v2
// Saves ~3ms of Calcite overhead vs SQL endpoint

13.8 Query Pattern Quick Reference

Use CaseSQL PatternNative TypeTypical Latency
Time-series chartGROUP BY TIME_FLOOR(__time, ...)Timeseries80–200ms
Top-N leaderboardGROUP BY dim ORDER BY metric LIMIT NTopN50–150ms
Multi-dim breakdownGROUP BY dim1, dim2GroupBy100–400ms
Funnel analysisSUM(CASE WHEN event_type=...)GroupBy/Timeseries100–300ms
Percentile monitoringAPPROX_QUANTILE_DS(sketch, 0.95)GroupBy100–300ms
User set overlapTHETA_SKETCH_INTERSECT(DS_THETA(...))GroupBy200–500ms
Data explorationSELECT * ... LIMIT 100Scan50–200ms
Cumulative / windowSUM(...) OVER (ORDER BY ...)MSE5–60s ⚠️
⚠️ Common Pitfall Combining patterns that look innocent but trigger slow execution: SELECT country, platform, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC LIMIT 10. This has 2 GROUP BY dimensions, so Calcite emits GroupBy (not TopN). If you can restructure to use a single dimension (e.g., CONCAT(country, ':', platform) as a derived dimension), it becomes TopN-eligible — 3× faster.
🎯 Interview Tip "Design a real-time dashboard with 5 panels using Druid." — Panel 1: Time-series revenue chart → Timeseries query (80ms). Panel 2: Top countries → TopN (50ms). Panel 3: Funnel view→cart→purchase → GroupBy with CASE (150ms). Panel 4: P50/P95/P99 latency → quantile sketch query (120ms). Panel 5: User overlap between products → Theta sketch intersection (200ms). Total: 5 parallel queries, wall clock ~200ms (slowest query dominates), all on rolled-up data with bitmap filtering.

Section 13 — Key Takeaways

PatternKey TechniqueProduction Tip
Time-seriesTIME_FLOOR + GROUP BY 1Fastest type — design dashboards around this
TopNSingle dim + ORDER + LIMIT3× faster than GroupBy — check EXPLAIN PLAN
FunnelCASE WHEN conditional aggregationSingle-pass — no self-join needed
PercentilesQuantile sketch at ingestionMust ingest as sketch column — cannot compute on raw
Set operationsTheta Sketch intersect/union/diffO(sketch_size) not O(user_count) — ~5ms for 100M users
Drill-downProgressive filter additionEach level adds a filter → bitmap AND → faster
Part 6 — Section 14

Druid Data Model — Timestamps, Dimensions, Metrics

The three column types that define every Druid datasource — and why getting them right determines everything.

14 — Druid Data Model — Timestamps, Dimensions, Metrics

14.1 The Three Column Types

Every column in a Druid datasource is classified as one of three types. This classification is not cosmetic — it fundamentally determines how the column is stored, indexed, compressed, and queryable. Getting this classification right is the single most impactful data modeling decision.

Figure 14.1 — Every Druid column is a Timestamp (one), Dimension (filter/group), or Metric (aggregate) — classification determines storage and indexing

14.2 The Timestamp Column (__time)

The __time column is required in every Druid datasource. It serves as the primary partition key and is the foundation of Druid's time-based architecture:

  • Storage: Delta-encoded LONG array (milliseconds since Unix epoch), sorted ascending. Extremely compact — typically 1–4 bytes per row after delta encoding.
  • Segment partitioning: segmentGranularity defines how __time divides data into segments (HOUR, DAY, MONTH...)
  • Query pruning: Every WHERE __time >= X filter prunes entire segments — the #1 performance optimization
  • Rollup bucketing: queryGranularity rounds __time before rollup — rows in the same time bucket with identical dimensions are collapsed
  • TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H'): The standard way to group by time in Druid SQL
timestampSpec — Source Column MappingJSON
"timestampSpec": {
    "column": "event_time",    // Source column name
    "format": "iso"            // "iso", "posix", "millis", or Java SimpleDateFormat
}
// Supported formats:
// "iso"    → "2024-03-15T10:30:00.000Z"
// "posix"  → 1710499800 (seconds since epoch)
// "millis" → 1710499800000 (milliseconds since epoch)
// Custom   → "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss"
⚠️ Common Pitfall Setting timestampSpec.column to a column that doesn't exist in the source data. Druid silently falls back to the current time for all rows, making your entire dataset land in a single segment for "now." This causes extreme segment imbalance and makes time-based queries useless. Always validate the timestamp column name matches your source schema exactly.

14.3 Dimensions — Filter and Group Columns

Dimensions are the columns you filter on (WHERE) and group by (GROUP BY). They receive Druid's richest indexing and encoding treatment:

Dimension TypeStorageBitmap IndexRollup ImpactWhen to Use
STRINGDictionary (string→int) + encoded int arrayYes (per unique value)Each unique value = separate rollup groupDefault for text — country, platform, event_type
LONGLZ4-compressed int64 arrayOptional (default: no)Each unique value = separate rollup groupIDs, counts stored as dims (session_id, http_status)
FLOAT / DOUBLELZ4-compressed float/double arrayOptional (default: no)Each unique value = separate rollup groupNumeric dims rarely needed — usually metrics instead
Multi-value STRINGDictionary + bitmap per valueYesBreaks rollup (multi-value = unique combo)Tags, categories where one row has multiple values

Dimension Design Rules

  • Rule 1: Only include dimensions you query. Every dimension column adds dictionary overhead, bitmap storage, and reduces rollup ratio. A dimension never used in WHERE or GROUP BY is pure waste.
  • Rule 2: Control cardinality ruthlessly. A dimension with 100M unique values (user_id) creates 100M bitmaps per segment. Either disable its bitmap index or replace it with a sketch metric.
  • Rule 3: Prefer STRING over numeric for filter dimensions. STRING dimensions get bitmap indexes by default, making WHERE clauses instant. LONG/DOUBLE dims do not get bitmaps by default.
  • Rule 4: Avoid multi-value dimensions for rollup. Multi-value dims create unique combinations that defeat rollup. Use ARRAY columns (Druid 28+) for proper array semantics.
dimensionsSpec — Mixed TypesJSON
"dimensionsSpec": {
    "dimensions": [
        "country",                     // STRING (default) — bitmap indexed
        "platform",                    // STRING — low cardinality, ideal
        "event_type",                  // STRING — medium cardinality, good
        {
            "type": "long",
            "name": "http_status"        // LONG — numeric dimension (200, 404, 500...)
        },
        {
            "type": "string",
            "name": "user_id",
            "createBitmapIndex": false    // HIGH cardinality — skip bitmap
        }
    ]
}
// NOTE: user_id as a dimension breaks rollup (every user = unique group)
// Better: move user_id to HLLSketch metric for distinct counting

14.4 Metrics — Pre-Aggregated Measure Columns

Metrics are the columns you aggregate in queries (SUM, COUNT, MIN, MAX). They are pre-aggregated at ingestion time when rollup is enabled, making query-time aggregation operate on already-reduced data.

Metric TypeIngestion AggregatorQuery FunctionUse Case
count{"type":"count","name":"event_count"}SUM(event_count)Row counts — note: query uses SUM not COUNT
longSum / doubleSum{"type":"doubleSum","name":"rev_sum","fieldName":"revenue"}SUM(rev_sum)Revenue, amounts, quantities
longMin / longMax{"type":"longMin","name":"min_latency","fieldName":"latency"}MIN(min_latency)Min/max tracking across rollup
HLLSketchBuild{"type":"HLLSketchBuild","name":"unique_users","fieldName":"user_id"}APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users)Approximate distinct count
thetaSketch{"type":"thetaSketch","name":"user_sketch","fieldName":"user_id"}THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(DS_THETA(...))Distinct count + set operations
quantilesDoublesSketch{"type":"quantilesDoublesSketch","name":"latency_sketch","fieldName":"latency_ms"}APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(latency_sketch, 0.95)Percentile distributions (P50/P95/P99)
⚙️ Engineering Insight A critical subtlety: when rollup is enabled, a count metric stores the number of raw rows collapsed into each rolled-up row. At query time, you must use SUM(event_count) — NOT COUNT(*) — to get the true event count. COUNT(*) counts rolled-up rows, not original events. This is the most common beginner mistake with rolled-up data.

14.5 Data Modeling Decision Tree

For each source column, ask these questions to determine the optimal Druid classification:

Figure 14.2 — Decision tree for classifying each source column as timestamp, dimension, metric, or drop
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you model an e-commerce clickstream in Druid?" — __time: event_time. Dimensions: country, platform, device_type, page_category, event_type (all low cardinality, bitmap indexed). Metrics: event_count (count), revenue_sum (doubleSum), unique_users (HLLSketchBuild on user_id), latency_sketch (quantilesDoublesSketch on page_load_ms). Drop: request_id, session_id (high cardinality, never queried). user_id: HLL sketch metric, NOT a dimension (avoids rollup destruction). queryGranularity: MINUTE. Result: ~50× rollup ratio.

Section 14 — Key Takeaways

Column TypeUsed ForStorageKey Rule
__timeTime partition, pruning, TIME_FLOORDelta-encoded LONGAlways required. Always filter on it.
DimensionWHERE, GROUP BYDictionary + bitmapOnly include if queried. Control cardinality.
MetricSUM, COUNT, APPROXCompressed numeric / sketchPre-aggregated. Use SUM not COUNT for rolled-up counts.
DropNot usedNot storedIf not in WHERE/GROUP BY/SUM → don't ingest.
Part 6 — Section 15

Sketch-Based Approximations — HLL, Theta, Quantile

How Druid delivers cardinality, set operations, and percentiles on pre-aggregated data — in constant space and time.

15 — Sketch-Based Approximations — HLL, Theta, Quantile

15.1 Why Sketches Are Essential

After rollup, raw values are gone — you can't compute exact COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) from pre-summed rows because the individual user_id values have been collapsed. This is where probabilistic data sketches become essential.

Sketches are compact, fixed-size data structures that summarize a set of values. Their critical property: they are mergeable. The sketch from rolled-up group A merged with the sketch from group B produces the same approximate result as computing from all raw events in both groups combined. This makes them compatible with Druid's rollup and distributed query architecture.

  • Memory: O(log N) or O(K) instead of O(N) for exact computation
  • Speed: Merge operations are O(K) where K is the sketch size parameter — independent of data size
  • Accuracy: Bounded, configurable error rates (typically 1–3%)
  • Distributability: Each Historical computes a partial sketch; the Broker merges them — same result as centralized computation

15.2 The Three Sketch Families

Figure 15.1 — Three sketch families: HLL for counts, Theta for set operations, Quantile for percentiles

15.3 When to Use Which Sketch

NeedSketch TypeWhy This OneIngestion Config
Simple distinct count (e.g., unique users per country)HLLSmallest memory (1.5KB), lowest error (1.6%), sufficient for most use casesHLLSketchBuild
Distinct count + union/intersection/differenceThetaOnly sketch supporting full set algebra. Essential for "users who did X AND Y" queriesthetaSketch
Distinct count across different datasourcesThetaTheta sketches from different datasources can be intersected (HLL cannot)thetaSketch
Percentile distributions (P50/P95/P99)QuantileOnly sketch type that returns values at specified quantilesquantilesDoublesSketch
SLA monitoring (% of requests under threshold)QuantileCan answer "what % of values are below X?" via CDF queryquantilesDoublesSketch
⚙️ Engineering Insight If unsure between HLL and Theta: default to HLL for simple distinct counts — it uses 20× less memory (1.5KB vs 32KB per sketch) and has lower error (1.6% vs ~3%). Only use Theta when you need set operations (intersection/difference) or need to combine sketches across different datasources. Many teams start with HLL everywhere and add Theta columns later only for the specific dimensions that need set operations.

15.4 Sketch Accuracy Tuning

SketchParameterDefaultEffect of IncreasingMemory Impact
HLLlgK12lgK=14 → error drops to ~0.8% (from 1.6%)Memory = 2^lgK bytes (4KB → 16KB)
Thetak (size)4096k=16384 → error drops to ~1.5% (from 3%)Memory = k × 8 bytes (32KB → 128KB)
Quantilek128k=256 → tighter rank accuracy boundsMemory = ~k × 16 bytes (2KB → 4KB)
Sketch Metrics in Ingestion SpecJSON
"metricsSpec": [
    { "type": "count", "name": "event_count" },
    { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "revenue_sum", "fieldName": "revenue" },

    // HLL — simple unique count, smallest memory
    {
        "type": "HLLSketchBuild",
        "name": "unique_users",
        "fieldName": "user_id",
        "lgK": 12,                // Default: ~1.6% error, 4KB per sketch
        "tgtHllType": "HLL_4"       // HLL_4 (default), HLL_6, or HLL_8
    },

    // Theta — unique count + set operations (intersect/union/diff)
    {
        "type": "thetaSketch",
        "name": "user_sketch",
        "fieldName": "user_id",
        "size": 4096               // Default: ~3% error, 32KB per sketch
    },

    // Quantile — percentile distributions (P50/P95/P99)
    {
        "type": "quantilesDoublesSketch",
        "name": "latency_sketch",
        "fieldName": "page_load_ms",
        "k": 128                   // Default: good accuracy for most SLAs
    }
]

15.5 Sketch Query Examples

HLL — Unique Users Per CountrySQL
SELECT
    country,
    APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users) AS unique_users
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 2 DESC
LIMIT 20
-- TopN query, HLL sketch merge, ~80ms
Theta — User Overlap Between Two ProductsSQL
SELECT
    -- Users who viewed product A
    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A')
    ) AS users_a,

    -- Users who viewed product B
    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
    ) AS users_b,

    -- Users who viewed BOTH
    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(THETA_SKETCH_INTERSECT(
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A'),
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
    )) AS users_both,

    -- Users who viewed A but NOT B
    THETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(THETA_SKETCH_NOT(
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'A'),
        DS_THETA(user_sketch) FILTER (WHERE product_id = 'B')
    )) AS users_a_only
FROM "product_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
-- All operations O(sketch_size) — ~200ms even on 100M users
Quantile — Latency Percentiles Per RegionSQL
SELECT
    region,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(latency_sketch, 0.50) AS p50_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(latency_sketch, 0.90) AS p90_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(latency_sketch, 0.95) AS p95_ms,
    APPROX_QUANTILE_DS(latency_sketch, 0.99) AS p99_ms
FROM "api_performance"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY p99_ms DESC
-- Returns actual latency values at each percentile
-- e.g., p50=120ms, p95=450ms, p99=1200ms

15.6 Sketch Gotchas and Production Patterns

⚠️ Common Pitfall Using COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) in SQL instead of APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users). Both return approximate results in Druid, but COUNT(DISTINCT) doesn't use the pre-built sketch — it builds one on the fly from the dimension column, which is 10–100× slower. Always use the pre-ingested sketch column with APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Forgetting to ingest a sketch column and trying to compute it at query time. APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(user_id) where user_id is a regular STRING dimension works — but it's slow because Druid must hash every unique value from the raw dimension. With a pre-built HLLSketch metric column, the sketch is already computed during ingestion and only needs merging at query time — orders of magnitude faster.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid handle COUNT DISTINCT on pre-aggregated data?" — After rollup, individual values are lost. Druid uses probabilistic sketches (HLL, Theta) ingested as metric columns. These sketches are mergeable: partial sketches from different Historicals merge at the Broker to produce the same approximate result as computing from raw data. HLL uses 1.5KB per sketch with 1.6% error for any cardinality — O(1) space. This is what makes rollup + distinct count possible simultaneously.

15.7 Sketch Memory Budget Planning

For capacity planning, estimate the sketch memory overhead per segment:

SketchSize Per Value100 Groups/Segment10,000 Groups/SegmentNotes
HLL (lgK=12)~4 KB400 KB40 MBSmallest — use by default
Theta (k=4096)~32 KB3.2 MB320 MB20× larger than HLL — only for set ops
Quantile (k=128)~2 KB200 KB20 MBVery compact

Example: A datasource with HOUR granularity, 100 countries, 5 platforms = 500 rollup groups per hour. With 3 sketch columns (1 HLL, 1 Theta, 1 Quantile): 500 × (4KB + 32KB + 2KB) = ~19 MB sketch overhead per segment. This is typically <10% of total segment size — acceptable.

But with 100,000 products as a dimension: 100,000 × 38KB = 3.8 GB sketch overhead per segment — unacceptable. This is why high-cardinality GROUP BY + multiple sketches can blow up segment sizes. Solution: aggregate at coarser granularity or fewer dimensions.

Section 15 — Key Takeaways

SketchUse CaseMemoryErrorKey Rule
HLLSimple distinct count~4 KB~1.6%Default choice. Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT.
ThetaDistinct + set operations~32 KB~3%Only when you need intersect/diff. 20× HLL size.
QuantilePercentiles (P50/P95/P99)~2 KBConfigurableMust ingest at ingestion time — can't compute on raw.
Part 7 — Section 16

Vectorized Query Execution Engine

How Druid processes 512 rows at a time in tight CPU loops — the 2–10× scan speed multiplier hiding in your cluster config.

16 — Vectorized Query Execution Engine

16.1 What Is Vectorized Execution?

Traditional Druid query processing (pre-0.18) evaluates data row-by-row: for each row, check the filter, read column values, update the aggregation accumulator, advance to the next row. This approach has three fundamental CPU-level problems:

  • Branch misprediction: each row's filter check is an unpredictable if/else — the CPU branch predictor fails frequently, stalling the pipeline
  • Cache thrashing: accessing different columns for each row jumps between memory regions — L1/L2 cache lines are evicted before reuse
  • Object overhead: each row creates temporary Java objects — GC pressure accumulates during long scans

Vectorized execution (Druid 0.18+) solves all three by processing data in batches of 512–1024 rows (configurable via vectorSize). Instead of iterating one row at a time, the engine loads a batch of column values into contiguous arrays and processes them in tight loops — the same pattern used by columnar databases like ClickHouse, DuckDB, and Apache Arrow.

16.2 Vectorized vs Non-Vectorized Pipeline

Figure 16.1 — Row-by-row processes 5M individual iterations; vectorized processes ~9,766 batches of 512, achieving 2–10× speedup through CPU-friendly memory access patterns

16.3 Vectorized Engine Internals — How It Works

The vectorized engine transforms the query processing pipeline into three distinct phases per batch, each operating on primitive arrays with zero object allocation:

Phase 1 — Column Vector Loading

The engine loads a batch of values from each referenced column into contiguous Java primitive arrays. For a query referencing country (dictionary-encoded int) and revenue_sum (double), the engine creates:

  • int[] countryVector = new int[512]; — 512 dictionary-encoded country values
  • double[] revenueVector = new double[512]; — 512 revenue values

These arrays are contiguous in memory — the CPU prefetcher can predict and pipeline the reads. This is in stark contrast to row-by-row processing, where each column access jumps to a different memory region.

Phase 2 — Selection Vector (Filter Evaluation)

The bitmap filter produces a selection vector — an array of row offsets within the batch that pass the filter. For example, if rows 0, 2, 5, 7, 11 pass WHERE country='US' AND platform='mobile':

  • int[] selectionVector = {0, 2, 5, 7, 11};
  • int selectionSize = 5;

Only these 5 positions will be read from the column vectors — the other 507 rows in the batch are skipped entirely. The selection vector itself is computed via bitwise AND on Roaring Bitmaps (the same mechanism from Section 6, but now applied to a 512-row batch window).

Phase 3 — Tight Loop Aggregation

The aggregation loop iterates over the selection vector and accumulates results from the column vectors:

Vectorized Aggregation — PseudocodeJava
// This is what runs inside the Historical for each 512-row batch
// Zero object allocation — all primitive arrays

double runningSum = 0.0;

// selectionVector = row offsets that passed bitmap filter
for (int i = 0; i < selectionSize; i++) {
    int rowOffset = selectionVector[i];
    runningSum += revenueVector[rowOffset];  // Single array access
}

// Key properties:
// • No object creation per row — zero GC pressure
// • revenueVector is contiguous double[] — CPU prefetch works
// • Loop body is ~3 CPU instructions — JIT-optimizable
// • Modern CPUs: SIMD can process 4–8 doubles per instruction (AVX2)
⚙️ Engineering Insight The vectorized engine achieves near-theoretical memory bandwidth utilization because the JVM JIT compiler can recognize the tight loop pattern and emit SIMD instructions (AVX2 on x86). Processing 8 doubles per CPU cycle means a single core can aggregate at ~2 GB/sec of raw column data. A 15-core Historical can scan 30 GB/sec — this is why Druid achieves sub-second latency on 500MB segments.

16.4 What Can and Cannot Be Vectorized

Not all queries can use the vectorized path. When vectorization isn't possible, the engine silently falls back to row-by-row — a 2–10× performance cliff. Understanding the boundaries is critical:

FeatureVectorizable?Notes
Timeseries queries✅ YesFully vectorized — fastest path
GroupBy v2 with simple aggregations✅ YeslongSum, doubleSum, count, min, max
TopN queries✅ YesSingle-dimension approximate top-K
Filtered aggregations (FILTER WHERE)✅ YesSelection vector handles filtered subsets
LONG, DOUBLE, FLOAT columns✅ YesPrimitive arrays — ideal for vectorization
Basic expression virtual columns✅ YesSimple math: revenue * 1.1
STRING dictionary-encoded columns✅ YesOperates on int-encoded values
HLL/Theta/Quantile sketch merge⚠️ PartialSome sketch types vectorized in newer Druid versions
Complex post-aggregators❌ NoFallback to row-by-row for complex expressions
Multi-value dimension GroupBy❌ NoRow explosion breaks batch model
Complex expression virtual columns❌ NoConditional logic, string manipulation
Custom (non-vectorized) aggregators❌ NoExtension aggregators must implement VectorAggregator
⚠️ Common Pitfall A single non-vectorizable element in the query forces the entire query to fall back to row-by-row. For example, adding a complex post-aggregator like CASE WHEN SUM(a) > 100 THEN SUM(b)/SUM(a) ELSE 0 END disables vectorization for the whole query — not just that one expression. Check EXPLAIN PLAN to verify "vectorize": "true" appears in the output.

16.5 Vectorization Configuration

Cluster-Level Vectorization Configproperties
# Enable vectorized execution globally (default since Druid 0.21)
druid.query.default.context.vectorize=true

# Batch size — 512 is the default and usually optimal
# Larger batches: better throughput but more memory per query
# Smaller batches: less memory but more loop overhead
druid.query.default.context.vectorSize=512
Per-Query Vectorization ControlJSON
{
  "query": "SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) ...",
  "context": {
    // "true"  — vectorize if possible, silent fallback if not (default)
    // "force" — vectorize or ERROR if not possible (use for debugging)
    // "false" — disable vectorization (benchmarking only)
    "vectorize": "force"
  }
}

Debugging non-vectorization: Set "vectorize": "force" in the query context. If the query can't be vectorized, Druid returns an error message identifying exactly which element prevented vectorization (e.g., "Cannot vectorize aggregator [customSketchAgg]"). This is the fastest way to find and fix vectorization blockers.

16.6 Benchmarking Vectorized vs Non-Vectorized

Real-world performance comparison on a 500MB segment with 5 million rows, 10 dimensions, 5 metrics — single Historical:

Query PatternNon-VectorizedVectorizedSpeedup
Timeseries — SUM(revenue) by hour85ms12ms7.1×
TopN — top 10 countries by revenue62ms18ms3.4×
GroupBy — country × platform, SUM + COUNT120ms35ms3.4×
Filtered agg — SUM WHERE event_type='purchase'95ms15ms6.3×
GroupBy with HLL sketch merge180ms85ms2.1×
GroupBy with expression virtual column150ms150ms (fallback)1× (not vectorized)
⚙️ Engineering Insight The single biggest query performance win on an existing Druid cluster is ensuring vectorized execution is active for all dashboard queries. Steps: (1) Run EXPLAIN PLAN on every dashboard query. (2) If vectorize=false, set "vectorize":"force" to identify the blocker. (3) Refactor the blocking expression (usually a complex post-aggregator or non-vectorized custom aggregator). (4) Verify 2–10× improvement. This takes an afternoon and can transform P99 latency.

16.7 Writing Vectorized Custom Aggregators

If you develop Druid extensions with custom aggregators, implementing the VectorAggregator interface is critical — without it, any query using your aggregator falls back to row-by-row for the entire query.

Custom Aggregator — Vectorized InterfaceJava
public class CustomSumAggregatorFactory extends AggregatorFactory {

    // Row-by-row path (legacy, still required as fallback)
    @Override
    public Aggregator factorize(ColumnSelectorFactory factory) {
        return new CustomSumAggregator(
            factory.makeColumnValueSelector(fieldName));
    }

    // Buffer aggregator (off-heap, row-by-row)
    @Override
    public BufferAggregator factorizeBuffered(ColumnSelectorFactory factory) {
        return new CustomSumBufferAggregator(
            factory.makeColumnValueSelector(fieldName));
    }

    // ★ VECTORIZED PATH — implement this for 2–10× speedup
    @Override
    public VectorAggregator factorizeVector(
            VectorColumnSelectorFactory selectorFactory) {
        return new CustomSumVectorAggregator(
            selectorFactory.makeValueSelector(fieldName));
    }

    // Must return true to signal vectorization support
    @Override
    public boolean canVectorize(ColumnInspector inspector) {
        return true;
    }
}

// The VectorAggregator operates on contiguous row ranges:
public class CustomSumVectorAggregator implements VectorAggregator {
    @Override
    public void aggregate(
            ByteBuffer buf, int position,
            int startRow, int endRow) {
        // Operates on a contiguous row range — tight loop
        double[] values = selector.getDoubleVector();
        double sum = buf.getDouble(position);
        for (int i = startRow; i < endRow; i++) {
            sum += values[i];
        }
        buf.putDouble(position, sum);
    }
}
🎯 Interview Tip "What is vectorized execution in Druid and why does it matter?" — Instead of processing one row at a time (N iterations, branch-heavy, cache-unfriendly), vectorized execution processes batches of 512 rows in tight array loops. Column data is loaded into contiguous primitive arrays; bitmap filters produce a selection vector of matching offsets; aggregation loops iterate over the selection vector with zero object allocation. This enables SIMD instructions (AVX2), L1-cache-optimal access patterns, and eliminates GC pressure — achieving 2–10× faster scans. It's the single biggest per-query optimization in Druid. Verify with EXPLAIN PLAN — look for "vectorize": "true".

16.8 Vectorization and the Processing Thread Pool

Vectorized execution interacts with Historical's processing thread pool. Each concurrent segment scan consumes one thread. With vectorization active, each thread processes 2–10× more data per second, which effectively increases the cluster's throughput capacity without adding hardware:

MetricWithout VectorizationWith VectorizationImpact
Scan throughput per thread~200 MB/sec~1.5 GB/sec7.5× more data processed
Thread utilization per query50ms per segment10ms per segmentThread freed 5× faster
Effective concurrency (15 threads)~30 concurrent segment scans/sec~150 concurrent segment scans/sec5× more queries handled
GC pressure during scanModerate (row objects)Near zero (primitive arrays)Fewer GC pauses
⚙️ Engineering Insight A counterintuitive implication: enabling vectorization can allow you to reduce Historical count by 2–3× while maintaining the same query latency and concurrency. If your Historicals are CPU-bound on scan operations (check query/segment/time metric), switching from non-vectorized to vectorized execution may let you decommission a third of your fleet. Validate in staging before production changes.

Section 16 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
Vectorized executionProcess 512 rows per batch in tight array loopsEnsure vectorize=true globally (default since 0.21)
2–10× speedupSIMD-accelerated, cache-friendly, zero GCBiggest single optimization for existing clusters
Fallback riskOne non-vectorizable element → entire query falls backUse "vectorize":"force" to identify blockers
EXPLAIN PLANShows vectorize status in outputCheck every dashboard query before deployment
Custom aggregatorsMust implement VectorAggregatorWithout it, all queries using the agg are 2–10× slower
Capacity impactVectorization = more throughput per threadMay allow reducing Historical fleet by 30–50%
Part 7 — Section 17

Multi-Stage Query Engine (MSE)

Druid's distributed execution framework — enabling window functions, complex JOINs, and SQL-based ingestion beyond scatter-gather's limits.

17 — Multi-Stage Query Engine (MSE)

17.1 Why MSE Exists — The Limits of Scatter-Gather

Druid's default scatter-gather execution is blazingly fast (50–500ms) but fundamentally limited to single-stage computation: the Broker fans out a query to Historicals, each computes a partial result, and the Broker merges them. This model cannot support operations that require data to be reshuffled between stages:

  • Window functions: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY revenue DESC) requires all rows for a given country to be on the same node — impossible in scatter-gather where country data is spread across many Historicals
  • Complex subquery JOINs: joining a large table with another large table requires one side to be materialized and shuffled
  • Multi-pass aggregation: queries like "top 10 countries where average revenue > 1000" require: stage 1 = compute averages, stage 2 = filter on average, stage 3 = sort and limit
  • SQL-based ingestion: INSERT INTO / REPLACE INTO requires reading external data, aggregating, partitioning, and building segments — a multi-stage pipeline

The Multi-Stage Query Engine (MSE) (Druid 0.23+) solves these limitations by adding a distributed DAG execution framework — conceptually similar to Spark or Presto — built on top of Druid's existing task infrastructure.

17.2 MSE Architecture

MSE decomposes a complex query into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of stages. Each stage runs as parallel worker tasks on MiddleManagers, with data shuffled between stages via durable or in-memory storage:

Figure 17.1 — MSE executes a multi-stage DAG: scan → shuffle → aggregate → shuffle → merge. Unlike scatter-gather, data is reshuffled between stages enabling window functions and complex analytics.

17.3 Scatter-Gather vs MSE — When Each Activates

Understanding which engine activates for a given query is critical for predicting latency. The Calcite planner decides automatically based on the SQL pattern:

Query PatternEngineTypical LatencyWhy
SELECT SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY country LIMIT 10Scatter-Gather (TopN)50–200msSingle-stage: each Historical computes partial top-10, Broker merges
SELECT country, platform, SUM(rev) ... GROUP BY 1,2Scatter-Gather (GroupBy)100–500msSingle-stage: each Historical builds partial hash table, Broker merges
SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY country ...)MSE5–60sWindow function requires all rows for a partition on one node → shuffle needed
SELECT ... FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.key = t2.key (large t2)MSE5–60sLarge join → right side materialized and shuffled to workers
INSERT INTO "ds" SELECT ... FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...))MSEMinutesSQL ingestion — multi-stage: read → aggregate → partition → build segments
REPLACE INTO "ds" OVERWRITE WHERE ... SELECT ...MSEMinutesSame as INSERT + atomic overshadow of old segments
SELECT ... FROM (SELECT ... GROUP BY ...) WHERE agg > XDependsVariesSimple subqueries may flatten to scatter-gather; complex ones trigger MSE
⚠️ Common Pitfall Adding ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) or LAG()/LEAD() to a dashboard query. This silently switches from scatter-gather (200ms) to MSE (15 seconds) — a 75× latency increase. If you need ranked results in a dashboard, pre-compute ranks in an ETL pipeline or use a TopN query with client-side ranking instead.

17.4 Window Functions via MSE

MSE enables standard SQL window functions that were previously impossible in Druid. These are powerful for analytical reports but carry significant latency cost:

Window FunctionSQL ExampleUse Case
ROW_NUMBER()ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY rev DESC)Rank items within each group
RANK() / DENSE_RANK()RANK() OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC)Rank with ties handling
LAG() / LEAD()LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY __time)Compare with previous/next period
SUM() OVERSUM(rev) OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY __time ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING)Cumulative / running totals
AVG() OVERAVG(rev) OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY __time ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)Moving averages
FIRST_VALUE() / LAST_VALUE()FIRST_VALUE(rev) OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY __time)First/last value in a window
Window Function — Cumulative Revenue + Daily RankSQL
SELECT
    __time,
    country,
    hourly_revenue,

    -- Running cumulative revenue per country
    SUM(hourly_revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY country
        ORDER BY __time
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS cumulative_revenue,

    -- Rank within each day by revenue
    RANK() OVER (
        PARTITION BY TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'P1D')
        ORDER BY hourly_revenue DESC
    ) AS daily_rank

FROM (
    SELECT
        TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H') AS __time,
        country,
        SUM(revenue_sum) AS hourly_revenue
    FROM "user_events"
    WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
    GROUP BY 1, 2
)

-- This triggers MSE:
-- Stage 0: Scan + aggregate (scatter-gather-like)
-- Stage 1: Shuffle by country → compute cumulative SUM + RANK
-- Stage 2: Final merge + return
-- Typical latency: 8–30 seconds (NOT for real-time dashboards)
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid handle window functions?" — Druid uses the Multi-Stage Query Engine (MSE), a distributed DAG execution framework. The query is decomposed into stages: Stage 0 scans segments (like scatter-gather). A shuffle redistributes data by the PARTITION BY key. Stage 1 computes window functions on each partition. Stage 2 merges and returns. MSE runs on MiddleManagers as tasks, not on Historicals. Latency is 5–60 seconds — acceptable for analytical reports but not dashboards. For dashboard use, pre-compute window results in ETL.

17.5 MSE Internals — How Shuffles Work

The shuffle is the critical mechanism that differentiates MSE from scatter-gather. Between stages, intermediate data must be repartitioned by a new key and sent to different workers:

Figure 17.2 — Shuffle repartitions data between stages so that all rows for a PARTITION BY key land on the same worker

Shuffle storage options:

  • Durable shuffle storage (S3/GCS): intermediate shuffle data written to object storage. Fault-tolerant — if a worker crashes, the next retry reads from S3 instead of recomputing. Recommended for production.
  • Local shuffle: data stays on worker local disk. Faster but no fault tolerance — worker crash requires full stage recomputation.
Enable Durable Shuffle Storageproperties
# Enable durable shuffle for MSE (production recommended)
druid.msq.intermediate.storage.enable=true
druid.msq.intermediate.storage.type=s3
druid.msq.intermediate.storage.bucket=my-druid-shuffle-bucket
druid.msq.intermediate.storage.prefix=druid/shuffle/

# Cleanup: shuffle data is ephemeral — auto-deleted after task completes
# Set S3 lifecycle rule: delete objects older than 7 days (safety net)

17.6 MSE Tuning Parameters

ParameterDefaultDescriptionTuning Guidance
maxNumTasksCluster-dependentMaximum total tasks (controller + workers)Set to available MiddleManager slots minus streaming tasks
rowsPerSegment3,000,000Target rows per output segment (INSERT/REPLACE)5M for production — matches Druid segment size targets
rowsPerPage100,000Rows per shuffle page between stagesIncrease for large shuffles; decrease for memory-constrained workers
taskAssignmentautoHow workers are assigned to MiddleManagersauto for most cases; max to use all available slots
durableShuffleStoragefalseUse S3 for intermediate shuffle datatrue for production — enables fault tolerance
faultTolerancefalseEnable automatic retry on worker failuretrue for production — retry failed stages automatically
MSE Context Parameters for a QueryJSON
{
  "query": "INSERT INTO \"user_events\" SELECT ... FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...)) PARTITIONED BY DAY CLUSTERED BY country",
  "context": {
    "maxNumTasks": 10,              // 1 controller + 9 workers
    "rowsPerSegment": 5000000,      // 5M rows per output segment
    "taskAssignment": "auto",       // Let MSE decide worker placement
    "durableShuffleStorage": true,  // Write shuffle to S3 for fault tolerance
    "faultTolerance": true          // Auto-retry failed workers
  }
}

17.7 MSE Resource Contention — The Scheduling Problem

MSE tasks run on MiddleManagers — the same infrastructure used for Kafka streaming ingestion. This creates a critical resource contention problem:

  • A MiddleManager with 8 task slots running 6 Kafka tasks has only 2 slots available for MSE workers
  • A large INSERT INTO with maxNumTasks=20 will queue until enough slots free up — potentially starving Kafka ingestion
  • If MSE tasks consume all slots, new Kafka task generations can't start after handoff → ingestion lag spike
⚠️ Common Pitfall Running a large MSE REPLACE INTO job during peak hours with maxNumTasks=20 on a cluster where Kafka ingestion uses 16 of 24 available MiddleManager slots. The MSE job takes 8 remaining slots, then when Kafka tasks hand off, the new generation can't start because MSE holds the slots. Result: Kafka lag grows for the duration of the REPLACE job. Solutions: (1) Schedule MSE during off-peak. (2) Use workerCategory (Druid 28+) to dedicate MiddleManagers for batch vs streaming. (3) Set conservative maxNumTasks.
⚙️ Engineering Insight Druid 28+ introduced worker categories — you can tag MiddleManagers as "streaming" or "batch" and configure MSE to only use "batch" workers. This completely eliminates the streaming/batch contention problem. If you're running Druid 28+, this is the recommended configuration for any cluster with both Kafka ingestion and MSE jobs.

17.8 MSE vs External Engines (Spark/Presto) — When to Use What

CapabilityMSE (Druid-native)Spark / PrestoRecommendation
SQL ingestion into Druid✅ Native INSERT/REPLACE❌ Requires custom connectorMSE — purpose-built for Druid segments
Window functions on Druid data✅ Moderate performance✅ Better for heavy windowingMSE for simple windows; Spark for complex
Large table JOINs⚠️ Limited (broadcast + shuffle)✅ Full SMJ, BHJ, etc.Spark/Trino for heavy JOIN workloads
ETL/transformation before Druid⚠️ SQL-only transforms✅ Full programming modelSpark for complex transformations
Ad-hoc exploration⚠️ Seconds latency, limited SQL✅ Full SQL, flexibleTrino for ad-hoc; Druid for dashboards
Operational simplicity✅ Runs within Druid cluster❌ Separate cluster to manageMSE if it covers the use case
🎯 Interview Tip "When would you use Druid's MSE vs running Spark/Trino for complex queries?" — Use MSE for: (1) SQL ingestion (INSERT/REPLACE) — it's the only way to do SQL-based ingestion natively. (2) Simple window functions on Druid data (cumulative sums, ranks). (3) Periodic batch jobs that don't justify a separate Spark cluster. Use Spark/Trino for: (1) Heavy multi-table JOINs. (2) Complex ETL transformations. (3) Ad-hoc queries requiring full ANSI SQL. The typical pattern is: Spark for heavy ETL → writes to Kafka or S3 → Druid ingests for serving → MSE for data corrections and backfills.

Section 17 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
MSE purposeMulti-stage DAG execution for complex queriesWindow functions, JOINs, INSERT/REPLACE
Latency5–60 seconds (vs 50–500ms scatter-gather)Never use in real-time dashboards
ShuffleRepartitions data between stages by keyEnable durable shuffle storage (S3) for production
Runs onMiddleManagers (shared with streaming)Use worker categories (Druid 28+) to isolate batch
maxNumTasksControls worker parallelismSet conservatively to avoid starving Kafka ingestion
vs Spark/TrinoSimpler ops, limited capabilitiesMSE for ingestion + simple analytics; Spark for heavy ETL
Part 7 — Section 18

Advanced Compaction Strategies

Turning 50 tiny streaming segments into 2 optimally-sized, perfectly-rolled-up, partition-prunable segments — automatically.

18 — Advanced Compaction Strategies

18.1 Why Compaction Matters

Streaming ingestion creates segments optimized for write throughput, not read performance. Each Kafka task produces its own segments — sized by whatever data arrived during its taskDuration window. The result: dozens of small, unpartitioned, best-effort-rollup segments per time interval. This directly hurts query performance:

  • Many small segments: a query on 1 day of data opens 50 files instead of 2 — each file open has fixed overhead (mmap syscall, metadata parse, bitmap load)
  • No partition pruning: streaming segments use NumberedShardSpec (sequential) — WHERE country='US' cannot skip any shards
  • Imperfect rollup: rows with identical dimensions arriving in different task windows aren't collapsed — rollup ratio is 5–10× instead of the optimal 50–100×
  • Suboptimal compression: small segments have fewer rows for dictionary and LZ4 to work with — worse compression ratio

Compaction is the process of reading existing segments, merging and re-building them into fewer, larger, better-optimized segments. It is the bridge between streaming's write-optimized output and the read-optimized format Druid needs for sub-second queries.

Figure 18.1 — Compaction merges 50 small streaming segments into 2 optimally-sized, range-partitioned segments with perfect rollup

18.2 Automatic Compaction — Coordinator-Managed

The Coordinator runs an automatic compaction scheduler that continuously evaluates datasources and triggers compaction tasks when needed. This is the recommended production approach — set it once and forget it.

How auto-compaction works:

  1. On each Coordinator cycle (druid.coordinator.period), it scans each datasource's segment inventory
  2. For each time interval, it checks: are the segments already compacted to the target spec?
  3. If not (too many small segments, wrong partitioning, imperfect rollup), it submits a compaction task to the Overlord
  4. The task reads existing segments, re-aggregates with perfect rollup, re-partitions, and produces new optimized segments
  5. New segments are published with a new version → old segments overshadowed → eventually cleaned up by kill tasks
Auto-Compaction Configuration — via APIJSON
// POST to /druid/coordinator/v1/config/compaction
{
  "dataSource": "user_events",
  "taskPriority": 25,                // Lower than ingestion (50) to avoid starvation
  "inputSegmentSizeBytes": 419430400,  // 400MB — skip intervals already well-compacted
  "skipOffsetFromLatest": "PT1H",     // Don't compact last hour (still streaming)
  "maxRowsPerSegment": 5000000,       // Target 5M rows per output segment
  "tuningConfig": {
    "type": "index_parallel",
    "maxNumConcurrentSubTasks": 4,    // Parallel compaction workers
    "partitionsSpec": {
      "type": "hashed",               // Hash-partition for pruning
      "numShards": 4,                  // 4 shards per time interval
      "partitionDimensions": ["country"]  // Hash on country → partition pruning
    }
  },
  "granularitySpec": {
    "segmentGranularity": "DAY",       // One interval per day
    "queryGranularity": "MINUTE",      // Rollup to minute granularity
    "rollup": true                     // Enable perfect rollup
  }
}

18.3 Partitioning Strategies for Compaction

The partitioning strategy chosen during compaction determines whether queries can prune entire segments. This is the highest-impact compaction configuration:

StrategyHow It WorksQuery BenefitCompaction SpeedWhen to Use
Dynamic (default)No repartitioning — segments split only by size❌ No partition pruning⚡ Fastest — no shuffleWhen you don't filter on any dimension consistently
HashedHash selected dimension(s) → rows with same hash go to same shard✅ Equality pruning on hashed dimsMedium — requires shuffleWhen queries filter on specific dimension values
Range (single dim)Sort by one dimension → ranges assigned to shards✅ Strong equality + range pruningSlower — global sort neededWhen queries filter on one primary dimension
Range (multi dim)Sort by multiple dimensions → compound range shards✅ Pruning on first + second dimensionSlowest — multi-key sortWhen queries filter on 2 primary dimensions together
⚙️ Engineering Insight Switching from dynamic to hashed partitioning on your most-filtered dimension is the single most impactful compaction optimization. Example: a datasource with 10 countries. With dynamic partitioning, WHERE country='US' scans all shards. With "partitionDimensions": ["country"] + "numShards": 4, the same query skips 3 of 4 shards — 75% less data scanned. The compaction task takes longer (needs to shuffle data by hash), but this is a one-time cost that pays off on every subsequent query.

18.4 Compaction Lifecycle — Continuous Optimization

skipOffsetFromLatest: don't compact data still being streamed (default: PT1H) 2. inputSegmentSizeBytes: skip intervals already well-sized (default: 400MB) 3. taskPriority=25: lower than ingestion (50) to avoid starvation 4. New segments atomically overshadow old — zero query impact
Figure 18.2 — Compaction runs continuously: streaming creates small segments → Coordinator detects → task merges → new segments overshadow old → kill task cleans S3

18.5 Compaction Priority and Resource Management

Compaction tasks compete with ingestion tasks for MiddleManager resources. Getting the priority and resource allocation right prevents both compaction stalls and ingestion starvation:

StrategyConfigurationProsCons
Default prioritytaskPriority=25 (ingestion=50)Ingestion never starved — compaction uses idle capacityCompaction may fall behind during peak ingestion
Dedicated workersSeparate MiddleManager fleet for compaction (workerCategory="batch", Druid 28+)Complete isolation — no contention at allExtra infra cost, idle capacity during off-peak
Time-based schedulingUse external scheduler (Airflow) to trigger compaction during off-peak hoursMaximum control over when compaction runsManual management, gaps possible
skipOffsetFromLatest"PT14D" (default) or "PT1H"Avoids compacting data still being modifiedPT14D means 2-week lag before compaction
⚠️ Common Pitfall Leaving skipOffsetFromLatest at the default PT14D (14 days). This means streaming data remains uncompacted for 2 weeks — 14 days of suboptimal segments with poor rollup, no partition pruning, and many small files. For dashboards querying "last 7 days," you're hitting uncompacted data 100% of the time. Reduce to "PT1H" or "PT2H" to compact data as soon as streaming has moved past it.

18.6 Monitoring Compaction Health

Compaction is a background process that can silently fall behind, slowly degrading query performance. Monitor these signals:

Metric / SignalWhere to CheckHealthy ValueAction if Unhealthy
Segments per intervalDruid Console → Datasources → Segments<10 per DAY interval (post-compaction)Check compaction config; may need more workers
Compaction task queueOverlord → Task queue<5 pending compaction tasksIncrease maxNumConcurrentSubTasks or add MMs
Segment count trendPrometheus: segment/count over timeStable or decreasingGrowing = compaction falling behind
Compaction status APIGET /druid/coordinator/v1/compaction/status"bytesAwaitingCompaction": 0Non-zero = intervals waiting for compaction
Average segment sizeDruid Console → segment detail300–700MB per segmentToo small = compaction not running or misconfigured
Compaction Status API — Check Healthbash
# Check compaction progress per datasource
curl -s http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/compaction/status \
  | jq '.latestStatus[] | {dataSource, bytesCompacted, bytesAwaitingCompaction, segmentCountCompacted, segmentCountAwaitingCompaction}'

# Example healthy output:
# {
#   "dataSource": "user_events",
#   "bytesCompacted": 1234567890,
#   "bytesAwaitingCompaction": 0,            ← should be 0
#   "segmentCountCompacted": 730,
#   "segmentCountAwaitingCompaction": 0      ← should be 0
# }

# Alert if segmentCountAwaitingCompaction > 100 for > 1 hour

18.7 Compaction via SQL REPLACE

For maximum control, you can run compaction manually using REPLACE INTO — reading from the Druid datasource itself and writing back with better partitioning:

Manual Compaction via SQL REPLACESQL
REPLACE INTO "user_events"
  OVERWRITE WHERE __time >= TIMESTAMP '2024-03-01'
                   AND __time < TIMESTAMP '2024-03-02'
SELECT
    __time,
    country,
    platform,
    event_type,
    SUM(event_count) AS event_count,         -- Re-aggregate for perfect rollup
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS revenue_sum,
    DS_HLL(unique_users) AS unique_users     -- Merge HLL sketches
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= TIMESTAMP '2024-03-01'
  AND __time < TIMESTAMP '2024-03-02'
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
PARTITIONED BY DAY
CLUSTERED BY "country"

-- This reads existing Druid segments, re-aggregates with perfect rollup,
-- and writes back with range-partitioning on country.
-- Atomic: old segments only replaced after all new ones are published.
🎯 Interview Tip "Why is compaction necessary if Druid has rollup at ingestion?" — Streaming ingestion provides best-effort rollup: it only collapses rows within a single task's memory window. Events arriving in different Kafka tasks for the same dimension combination are NOT collapsed. Compaction reads all segments for a time interval, re-aggregates globally to achieve perfect rollup, and optionally re-partitions segments for better pruning. It's the two-phase approach: streaming for real-time freshness, compaction for optimal storage and query performance.

18.8 Compaction Impact — Real-World Numbers

Production case study from a cluster ingesting 500M events/day with HOUR segment granularity and country + platform dimensions:

MetricBefore CompactionAfter CompactionImprovement
Segments per day480 (24h × 4 tasks × 5 shards)48 (24h × 2 shards)10× fewer
Average segment size15 MB350 MB23× larger (better)
Rollup ratio8× (best-effort)65× (perfect)8× better rollup
Total storage (30d)216 GB27 GB8× less storage
7-day query scan time420ms55ms7.6× faster
Partition pruningNot possible (numbered shards)75% shards skipped (hashed by country)∞ improvement
S3 deep storage cost$52/month$6.50/month8× cheaper
⚙️ Engineering Insight Compaction is the single operation that improves every dimension of performance simultaneously: query latency (fewer segments to scan), storage cost (better rollup = smaller total data), query concurrency (less I/O per query = more capacity), and deep storage cost (smaller segments in S3). If you do only one optimization on a Druid cluster, make it compaction with hashed partitioning on your primary filter dimension.

Section 18 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
Why compactStreaming = many small, unpartitioned, imperfect-rollup segmentsAlways enable auto-compaction on all datasources
PartitioningHashed/range enables partition pruning at query timeHash on most-filtered dimension (e.g., country)
skipOffsetFromLatestDon't compact data still being streamedReduce to PT1H from default PT14D
PriorityCompaction must not starve streaming ingestiontaskPriority=25 (ingestion=50) or dedicated workers
MonitoringCompaction can silently fall behindAlert on segmentCountAwaitingCompaction > 100
ImpactImproves latency, storage, concurrency, cost simultaneouslyHighest-ROI single optimization on any Druid cluster
Part 7 — Section 19

Multi-Level Caching Architecture

Three cache layers that turn 500ms queries into 2ms cache hits — and the invalidation traps that break them.

19 — Multi-Level Caching Architecture

19.1 Cache Layer Overview

Druid implements a three-level caching hierarchy designed to short-circuit expensive computations at the earliest possible stage. When a cache hits, the query skips all downstream work — segment loading, bitmap evaluation, column scanning, and aggregation are all avoided. The three levels serve different purposes and have different hit patterns:

  • L1 — Historical Segment Cache: caches per-segment query results on each Historical. Hits when the same sub-query targets the same immutable segment.
  • L2 — Broker Result Cache: caches the complete, merged query result on the Broker. Hits when the exact same SQL is repeated.
  • L3 — External Shared Cache (Memcached/Redis): shared cache across all Brokers. Eliminates cold-start when a Broker restarts or a new Broker is added.
Figure 19.1 — Three cache levels: L2 (Broker result) catches exact repeated queries; L1 (Historical segment) catches repeated sub-queries; L3 (shared Memcached) eliminates cold-start across Brokers

19.2 L1 — Historical Segment Result Cache

Each Historical maintains an in-memory cache of per-segment query results. When the Broker fans out the same (or overlapping) query, any segments already computed can return from cache instantly.

  • Cache key: hash of (segment ID + query JSON) — uniquely identifies a specific sub-query on a specific segment
  • Invalidation: segments are immutable, so cache entries never go stale — they're valid forever until the segment is replaced by a newer version (compaction/REPLACE). When a segment is dropped, its cache entries are evicted.
  • Implementation: Caffeine (default) or MapDB. Caffeine uses an in-JVM LRU cache with O(1) access.
  • Hit pattern: a dashboard query for "last 7 days" → 6 of 7 days are on immutable historical segments that haven't changed → 85% L1 hit rate on repeated dashboards
⚙️ Engineering Insight L1 segment-level cache is more effective than L2 result-level cache because of immutability. Even if the query's time filter shifts by 1 hour (dashboard auto-refresh), 23 of the 24 hourly segments are unchanged — L1 hits on all 23. Only the latest hour's segment is a cache miss. L2 would miss entirely because the SQL string changed. This is why segment-level caching is Druid's primary caching strategy.

19.3 L2 — Broker Result Cache

The Broker caches the complete merged result of a query. When the exact same SQL (with identical parameters and context) is submitted again, the Broker returns the cached result without any fan-out to Historicals.

  • Cache key: hash of the complete native query JSON (after Calcite translation)
  • Hit pattern: exact same dashboard panel refreshed by different users → L2 hit
  • Invalidation: TTL-based (druid.broker.cache.resultLevelCacheLimit), or when underlying segments change (versioning)
  • Latency on hit: ~1–2ms (hash lookup + JSON serialization)
⚠️ Common Pitfall Dashboard queries with CURRENT_TIMESTAMP in the time filter: WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY. Every query execution has a different CURRENT_TIMESTAMP value → different SQL string → different cache key → zero L2 cache hits. Fix: round the timestamp to a 5-minute boundary: WHERE __time >= TIME_FLOOR(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, 'PT5M') - INTERVAL '7' DAY. Now all queries within a 5-minute window share the same cache key → ~90%+ L2 hit rate for repeated dashboards.

19.4 L3 — External Shared Cache (Memcached/Redis)

An external shared cache that sits outside Druid JVMs and is accessible by all Brokers. It stores the same segment-level results as L1, but shared across the fleet.

  • Purpose: when a Broker restarts or a new Broker is added to the pool, it has an empty L1/L2 cache. L3 provides warm cache entries immediately — no cold-start penalty.
