  • Implementation: Memcached (most common) or Redis
  • Trade-off: network round-trip to Memcached (~0.5–1ms) vs compute cost of segment scan (50–500ms). Clear win for any cache hit.
  • Sizing: typically 10–50GB across Memcached fleet. Size based on working set of frequently queried segment results.

19.5 Complete Cache Configuration

Multi-Level Cache — Full Production Configproperties
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# L1 — Historical Segment Result Cache
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
druid.historical.cache.useCache=true          # Read from L1 cache
druid.historical.cache.populateCache=true      # Write scan results to L1

# Caffeine (in-JVM) — recommended for L1
druid.cache.type=caffeine
druid.cache.sizeInBytes=2147483648            # 2GB — from JVM heap
druid.cache.expireAfter=3600000               # 1 hour TTL (safety net)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# L2 — Broker Result-Level Cache
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
druid.broker.cache.useCache=true              # Read from L1 on Historical (via Broker)
druid.broker.cache.populateCache=true         # Populate L1 on Historical scan
druid.broker.cache.useResultLevelCache=true   # Enable L2 result cache on Broker
druid.broker.cache.populateResultLevelCache=true
druid.broker.cache.resultLevelCacheLimit=10000  # Max entries in L2

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# L3 — External Shared Cache (Memcached)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# Override L1 cache type on Broker to use Memcached for shared access
druid.cache.type=memcached
druid.cache.hosts=memcached-1:11211,memcached-2:11211
druid.cache.expiration=3600                   # 1 hour TTL
druid.cache.timeout=500                       # 500ms timeout — don't block on cache miss
druid.cache.maxObjectSize=52428800            # 50MB max per cache entry
⚙️ Engineering Insight Which cache layer to prioritize? In most production deployments, L1 (Historical segment cache) provides the highest hit rate and best ROI because of Druid's immutability model. L2 (Broker result cache) is a bonus for exact-repeat queries but fragile (any parameter change = miss). L3 (Memcached) is most valuable for clusters with frequent Broker restarts or autoscaling. Start with L1 + L2 enabled; add L3 only if you observe cold-start latency spikes after Broker deployments.

19.6 Cache Invalidation — Why Immutability Makes It Simple

Cache invalidation is famously one of the hardest problems in computer science. Druid sidesteps most of this complexity through segment immutability:

Cache LevelInvalidation TriggerHow It WorksComplexity
L1 (segment)Segment replaced by newer version (compaction/REPLACE)Old segment dropped → its cache entries evicted. New segment = new cache key → cold.✅ Trivial — immutability guarantees correctness
L2 (result)TTL expiry, or underlying segments changedDruid tracks segment versions per query interval. If any segment version changes, L2 entries for that interval are invalidated.⚠️ Moderate — version tracking required
L3 (Memcached)TTL expiry onlyMemcached doesn't know about segment versions — relies on TTL. Stale reads possible for TTL duration after compaction.⚠️ Stale risk — set TTL appropriately
⚠️ Common Pitfall After compaction creates new segment versions, L3 Memcached may serve stale results for up to druid.cache.expiration seconds. If your compaction runs hourly and TTL is 1 hour, there's a window where dashboards show pre-compaction results (which are correct data, just from less-optimized segments — the actual values don't change). This is cosmetic for most use cases, but for latency-sensitive monitoring (where you're tracking query performance), it can be confusing. Solution: reduce Memcached TTL to 10–15 minutes for frequently compacted datasources.

19.7 Cache Hit Rate Optimization

A healthy Druid cluster should achieve 40–80% overall cache hit rate for dashboard workloads. Below that, queries are doing unnecessary work. Here are the key optimization techniques:

TechniqueImpact on Hit RateHow to ImplementTrade-off
Round time filters to 5-min boundariesL2: 0% → 90%+TIME_FLOOR(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, 'PT5M')Up to 5-min stale data on dashboard — usually acceptable
Increase L1 cache sizeL1: +10–30%druid.cache.sizeInBytes=4294967296 (4GB)Uses JVM heap — may increase GC pressure
Enable L3 MemcachedCold-start: 0% → 60%+Deploy Memcached fleet + configureExtra infrastructure, network latency per check
Use immutable time ranges in dashboardL1: +20%"Last 7 complete days" instead of "last 168 hours from now"Dashboard shows completed days only
Compact segments (fewer versions)L1: fewer invalidationsRun compaction with stable partition specCompaction itself temporarily invalidates L1
Standardize query patternsL2: +30%Dashboard templates produce identical SQL per panelLimits ad-hoc flexibility
Cache-Friendly Time Filter PatternSQL
-- ❌ BAD: different CURRENT_TIMESTAMP every execution → zero L2 cache hits
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY

-- ✅ GOOD: rounded to 5-minute boundary → same SQL for 5 minutes → L2 hits
WHERE __time >= TIME_FLOOR(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, 'PT5M') - INTERVAL '7' DAY

-- ✅ BETTER: use complete days for maximum L1 hits
WHERE __time >= TIME_FLOOR(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, 'P1D') - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND __time < TIME_FLOOR(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, 'P1D')

-- This gives aligned day boundaries:
-- All 7 segments are immutable (completed days) → 100% L1 hit on repeat
-- For "today" data: add a separate real-time panel with a tighter filter

19.8 Monitoring Cache Health

MetricPrometheus NameHealthy ValueAction if Unhealthy
L1 hit ratedruid_historical_cache_hit_rate>50% for dashboardsIncrease sizeInBytes; check if compaction invalidating too often
L2 hit ratedruid_broker_cache_result_hit_rate>30% for dashboardsRound time filters; standardize query templates
L1 evictions/secdruid_cache_eviction_count<100/secCache too small — increase size or add Historical memory
L3 timeout ratedruid_cache_timeout_count<1%Memcached overloaded — scale fleet or increase timeout
Cache size bytesdruid_cache_total_bytes<80% of configured maxNear 100% = constant eviction churn — increase max
Grafana Dashboard Query — Cache Hit RatePromQL
# Overall L1 cache hit rate per Historical
rate(druid_historical_cache_hit_count[5m])
  / (
    rate(druid_historical_cache_hit_count[5m])
    + rate(druid_historical_cache_miss_count[5m])
  )

# Alert: L1 hit rate below 30% for 15 minutes
# → indicates cache too small, compaction churn, or non-cacheable query patterns

19.9 Cache Architecture Decision Matrix

Cluster ProfileL1 (Historical)L2 (Broker Result)L3 (Memcached)Rationale
Small team dashboards
(<10 users, 3 Brokers)		✅ 2GB✅ Enabled❌ Not neededFew users → L2 catches repeated queries. L3 overkill.
Medium production
(50 users, 5 Brokers)		✅ 4GB✅ Enabled⚠️ OptionalL1+L2 handle most hits. Add L3 if Broker deploys cause latency spikes.
Large multi-tenant
(500 users, 10+ Brokers)		✅ 4–8GB✅ Enabled✅ 20–50GB fleetMany Brokers + autoscaling = frequent cold starts. L3 essential.
Ad-hoc heavy
(analysts, varied queries)		✅ 2GB❌ Low value❌ Low valueQueries rarely repeat exactly → L2/L3 miss rate high. Invest in L1 + vectorize.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid's caching architecture leverage immutability?" — Druid's segments are immutable — once built, they never change. This means L1 cache entries for a given segment are valid forever (no invalidation needed). When a dashboard queries "last 7 days," 6 of 7 days hit L1 cache on repeated access — only today's data misses because it's still being streamed. This 85%+ baseline hit rate is free because immutability eliminates the cache invalidation problem entirely. Compare with PostgreSQL where any UPDATE invalidates cached query results — Druid's model is fundamentally simpler and more cache-friendly.

Section 19 — Key Takeaways

Cache LevelWhat It CachesHit LatencyKey Optimization
L1 (Historical)Per-segment query results~0.1ms (in-JVM)Immutability = never stale. Size to 2–8GB. Highest ROI.
L2 (Broker)Complete merged query result~1–2msRound time filters to 5-min boundaries for hit rate.
L3 (Memcached)Shared segment results across Brokers~0.5–1ms (network)Eliminates cold-start. Essential for large/autoscaled clusters.
Target: 40–80% overall hit rate for dashboard workloads
Part 8 — Section 20

Ingestion Transforms, Filters, and Schema Evolution

Reshaping data at the ingestion boundary — computed columns, row filtering, nested JSON flattening, and evolving schemas without downtime.

20 — Ingestion Transforms, Filters, and Schema Evolution

20.1 The Transform Spec — Reshaping Data at Ingestion

The transformSpec sits between raw input parsing and segment building. It applies row-level transformations and filters before data enters the segment — reducing storage, improving rollup ratios, and enriching data without external ETL:

Figure 20.1 — Transforms and filters execute after parsing but before rollup — modifying and filtering data before it enters the segment

20.2 Expression Transforms — Computed Columns at Ingestion

Transforms create new derived columns from raw input using Druid's expression language. These computed columns participate in rollup and indexing just like raw columns — but are computed once at ingestion, not on every query:

transformSpec — Production ExampleJSON
{
  "transformSpec": {
    "transforms": [
      {
        // Currency conversion — compute USD amount at ingestion
        "type": "expression",
        "name": "revenue_usd",
        "expression": "case_simple(currency, 'EUR', revenue * 1.08, 'GBP', revenue * 1.27, 'JPY', revenue * 0.0067, revenue)"
      },
      {
        // Extract hour-of-day for time-of-day analysis
        "type": "expression",
        "name": "hour_of_day",
        "expression": "timestamp_extract(__time, 'HOUR', 'UTC')"
      },
      {
        // Categorize HTTP status codes into buckets
        "type": "expression",
        "name": "status_category",
        "expression": "case_simple(div(http_status, 100), 2, '2xx_success', 3, '3xx_redirect', 4, '4xx_client_error', 5, '5xx_server_error', 'other')"
      },
      {
        // Reduce browser version cardinality: "Chrome/120.0.6099.130" → "Chrome"
        "type": "expression",
        "name": "browser_family",
        "expression": "regexp_extract(user_agent, '^([A-Za-z]+)', 1)"
      },
      {
        // Boolean flag for high-value transactions
        "type": "expression",
        "name": "is_high_value",
        "expression": "if(revenue > 100, 'true', 'false')"
      }
    ]
  }
}
⚙️ Engineering Insight Transforms that reduce cardinality are the most valuable. Replacing user_agent (100K+ unique values) with browser_family (5 values) dramatically improves rollup ratio. A 100K-cardinality dimension that becomes a 5-cardinality dimension can improve rollup by 20,000×. Always ask: "can I derive a lower-cardinality version of this dimension at ingestion time?"

20.3 Ingestion-Time Filters — Drop Before Storage

The filter in transformSpec drops rows before they enter the segment. Filtered rows are never stored, never indexed, and never scanned. This is the cheapest possible way to reduce data volume:

Ingestion Filter — Drop Unwanted EventsJSON
{
  "transformSpec": {
    "transforms": [ /* ... transforms from above ... */ ],
    "filter": {
      "type": "and",
      "fields": [
        {
          // Drop heartbeat/health-check events (30% of traffic)
          "type": "not",
          "field": {
            "type": "selector",
            "dimension": "event_type",
            "value": "heartbeat"
          }
        },
        {
          // Drop zero-revenue events (noise)
          "type": "bound",
          "dimension": "revenue",
          "lower": "0",
          "lowerStrict": true,
          "ordering": "numeric"
        },
        {
          // Drop test traffic (internal QA)
          "type": "not",
          "field": {
            "type": "selector",
            "dimension": "is_test",
            "value": "true"
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
// Impact: dropping heartbeats + zero-revenue + test traffic
// typically eliminates 30–50% of raw events before storage
Filter Use CaseTraffic ReductionStorage Savings
Drop heartbeat/health-check events20–40%Proportional — often the #1 win
Drop internal test traffic5–15%Eliminates noise from analytics
Drop zero-value events (revenue=0, duration=0)10–30%Fewer rows = better rollup ratio
Drop bot/crawler traffic15–40%Cleaner user analytics
Drop events outside retention windowVariesLate-arriving data outside useful range
⚠️ Common Pitfall Applying ingestion filters on Kafka streaming without updating the filter in the supervisor spec AND restarting the supervisor. Simply editing the spec on the Overlord API is not enough — you must POST /druid/indexer/v1/supervisor with the updated spec to take effect on the next task generation. Running tasks continue with the old filter until they complete and hand off.

20.4 Schema Auto-Discovery

Instead of explicitly listing every dimension, Druid can auto-detect dimensions from incoming data:

Enable Schema Auto-DiscoveryJSON
"dimensionsSpec": {
    "useSchemaDiscovery": true,
    "dimensionExclusions": [
        "request_id",    // Exclude high-cardinality UUIDs
        "session_id",    // Exclude session identifiers
        "raw_body"       // Exclude large unstructured text
    ]
}
// Druid auto-detects: country, platform, event_type, device_os, etc.
// New fields appearing in future events are automatically added to new segments
// Old segments retain their original schema — queries across both work (NULLs for missing dims)
⚠️ Common Pitfall Auto-discovery with variable JSON schemas can create hundreds of dimensions from inconsistent data. Each adds bitmap overhead and reduces rollup. A single malformed event with 50 nested fields creates 50 new dimensions — permanently. Always combine auto-discovery with dimensionExclusions and monitor dimension count per datasource. For production, explicit dimension lists are safer than auto-discovery.

20.5 Schema Evolution Patterns

Druid datasources evolve over time — new dimensions appear, old ones become irrelevant, types need to change. Understanding how schema changes propagate is critical for zero-downtime operations:

Change TypeHow to Do ItImpact on Old SegmentsImpact on Queries
Add new dimensionAdd to dimensionsSpec in ingestion specOld segments don't have itQueries return NULL for old data — transparent
Remove dimensionRemove from dimensionsSpecOld segments still have itQueries still work on old segments — dimension just isn't in new ones
Change type
(e.g., STRING → LONG)		Cannot change in place. Add new dimension with new type, reingest historical data with REPLACE INTOMust reingest affected intervalsMixed types across segments cause query errors — reingest to fix
Rename dimensionAdd new name as transform ("expression": "old_name"), reingest historical dataMust reingest affected intervalsUse transform to alias during transition period
Add new metricAdd to metricsSpecOld segments don't have itAggregation returns 0 or NULL for old data
Change rollup granularityUpdate queryGranularity in spec + reingest via REPLACE INTOOld segments have old granularityMixed granularity works but may be confusing
⚙️ Engineering Insight The safest schema evolution strategy is "add, never modify, reingest when ready": (1) Add the new dimension/metric to the ingestion spec — new segments include it, old segments return NULL. (2) Verify queries work correctly with the new field. (3) When ready, run REPLACE INTO on historical intervals to backfill the new field. (4) Only then remove the old field from the spec. This approach has zero downtime and zero query impact at every step.

20.6 Flatten Specs for Nested JSON Input

When raw input is nested JSON, the flattenSpec extracts nested fields into top-level dimensions before transforms and rollup. This runs inside the input parser — before transformSpec:

Flatten Spec — Extracting Nested JSON FieldsJSON
"inputFormat": {
  "type": "json",
  "flattenSpec": {
    "useFieldDiscovery": true,  // Auto-discover top-level fields
    "fields": [
      {
        // JSONPath — extract nested object field
        "type": "path",
        "name": "device_os",
        "expr": "$.device.os"
      },
      {
        "type": "path",
        "name": "device_type",
        "expr": "$.device.type"
      },
      {
        "type": "path",
        "name": "geo_country",
        "expr": "$.geo.country"
      },
      {
        // JQ expression — join array into CSV string
        "type": "jq",
        "name": "tags_csv",
        "expr": ".tags | join(\",\")"
      },
      {
        // Deep nested with array index
        "type": "path",
        "name": "first_item_sku",
        "expr": "$.cart.items[0].sku"
      }
    ]
  }
}

// Input event:
// {
//   "event_time": "2024-03-15T10:00:00Z",
//   "user_id": "alice",
//   "device": { "os": "iOS", "type": "mobile" },
//   "geo": { "country": "US", "city": "NYC" },
//   "tags": ["premium", "returning"],
//   "cart": { "items": [{ "sku": "A123", "qty": 2 }] }
// }
//
// After flattening → top-level columns:
// device_os="iOS", device_type="mobile", geo_country="US",
// tags_csv="premium,returning", first_item_sku="A123"

Flatten Spec vs Nested JSON Columns — When to Use Which

ApproachWhen to UseProsCons
flattenSpecKnown, stable nested structure; fields used in WHERE/GROUP BYFull bitmap indexing, participates in rollup, best query performanceSchema must be defined upfront; changes require spec update
Nested JSON column
(COMPLEX<json>)		Variable/evolving structure; exploration phase; many nested pathsSchema-on-read — no upfront definition needed; auto-flattened sub-columnsLarger segments; partial bitmap support; less efficient than pre-flattened
Both combinedCore fields flattened for performance + remaining JSON stored as nested column for flexibilityBest of both — known fields indexed, unknown fields queryableMore complex ingestion spec; larger segments

20.7 Transform Expression Language Reference

Druid's expression language is used in transforms, virtual columns, and filtered aggregations. Key functions for ingestion-time transforms:

CategoryFunctionsExample
Conditionalif(cond, then, else), case_simple(expr, v1, r1, v2, r2, default), nvl(expr, default)if(revenue > 100, 'high', 'low')
Stringconcat(a, b), substring(s, start, len), lower(s), upper(s), regexp_extract(s, pattern, group), replace(s, search, replace)regexp_extract(url, '/([^/]+)$', 1)
Math+ - * /, div(a,b), mod(a,b), abs(x), round(x, digits), log(x), pow(x,y)round(revenue * exchange_rate, 2)
Timetimestamp_extract(__time, 'HOUR', 'UTC'), timestamp_shift(__time, 'PT1H', 1), timestamp_format(__time, 'yyyy-MM-dd')timestamp_extract(__time, 'DOW', 'UTC')
Null handlingnvl(x, default), isnull(x), notnull(x)nvl(country, 'unknown')
Arrayarray_length(arr), array_contains(arr, val), string_to_array(s, delim)array_length(tags)
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you handle currency conversion in a multi-currency Druid datasource?" — Use an ingestion-time transform: case_simple(currency, 'EUR', revenue * rate_eur, 'GBP', revenue * rate_gbp, revenue) to create a revenue_usd column. This is computed once at ingestion — not on every query. For dynamic exchange rates, either: (1) use a lookup table that the transform references (limited support), or (2) normalize in a Kafka Streams / Flink pipeline before Druid ingestion. Approach (2) is more flexible and the production standard.

20.8 SQL-Based Transforms (INSERT INTO Alternative)

For modern Druid (25+), SQL-based ingestion with INSERT INTO provides a cleaner alternative to JSON transform specs for batch data:

SQL-Based Transforms — Cleaner Than JSON SpecSQL
INSERT INTO "user_events"
SELECT
    TIME_PARSE("event_time") AS __time,

    -- Standard dimensions
    "country",
    "platform",
    "event_type",

    -- Transform: reduce cardinality
    CASE
        WHEN "http_status" BETWEEN 200 AND 299 THEN '2xx'
        WHEN "http_status" BETWEEN 400 AND 499 THEN '4xx'
        WHEN "http_status" BETWEEN 500 AND 599 THEN '5xx'
        ELSE 'other'
    END AS status_category,

    -- Transform: currency conversion
    CASE "currency"
        WHEN 'EUR' THEN "revenue" * 1.08
        WHEN 'GBP' THEN "revenue" * 1.27
        ELSE "revenue"
    END AS revenue_usd,

    -- Metrics with rollup
    COUNT(*) AS event_count,
    SUM("revenue") AS revenue_sum

FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...))
WHERE "event_type" != 'heartbeat'     -- Filter at ingestion
  AND "revenue" > 0                     -- Drop zero-revenue
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
PARTITIONED BY DAY
CLUSTERED BY "country"

-- SQL provides: transforms (CASE), filters (WHERE), rollup (GROUP BY),
-- and partitioning (CLUSTERED BY) — all in one readable statement.
-- Much cleaner than equivalent JSON transformSpec.
⚙️ Engineering Insight For batch ingestion, SQL-based INSERT INTO with inline transforms is now the recommended approach over JSON ingestion specs. It's more readable, easier to test (run as a SELECT first to validate), and supports the full SQL expression language. Reserve JSON transformSpec for Kafka streaming ingestion where SQL-based ingestion isn't applicable (streaming still requires JSON supervisor specs).

Section 20 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
TransformsComputed columns at ingestion timeReduce cardinality (browser_family), normalize currency, extract time parts
Ingestion filtersDrop rows before storageDrop heartbeats, test traffic, zero-value events — 30–50% savings typical
Schema discoveryAuto-detect dimensions from dataUse sparingly — explicit dims safer for production. Always set exclusions.
Schema evolutionAdd/remove/change columns over time"Add, never modify, reingest when ready" — zero downtime pattern
Flatten specExtract nested JSON to top-level dimsUse for known stable paths; nested JSON column for variable structures
SQL transformsCASE/expressions in INSERT INTOPreferred over JSON transformSpec for all batch ingestion
Part 8 — Section 21

Nested Columns, Complex Types, and ARRAY Support

Schema-on-read with columnar performance — how Druid handles semi-structured JSON, sketch types, and native arrays.

21 — Nested Columns, Complex Types, and ARRAY Support

21.1 Nested JSON Column Type (Druid 25+)

Druid 25 introduced native COMPLEX<json> columns that store entire nested JSON objects within a single segment column. Instead of pre-flattening every JSON path at ingestion time, you ingest the raw JSON and query it with JSON_VALUE() at read time — schema-on-read with near-columnar performance.

The key innovation: Druid doesn't store the JSON as a blob. It auto-flattens the nested structure into internal sub-columns with dedicated dictionary encoding and bitmap indexes per JSON path. This means JSON_VALUE(col, '$.device.os') reads a pre-built sub-column — not parsing raw JSON at query time.

Figure 21.1 — Nested JSON is auto-flattened into sub-columns with dedicated dictionaries and bitmap indexes — not stored as raw blobs
Querying Nested JSON ColumnsSQL
-- Ingest: store event_properties as nested JSON column
-- dimensionsSpec: { "dimensions": [
--   { "type": "json", "name": "event_properties" }
-- ]}

-- Query: extract + filter + group on nested paths
SELECT
    JSON_VALUE(event_properties, '$.device.os' RETURNING VARCHAR) AS device_os,
    JSON_VALUE(event_properties, '$.device.type' RETURNING VARCHAR) AS device_type,
    COUNT(*) AS events,
    SUM(CAST(JSON_VALUE(event_properties, '$.revenue' RETURNING DOUBLE) AS DOUBLE)) AS total_rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
  AND JSON_VALUE(event_properties, '$.device.type' RETURNING VARCHAR) = 'mobile'
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY events DESC

-- The WHERE filter on $.device.type uses the auto-built bitmap index
-- → same performance as a pre-flattened top-level dimension
-- Typical latency: 100–400ms (comparable to standard GroupBy)
⚙️ Engineering Insight Nested JSON columns are ideal for event properties bags — fields that vary across event types (e.g., "purchase" events have cart_total, "search" events have query_text, "video" events have duration_ms). Instead of defining dozens of sparse dimensions (most NULL for any given event type), store all event-specific properties in a single COMPLEX<json> column. Query specific paths as needed.

21.2 Nested JSON vs Pre-Flattened Dimensions — Performance Trade-offs

AspectPre-Flattened (flattenSpec)Nested JSON (COMPLEX<json>)
Schema definitionMust declare each path upfrontSchema-on-read — no upfront declaration
Query performance✅ Best — first-class dimension with full bitmap⚠️ Near-equal for indexed paths; ~20% slower for non-indexed
Rollup participation✅ Full — flattened dims drive rollup groups❌ Nested columns are treated as single value — breaks rollup
Bitmap indexing✅ Full bitmap per unique value✅ Auto-built bitmaps per JSON path value (Druid 26+)
Segment sizeSmaller — only needed paths stored as columnsLarger — entire JSON stored + sub-column overhead
Schema evolutionRequires spec update + restart/reingest✅ Auto-adapts — new fields appear automatically
Best forStable, known schemas with high-volume queryingVariable schemas, exploration, event properties bags
⚠️ Common Pitfall Storing your primary filter/group dimensions (country, platform) inside a nested JSON column instead of as top-level dimensions. While the auto-built sub-column bitmaps work, nested JSON columns don't participate in rollup — the entire JSON blob is treated as the "dimension value" for rollup purposes, meaning every unique JSON object creates a separate rollup group. Result: rollup ratio plummets from 50× to ~1×. Rule: top-level dimensions for rollup-critical and high-frequency-filter fields; nested JSON for variable/sparse properties.

21.3 Complex Column Types

Beyond standard STRING/LONG/DOUBLE and nested JSON, Druid supports COMPLEX column types for specialized data structures. These are used exclusively as aggregation-only columns — they cannot appear in WHERE or GROUP BY:

Complex TypePurposeIngestion AggregatorQuery Function
COMPLEX<HLLSketch>Approximate distinct countHLLSketchBuildAPPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(col)
COMPLEX<thetaSketch>Distinct + set operationsthetaSketchTHETA_SKETCH_ESTIMATE(DS_THETA(...))
COMPLEX<quantilesDoublesSketch>Percentile distributionsquantilesDoublesSketchAPPROX_QUANTILE_DS(col, 0.95)
COMPLEX<json>Nested semi-structured dataDirect column typeJSON_VALUE(col, '$.path')
COMPLEX<serializablePairLongString>EARLIEST/LATEST aggregatorsstringFirst / stringLastEARLIEST(col) / LATEST(col)

Complex columns are stored as serialized binary blobs within segments. They're not dictionary-encoded or bitmap-indexed (except nested JSON which has special handling). Their only purpose is to be passed to their corresponding aggregation function at query time.

21.4 ARRAY Columns (Druid 28+)

Druid 28 introduced native ARRAY<STRING>, ARRAY<LONG>, and ARRAY<DOUBLE> column types with proper array semantics. These are distinct from multi-value dimensions and provide a cleaner data model for columns that naturally contain multiple values per row:

ARRAY Column Ingestion and QueryingSQL
-- Ingestion: tags is an ARRAY column
INSERT INTO "articles"
SELECT
    TIME_PARSE("published_at") AS __time,
    "author",
    "category",
    ARRAY["tag1", "tag2", "tag3"] AS tags,  -- Native ARRAY column
    COUNT(*) AS article_count
FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...))
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
PARTITIONED BY DAY

-- Query: filter and inspect arrays
SELECT
    tags,
    COUNT(*) AS article_count,
    ARRAY_LENGTH(tags) AS num_tags
FROM "articles"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND ARRAY_CONTAINS(tags, 'technology')   -- Filter: array contains value
GROUP BY tags
ORDER BY article_count DESC
LIMIT 20

-- UNNEST: explode array into individual rows
SELECT
    tag,
    COUNT(*) AS articles_with_tag
FROM "articles"
  CROSS JOIN UNNEST(tags) AS t(tag)
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
GROUP BY tag
ORDER BY articles_with_tag DESC
LIMIT 50

ARRAY SQL Functions

FunctionDescriptionExample
ARRAY_CONTAINS(arr, val)Returns true if array contains valueWHERE ARRAY_CONTAINS(tags, 'tech')
ARRAY_OVERLAP(arr1, arr2)Returns true if arrays share any elementWHERE ARRAY_OVERLAP(tags, ARRAY['tech','ai'])
ARRAY_LENGTH(arr)Returns number of elementsSELECT ARRAY_LENGTH(tags) AS num_tags
UNNEST(arr)Explodes array into rows (one per element)CROSS JOIN UNNEST(tags) AS t(tag)
ARRAY_CONCAT(arr1, arr2)Concatenates two arraysARRAY_CONCAT(tags, extra_tags)
ARRAY_SLICE(arr, start, end)Returns sub-arrayARRAY_SLICE(tags, 0, 3)
ARRAY[v1, v2, v3]Construct array literalWHERE ARRAY_OVERLAP(tags, ARRAY['a','b'])

21.5 Multi-Value Dimensions vs ARRAY Columns — Critical Differences

This is one of the most confusing areas in Druid. Multi-value dimensions (legacy) and ARRAY columns (modern) both store multiple values per row, but behave fundamentally differently in queries:

AspectMulti-Value Dimension (legacy)ARRAY Column (Druid 28+)
GROUP BY behaviorExplodes — one output row per value. tags=["a","b"] → 2 rowsGroups as array — the array itself is the group key
WHERE semantics= 'a' matches if 'a' is in the setUse ARRAY_CONTAINS(tags, 'a')
Rollup impactBreaks rollup (exploded rows = unique combos)Also breaks rollup (array value = unique combo)
Bitmap indexingPer unique value within the multi-value setOptional — configurable at ingestion
UNNEST supportImplicit (GROUP BY explodes automatically)Explicit via CROSS JOIN UNNEST()
CountingCOUNT(*) counts after explosion (inflated)COUNT(*) counts original rows (accurate)
RecommendedLegacy compatibility only✅ New deployments should use ARRAY
⚠️ Common Pitfall Multi-value dimensions silently inflate COUNT(*) results. If a row has tags=["a","b","c"] and you run SELECT tag, COUNT(*) FROM ds GROUP BY tag, that one row contributes 1 to each of "a", "b", and "c" — tripling the apparent count. With ARRAY columns and explicit UNNEST, this behavior is visible in the SQL. For new datasources, always use ARRAY columns to avoid surprise explosions in GROUP BY.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid handle tags or categories where a row has multiple values?" — Two approaches: (1) Legacy multi-value dimensions — GROUP BY implicitly explodes, = matches if value is in the set. Counts are inflated. (2) Modern ARRAY columns (Druid 28+) — explicit ARRAY_CONTAINS() for filtering, CROSS JOIN UNNEST() for explosion. ARRAY gives clearer semantics and accurate counts. For new deployments, ARRAY is recommended. Both break rollup since each unique array/multi-value combination is a separate group.

21.6 Dimension Cardinality Impact Analysis

Understanding how dimension cardinality impacts segment size and query performance is one of the most important data modeling skills for Druid:

Figure 21.2 — Dimension cardinality has exponential impact on segment size: <10K is ideal; >1M requires bitmap suppression; >10M should use sketches instead
CardinalityBitmap Memory (5M rows)Rollup ImpactRecommendation
<100 (country, platform)~50 KBExcellent — very few rollup groups✅ Ideal. Bitmap indexed. Rollup-friendly.
100–10K (city, product_category)~500 KB – 5 MBGood — manageable rollup groups✅ Standard. Bitmap works well.
10K–100K (product_id, campaign_id)~5 – 50 MBModerate — consider if needed in WHERE⚠️ OK if filtered. Monitor segment size.
100K–1M (IP address, zip code)~50 – 500 MBPoor — rollup ratio suffers significantly⚠️ Disable bitmap if not in WHERE. Consider reducing cardinality.
1M–10M (device_id, email hash)~500 MB – 5 GBVery poor — rollup nearly 1:1❌ Bitmap OFF. Use only if GROUP BY is required. Prefer sketch.
>10M (user_id, session_id, UUID)>5 GB (exceeds data itself)None — every value unique❌ Anti-pattern. Use HLL/Theta sketch metric. Never as dimension.
⚙️ Engineering Insight The most common data modeling mistake in Druid: including user_id as a STRING dimension "because we might need to GROUP BY it someday." With 100M users, this creates 100M bitmaps per segment (~5GB), destroys rollup (ratio drops to 1:1 since every user is unique), and inflates segment sizes by 10×. Instead: (1) Ingest user_id as an HLLSketchBuild metric for distinct counting. (2) If you truly need user-level drill-down, store user_id as a dimension with "createBitmapIndex": false and accept the rollup penalty — but question whether this use case belongs in Druid (maybe Redis or Elasticsearch is better for user-level lookups).

21.7 Practical Data Modeling Example

Putting it all together — a real-world e-commerce event datasource combining all column types:

Complete Data Model — E-Commerce EventsJSON
{
  "dataSchema": {
    "dataSource": "ecommerce_events",
    "timestampSpec": { "column": "event_time", "format": "iso" },
    "dimensionsSpec": {
      "dimensions": [
        // Low cardinality — full bitmap, rollup-friendly
        "country",              // ~200 unique
        "platform",             // ~3 unique (mobile/desktop/tablet)
        "event_type",           // ~10 unique (view/cart/purchase/...)
        "product_category",     // ~500 unique

        // Medium cardinality — bitmap useful for filtering
        "product_id",           // ~50K unique — OK for top-N queries

        // Variable structure — nested JSON for flexibility
        {
          "type": "json",
          "name": "event_properties"  // Variable per event_type
        },                          // $.cart_total, $.search_query, $.video_duration

        // Array column — tags per event
        {
          "type": "ARRAY<STRING>",
          "name": "tags"              // ["premium", "returning", "mobile-app"]
        }
      ]
    },
    "metricsSpec": [
      { "type": "count", "name": "event_count" },
      { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "revenue_sum", "fieldName": "revenue" },
      { "type": "doubleMin", "name": "min_price", "fieldName": "price" },
      { "type": "doubleMax", "name": "max_price", "fieldName": "price" },

      // Sketches — approximate analytics on high-cardinality fields
      { "type": "HLLSketchBuild", "name": "unique_users",
        "fieldName": "user_id", "lgK": 12 },
      { "type": "thetaSketch", "name": "user_sketch",
        "fieldName": "user_id", "size": 4096 },
      { "type": "quantilesDoublesSketch", "name": "latency_sketch",
        "fieldName": "page_load_ms", "k": 128 }
    ],
    "granularitySpec": {
      "segmentGranularity": "HOUR",
      "queryGranularity": "MINUTE",
      "rollup": true
    }
  }
}
// Expected rollup ratio: ~30× (product_id is medium cardinality)
// user_id is NOT a dimension — it's an HLL + Theta sketch metric
// event_properties handles variable fields per event_type
// tags uses native ARRAY for proper multi-value semantics

Section 21 — Key Takeaways

FeatureWhen to UseKey Trade-off
Nested JSONVariable/sparse properties per event typeSchema-on-read flexibility vs larger segments, no rollup participation
Complex (sketches)Approximate analytics on high-cardinality fieldsCannot be used in WHERE/GROUP BY — aggregation only
ARRAY columnsMulti-value data with proper array semanticsClearer than multi-value dims; use UNNEST for explosion
Multi-value dimsLegacy compatibility onlyImplicit GROUP BY explosion, inflated counts — avoid for new data
Cardinality zones<10K ideal; >1M disable bitmap; >10M use sketchesEach unique value = one bitmap. High cardinality = huge segment overhead.
Part 9 — Section 22

Multi-Tenancy Design Patterns

Three isolation models for serving multiple customers from one Druid cluster — from shared datasource to fully dedicated infrastructure.

22 — Multi-Tenancy Design Patterns

22.1 The Multi-Tenancy Challenge

When a Druid cluster serves multiple tenants (customers, business units, or teams), three competing concerns emerge:

  • Isolation: Tenant A's heavy query should never degrade Tenant B's dashboard experience
  • Security: Tenant A must never access Tenant B's data — even via crafted queries
  • Efficiency: Dedicated infrastructure per tenant is expensive — sharing infrastructure reduces cost per tenant dramatically

Druid doesn't have built-in multi-tenancy primitives (no row-level security, no tenant-aware scheduler). Multi-tenancy must be architected using a combination of datasource design, query proxies, RBAC, and resource isolation. The right pattern depends on your tenant count, data volume, isolation requirements, and compliance obligations.

22.2 The Three Isolation Models

Figure 22.1 — Three multi-tenancy approaches: trade isolation for efficiency as you move from right (separate clusters) to left (shared datasource)

22.3 Pattern 1 — Shared Cluster, Separate Datasources

Each tenant has its own datasources (tenant_a_events, tenant_b_events). Access is controlled via Druid's native RBAC — each tenant's credentials only grant READ on their own datasources.

Architecture:

  • One Druid cluster shared by all tenants
  • Each tenant gets N datasources (e.g., tenant_a_events, tenant_a_metrics)
  • Druid RBAC restricts each tenant's user to their own datasource names
  • Each datasource can have its own schema, compaction schedule, and retention rules
RBAC Role for Tenant AJSON
{
  "name": "tenant_a_reader",
  "permissions": [
    {
      "resourceAction": {
        "resource": {
          "name": "tenant_a_.*",     // Regex match: all tenant_a datasources
          "type": "DATASOURCE"
        },
        "action": "READ"
      }
    }
  ]
}
// Tenant A can only query datasources matching "tenant_a_*"
// Attempting to query "tenant_b_events" returns 403 Forbidden
AspectSeparate Datasources
Isolation✅ Strong — separate segments, separate bitmap indexes, no data mixing
Schema flexibility✅ Each tenant can have different dimensions, metrics, granularity
Compaction✅ Independent compaction schedules per tenant
Retention✅ Independent retention rules per tenant
Noisy-neighbor⚠️ Shared Historicals — a heavy tenant's query can slow Historicals for others
Scaling limit❌ 100+ tenants × 5 datasources each = 500+ datasources → Coordinator metadata overhead grows
Cost⚠️ Medium — shared infra but per-datasource overhead
⚙️ Engineering Insight The practical limit for this pattern is ~50 tenants before metadata overhead becomes significant. Each datasource adds segments to the Coordinator's inventory, compaction evaluation loop, and Broker's segment timeline. At 500+ datasources, Coordinator cycle time can grow from 10 seconds to minutes, delaying segment assignments and compaction. Monitor coordinator/time metric — if it exceeds 60 seconds, you've outgrown this pattern.

22.4 Pattern 2 — Shared Datasource with Tenant Dimension

All tenants' data lives in a single datasource with a tenant_id dimension. A query proxy sits in front of Druid and injects WHERE tenant_id = 'X' into every query based on the authenticated user's identity.

Figure 22.2 — Query proxy extracts tenant_id from JWT and injects WHERE clause — Druid sees pre-filtered queries
Nginx/OpenResty Proxy — Inject Tenant FilterLua
-- OpenResty Lua filter: inject tenant_id into Druid SQL
local jwt = require "resty.jwt"
local cjson = require "cjson"

-- 1. Validate JWT and extract tenant_id
local auth_header = ngx.var.http_Authorization
local jwt_obj = jwt:verify("secret", auth_header:sub(8))
local tenant_id = jwt_obj.payload.tenant_id

-- 2. Read original SQL body
ngx.req.read_body()
local body = cjson.decode(ngx.req.get_body_data())
local original_sql = body.query

-- 3. Inject tenant filter
-- Simple approach: wrap in subquery with filter
body.query = "SELECT * FROM (" .. original_sql ..
             ") WHERE tenant_id = '" .. tenant_id .. "'"

-- Production: use SQL parser to inject into WHERE clause properly
-- Prevents SQL injection and handles complex queries correctly

ngx.req.set_body_data(cjson.encode(body))
ngx.req.set_header("Content-Length", #cjson.encode(body))
⚠️ Common Pitfall Using simple string concatenation to inject tenant filters (as shown above for illustration). A malicious SQL string can bypass the filter: SELECT * FROM events WHERE 1=1) UNION SELECT * FROM events WHERE tenant_id='other_tenant' --. Production implementations must use a proper SQL parser (e.g., Apache Calcite, sqlparse) to inject the filter into the AST, not string manipulation. Alternatively, use Druid's native RBAC with separate datasources per tenant where security is critical.

22.5 Pattern 3 — Separate Clusters

Each tenant gets a fully dedicated Druid cluster — separate Brokers, Historicals, MiddleManagers, ZooKeeper, metadata store, and deep storage. Maximum isolation at maximum cost.

  • When to use: regulatory requirements (HIPAA, SOC 2), very large tenants (>10TB each), contractual SLA guarantees, customers who demand dedicated infrastructure
  • Implementation: Kubernetes namespaces per tenant with dedicated Helm releases, or separate EC2 auto-scaling groups per tenant
  • Cost: 3–5× more expensive than shared cluster at small scale; cost-effective at very large per-tenant data volumes
  • Ops overhead: Druid upgrade = N cluster upgrades. Each cluster needs its own monitoring, alerting, and on-call response.

22.6 Resource Isolation with Query Lanes

Even within shared clusters (Patterns 1 and 2), query lanes prevent one tenant from monopolizing Historical processing threads:

Tenant-Based Query Lane Configurationproperties
# Define lanes with resource allocation percentages
druid.query.scheduler.laning.type=manual
druid.query.scheduler.laning.lanes={
    "tenant_premium": 0.5,    // 50% of processing threads
    "tenant_standard": 0.3,   // 30% of processing threads
    "tenant_free": 0.2        // 20% of processing threads
}

// Usage: proxy injects lane into query context based on tenant tier
// Premium tenant: {"context": {"lane": "tenant_premium", "timeout": 10000}}
// Free tenant:    {"context": {"lane": "tenant_free", "timeout": 30000}}

// Effect: even if free-tier tenant runs a heavy query consuming all 20% of
// their lane's threads, premium tenants still have 50% capacity guaranteed.

22.7 Noisy-Neighbor Detection and Mitigation

In shared environments, identifying and mitigating noisy tenants is an ongoing operational challenge:

SignalHow to DetectMitigation
Heavy query volumePer-tenant query count from Broker request log (filter by tenant_id in SQL)Rate limiting at proxy layer: max N queries/sec per tenant
Expensive queriesPer-tenant P99 query/time and query/bytes from request logPer-tenant timeout (context.timeout); reject queries scanning >X segments
Unbounded GROUP BYQuery returning >100K groups without LIMITInject LIMIT 10000 at proxy for non-admin tenants; use maxScatterGatherBytes
Missing time filterQuery with no __time predicateProxy rejects queries without time filter; or auto-inject last 7 days default
Lane saturationquery/wait/time metric increasing for a specific laneDowngrade tenant to lower-priority lane; alert ops team
Proxy Rate Limiting — Per-TenantYAML
# Envoy rate limit configuration
rate_limits:
  - actions:
      - request_headers:
          header_name: "x-tenant-id"
          descriptor_key: "tenant_id"

# Rate limit service config
descriptors:
  - key: tenant_id
    value: acme              # Premium tenant
    rate_limit:
      requests_per_unit: 100
      unit: second
  - key: tenant_id
    value: initech            # Free tenant
    rate_limit:
      requests_per_unit: 10
      unit: second

22.8 Choosing the Right Pattern

FactorPattern 1 (Separate DS)Pattern 2 (Shared DS)Pattern 3 (Separate Clusters)
Tenant count5–5050–10,000+1–20 (large)
Data per tenantGB–TB eachMB–GB each (long tail)TB+ each
Schema varianceHigh (different dims per tenant)Low (same schema + tenant_id)Any
Security requirementMedium (RBAC sufficient)Medium (proxy must be robust)High (compliance, regulatory)
Noisy-neighbor riskMedium (shared Historicals)High (shared everything)None
Cost per tenant$$$$$$
Ops complexityMediumLow (one cluster, one schema)Very high (N clusters to manage)
Best exampleInternal teams with different data needsSaaS product with many small customersEnterprise customer with SLA contract
🎯 Interview Tip "Design a multi-tenant Druid architecture for a SaaS analytics platform with 500 customers." — Use Pattern 2 (shared datasource with tenant_id dimension). Deploy an Envoy proxy that validates JWT → extracts tenant_id → injects WHERE tenant_id = 'X' via SQL AST rewriting. Use CLUSTERED BY tenant_id at compaction to enable partition pruning. Implement query lanes: premium tenants get 60% of threads, standard 30%, free 10%. Add per-tenant rate limiting (100 QPS premium, 10 QPS free). Monitor per-tenant query/time P99 and alert on noisy-neighbor spikes. For the 5 largest enterprise customers who need compliance, run Pattern 3 (dedicated clusters via separate K8s namespaces).

22.9 Multi-Tenancy with Partition Pruning

For Pattern 2 (shared datasource), the tenant_id dimension should be the first CLUSTERED BY dimension during compaction. This enables partition pruning — when the proxy injects WHERE tenant_id='acme', Druid skips all shards that don't contain acme data.

Compaction with Tenant-Aware PartitioningJSON
{
  "dataSource": "all_events",
  "tuningConfig": {
    "partitionsSpec": {
      "type": "hashed",
      "numShards": 8,
      "partitionDimensions": ["tenant_id"]
    }
  }
}
// With 500 tenants and 8 shards:
// Each shard holds ~62 tenants (hashed)
// WHERE tenant_id='acme' → only 1 of 8 shards scanned → 87.5% pruned
//
// For large tenants dominating volume, consider range partitioning:
// Large tenants get dedicated shards; small tenants share remaining shards
⚙️ Engineering Insight The combination of tenant_id partition pruning + bitmap filtering makes shared-datasource multi-tenancy surprisingly efficient. After hash-partition pruning skips 7/8 shards, the bitmap filter on the remaining shard further narrows to only rows for that specific tenant. For a 500-tenant datasource, each tenant query effectively sees ~0.2% of total data — query latency is comparable to a single-tenant dedicated datasource.

Section 22 — Key Takeaways

PatternBest ForKey ImplementationWatch Out For
Separate DS5–50 tenants, different schemasRBAC per datasourceMetadata overhead beyond 50 tenants
Shared DS + proxy50–10K tenants (SaaS)JWT proxy + tenant_id filter injection + CLUSTERED BY tenant_idProxy security, noisy-neighbor
Separate clustersRegulated, large tenantsK8s namespaces, dedicated infraOps overhead, cost
Query lanesAll shared patternsPer-tier thread allocationTuning lane percentages
Rate limitingAll shared patternsEnvoy/proxy per-tenant QPS limitsSetting appropriate limits per tier
Part 9 — Section 23

Druid on Kubernetes

Mapping Druid's distributed architecture onto K8s primitives — StatefulSets, PVCs, HPA, ZK-free mode, and production-grade Helm configuration.

23 — Druid on Kubernetes

23.1 Component-to-K8s Resource Mapping

Each Druid component has different statefulness and scaling characteristics, which map to different Kubernetes resource types. Getting this mapping right is the foundation of a stable Druid-on-K8s deployment:

Druid ComponentK8s ResourceStateful?Rationale
HistoricalStatefulSetYes — local segment cacheStable pod identity + PVC for segment cache (survives restarts). Losing cache = re-download GBs from S3.
MiddleManagerStatefulSetYes — intermediate dataPersistent local storage for intermediate persists during ingestion. Pod restart mid-task = data loss.
BrokerDeploymentNoStateless — any pod handles any query. Horizontal scaling behind Service.
RouterDeploymentNoStateless gateway. Deploy 2+ pods behind an Ingress for HA.
CoordinatorDeployment (1 active)No (reads metadata DB)Leader election via ZK or K8s. Only 1 active; standby ready for failover.
OverlordDeployment (1 active)No (reads metadata DB)Same as Coordinator. Co-locate with Coordinator in one Deployment for simplicity.
ZooKeeperStatefulSet (3+)Yes — consensus stateStable identity + persistent storage. Or eliminate ZK entirely with K8s-native discovery.
Metadata StoreExternal (RDS)Yes — criticalNever run on K8s. Use managed RDS/Cloud SQL with multi-AZ + automated backups.
Figure 23.1 — Druid components mapped to K8s resources across 4 namespaces, with external managed dependencies

23.2 Helm Chart Configuration — Production Grade

Historical StatefulSet — Helm values.yamlYAML
historical:
  replicaCount: 10
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "8", memory: "64Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "16", memory: "64Gi" }  # requests = limits prevents OOM kill priority issues
  persistence:
    enabled: true
    size: 500Gi                # Segment cache — 2× expected hot data per pod
    storageClass: gp3-ssd     # High IOPS for mmap — gp3 at 3000 IOPS baseline
  javaOpts: "-Xms24g -Xmx24g -XX:+UseZGC -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g"
  # Memory budget (64Gi total):
  #   24Gi heap → JVM objects, GroupBy hash tables, cache
  #   12Gi direct → processing buffers (numThreads × bufferSize)
  #   28Gi → OS page cache for mmap'd segments (most critical!)
  nodeSelector:
    druid-role: historical     # Dedicated node pool with SSD instance types
  tolerations:
    - key: "druid-historical"
      effect: "NoSchedule"    # Only Historical pods on these nodes
  podAntiAffinity:
    preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        topologyKey: "kubernetes.io/hostname"  # Spread across nodes
Broker Deployment — Helm values.yamlYAML
broker:
  replicaCount: 3
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "4", memory: "16Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "8", memory: "16Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms8g -Xmx8g -XX:+UseZGC -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=4g"
  # Brokers are stateless — Deployment, not StatefulSet
  # No PVC needed — cache is in-memory (Caffeine)
  service:
    type: ClusterIP
    port: 8082
  ingress:
    enabled: true
    className: alb
    annotations:
      alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme: internal
      alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/target-type: ip
Master (Coordinator + Overlord) — Helm values.yamlYAML
coordinator:
  replicaCount: 2            # Active/standby via ZK leader election
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "2", memory: "8Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "4", memory: "8Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms4g -Xmx4g -XX:+UseG1GC"
  extraConfig:
    druid.coordinator.asOverlord.enabled: "true"  # Co-locate in single pod
    druid.coordinator.period: "PT10S"             # Faster segment assignment

23.3 ZooKeeper Replacement — K8s-Native Discovery

Druid 0.22+ supports Kubernetes-native service discovery as a complete ZooKeeper replacement. This eliminates ZK as a dependency — one fewer StatefulSet to manage, no ZK-related outages, simpler operations:

ZK-Free Mode — Kubernetes Discovery Configurationproperties
# ═══ Disable ZooKeeper entirely ═══
druid.zk.service.enabled=false

# ═══ Use K8s API for service discovery ═══
druid.discovery.type=k8s
druid.discovery.k8s.clusterIdentifier=druid-prod
druid.discovery.k8s.podSpec.annotations.druidDiscovery=true

# ═══ HTTP-based segment management (replaces ZK load queue) ═══
druid.serverview.type=http
druid.coordinator.loadqueuepeon.type=http

# ═══ Leader election via K8s lease API ═══
# Coordinator and Overlord use K8s Lease objects instead of ZK
# Requires: ServiceAccount with Lease create/get/update permissions

# Benefits:
# • Eliminate ZK StatefulSet (3 pods + PVCs)
# • No ZK session timeout issues
# • Faster segment discovery (HTTP polling vs ZK watches)
# • Simpler operations and monitoring
RBAC for K8s Discovery — ServiceAccount PermissionsYAML
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: druid-discovery
  namespace: druid
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: ["coordination.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["leases"]            # Leader election via K8s Lease API
    verbs: ["get", "create", "update", "delete"]
⚙️ Engineering Insight ZK-free mode is production-ready in Druid 28+ and is the recommended configuration for new K8s deployments. It eliminates the single most common source of Druid operational incidents (ZK session expirations, ZK leader loss, ZK disk full). If you're running Druid on K8s with ZK, migrating to K8s-native discovery should be a high-priority project.

23.4 HPA — Autoscaling Strategies

Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA) must be configured carefully per component. The rules are different for stateless vs stateful services:

ComponentHPA StrategyMetricTargetCaveats
Broker✅ AutoscaleCPU utilization or druid_query_count (custom)CPU: 60% averageSafe — stateless, fast to start
Router✅ AutoscaleCPU utilizationCPU: 60%Safe — stateless proxy, minimal startup cost
MiddleManager⚠️ CarefulTask queue depth (custom metric via Prometheus adapter)Queue > N for 5 minNew MM takes ~30s to register; tasks may queue briefly
Historical❌ Do NOT autoN/AManual scalingEach new pod downloads GBs from S3 — thundering herd risk
Coordinator❌ FixedN/AAlways 2 podsLeader election — only 1 active, 1 standby
Broker HPA ConfigurationYAML
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: druid-broker-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: druid-broker
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 60
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 120   # Wait 2 min before scaling up
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 2                          # Add max 2 pods at a time
          periodSeconds: 120
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 600   # Wait 10 min before scaling down
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 1                          # Remove max 1 pod at a time
          periodSeconds: 300
⚠️ Common Pitfall Autoscaling Historicals: Each new Historical pod must download its assigned segments from S3 before serving queries. If HPA adds 5 Historical pods simultaneously, all 5 download GBs of segments from S3 at the same time — a thundering herd that saturates S3 bandwidth and slows the existing Historicals' queries (they share the S3 connection pool). Fix: never autoscale Historicals. Scale them manually with controlled rollouts: add 1–2 pods at a time, wait for segment loading to complete, then add more.

23.5 Persistent Volume Design

ComponentPV PurposeStorage ClassSize GuidanceIOPS Requirement
Historical (hot)Segment cachegp3-ssd / pd-ssd2× expected hot data per podHigh (3000+ baseline) — mmap reads
Historical (warm)Segment cachegp3 / pd-standard2× warm data per podStandard sufficient
MiddleManagerIntermediate persistsgp3-ssd100–200Gi (2× max concurrent segment builds)High during ingestion spikes
ZooKeeperTxn log + snapshotgp3-ssd20Gi (ZK data is tiny)Low — but latency-sensitive
⚙️ Engineering Insight PV sizing for Historicals: Size the PVC to 2× expected hot data per pod. If your hot tier holds 5TB total across 10 Historicals with replication=2, each Historical serves ~1TB of data. Size PVC to 2TB to handle rebalancing during rolling updates (temporarily holds segments for departing + arriving pods). On AWS, use gp3 with explicit IOPS provisioning (3000 baseline, up to 16000 provisioned) — mmap performance directly correlates with disk IOPS.

23.6 Rolling Updates and Upgrade Strategy

Upgrading Druid on K8s requires a specific order to avoid query failures and data loss. The general principle: data-serving components last, coordination components first.

StepComponentStrategyValidation Before Next Step
1Coordinator + OverlordRolling update, 1 at a timeLeader re-elected, segment assignments resuming
2MiddleManagersRolling update, 1 at a timeRunning tasks complete; new tasks start on updated MMs
3HistoricalsRolling update, 1 at a time, maxUnavailable=1Segments rebalanced; query latency stable
4BrokersRolling updateQueries returning successfully on new version
5RouterRolling updateAPI gateway healthy; console accessible
Historical Rolling Update — Controlled RolloutYAML
historical:
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1           # Only 1 Historical pod down at a time
      # StatefulSets update in reverse order: hist-9, hist-8, ..., hist-0
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 120  # Allow in-flight queries to complete
  lifecycle:
    preStop:
      exec:
        command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 30"]  # Drain in-flight queries
  readinessProbe:
    httpGet:
      path: /status/health
      port: 8083
    initialDelaySeconds: 60       # Wait for segment loading after restart
    periodSeconds: 10
⚠️ Common Pitfall Setting terminationGracePeriodSeconds too low on Historicals. When a Historical pod is terminated, it must: (1) finish in-flight queries (~5s), (2) announce departure to ZK/K8s (~5s), (3) allow Coordinator to reassign its segments to other Historicals. With the default 30s, step 3 often doesn't complete — segments are temporarily unavailable until Coordinator reassigns. Use 120s+ grace period and a preStop sleep to ensure clean handoff.

23.7 Monitoring Druid on K8s

Prometheus Emitter + ServiceMonitorYAML
# Druid config: expose Prometheus metrics
druid.emitter=prometheus
druid.emitter.prometheus.port=9090
druid.emitter.prometheus.namespace=druid

# ---
# K8s ServiceMonitor for Prometheus Operator
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: druid-historical
  labels:
    release: prometheus
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: druid-historical
  endpoints:
    - port: metrics
      interval: 15s
      path: /metrics

23.8 Spot/Preemptible Instance Strategy

ComponentSpot/Preemptible?RationaleRisk Mitigation
Broker✅ ExcellentStateless — instant replacement from poolRun min 2 on-demand, rest on Spot
Router✅ ExcellentStateless proxy — fast restartRun 1 on-demand minimum
Historical (warm/cold)✅ Good (with replication=2)If one Spot pod dies, replica on other pod servesEnsure replication ≥2; PVC survives pod eviction
Historical (hot)⚠️ RiskySegment re-download on eviction = latency spikeAvoid — hot tier latency too critical for Spot disruption
MiddleManager❌ Avoid for streamingKafka offset rollback on eviction = re-ingestionOK for batch-only MMs if fault tolerance is enabled
Coordinator/Overlord❌ AvoidLeader election disruption = coordination gapRun on on-demand — only 2 small pods
🎯 Interview Tip "Design a production Druid deployment on EKS for a 10TB dataset with 200 concurrent users." — K8s cluster: 3 namespaces (query, data, master). Brokers: Deployment ×3 with HPA (CPU 60%, max 8), Spot instances, ALB Ingress. Historicals: StatefulSet ×20 (hot tier, gp3-ssd 500Gi PVC each), on-demand instances, no autoscaling, maxUnavailable=1 rolling update. MiddleManagers: StatefulSet ×8, on-demand, gp3-ssd 200Gi PVC. Master: Deployment ×2 (Coordinator+Overlord co-located), on-demand. ZK-free: druid.discovery.type=k8s. External: RDS MySQL Multi-AZ, S3 with lifecycle rules. Monitoring: Prometheus emitter + ServiceMonitor + Grafana dashboards for query/time, segment/loadQueue, ingest/kafka/lag.

Section 23 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
K8s mappingStateless → Deployment; Stateful → StatefulSetHistorical + MM = StatefulSet with PVC; Broker = Deployment
ZK-freeK8s-native discovery eliminates ZKUse for all new deployments (Druid 28+). Migrate existing.
HPAAutoscale Brokers; never autoscale HistoricalsHistorical thundering herd on scale-up = S3 saturation
PVCsSegment cache survives restartsSize to 2× hot data per pod. gp3-ssd for Historicals.
Rolling updatesMaster first, Historicals last, 1 at a time120s+ grace period. preStop sleep. readiness probe with delay.
Spot instancesBrokers + warm Historicals = safe. Hot Historicals + MMs = risky.30–50% cost savings on Brokers via Spot
Part 9 — Section 24

Security and Authentication

Defense-in-depth for Druid — authentication chains, RBAC authorization, TLS encryption, and row-level security patterns.

24 — Security and Authentication

24.1 Security Architecture Overview

Druid's security model is layered: each layer independently protects against different threat vectors. In production, all layers should be active simultaneously — defense-in-depth:

  • Layer 1 — Network: TLS encryption on all endpoints; firewall/security groups restricting port access
  • Layer 2 — Authentication: verify identity — who is making this request? (Basic Auth, LDAP, OAuth/OIDC, Kerberos)
  • Layer 3 — Authorization: verify permission — is this identity allowed to perform this action? (RBAC with datasource-level grants)
  • Layer 4 — Encryption at rest: S3 SSE-KMS, EBS encryption, HDFS TDE
  • Layer 5 — Audit: who queried what, when, and how long did it take? (request logging)
Figure 24.1 — Five security layers: TLS → Authentication → Authorization → Encryption at Rest → Audit Logging

24.2 Authentication Mechanisms

Druid supports multiple authentication methods via an authenticator chain. Each request is tested against authenticators in order until one succeeds:

MethodUse CaseComplexityConfiguration
Basic AuthSimple deployments, internal toolsLowUsername/password stored in metadata store (bcrypt hashed)
LDAPCorporate directory integration (Active Directory)MediumConnects to LDAP server; maps LDAP groups to Druid roles
OAuth 2.0 / OIDCModern SSO (Okta, Auth0, Azure AD, Google)MediumValidates JWT tokens; extracts user identity from claims
KerberosHadoop/enterprise environments with existing KDCHighSPNEGO negotiation; keytab-based service auth
mTLS (client certificates)Service-to-service authenticationMediumClient presents X.509 certificate; CN/SAN mapped to identity
Basic Auth — Quick Start Configurationproperties
# ═══ Enable Basic Auth ═══
druid.auth.authenticatorChain=["basic"]
druid.auth.authenticator.basic.type=basic
druid.auth.authenticator.basic.initialAdminPassword=changeme123!
druid.auth.authenticator.basic.initialInternalClientPassword=internal_secret
druid.auth.authenticator.basic.credentialsValidator.type=metadata
druid.auth.authenticator.basic.skipOnFailure=false

# ═══ Enable Basic Auth Authorizer ═══
druid.auth.authorizers=["basic"]
druid.auth.authorizer.basic.type=basic

# ═══ Escalator for internal service communication ═══
druid.escalator.type=basic
druid.escalator.internalClientUsername=druid_internal
druid.escalator.internalClientPassword=internal_secret
LDAP Authentication — Active Directory Integrationproperties
# ═══ LDAP Authenticator ═══
druid.auth.authenticatorChain=["ldap"]
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.type=basic
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.enableCacheNotifications=true
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.type=ldap

# LDAP server connection
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.url=ldaps://ldap.corp.example.com:636
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.bindUser=cn=druid-svc,ou=services,dc=corp
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.bindPassword=ldap_bind_password
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.baseDn=ou=users,dc=corp,dc=example,dc=com
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.userSearch=(sAMAccountName=%s)

# Map LDAP groups to Druid roles
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.groupBaseDn=ou=groups,dc=corp
druid.auth.authenticator.ldap.credentialsValidator.groupSearch=(member=%s)
⚙️ Engineering Insight For production with multiple auth methods (e.g., LDAP for humans, mTLS for services), use an authenticator chain: druid.auth.authenticatorChain=["ldap","mtls","basic"]. Druid tests each authenticator in order — the first successful match wins. Put the most common method first to minimize unnecessary LDAP lookups for service accounts that use mTLS.

24.3 Authorization — RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)

After authentication establishes who the user is, authorization determines what they can do. Druid uses a role-based model with three resource types:

  • DATASOURCE: controls which datasources a user can query (READ) or ingest into (WRITE)
  • CONFIG: controls access to system configuration (Coordinator rules, compaction settings)
  • STATE: controls access to cluster state (task status, segment inventory)
RBAC — Create Role and Assign Permissionsbash
# 1. Create a role for the analytics team
curl -u admin:password -X POST \
  http://coordinator:8081/druid-ext/basic-security/authorization/db/basic/roles/analytics_reader

# 2. Grant READ on specific datasources
curl -u admin:password -X POST \
  http://coordinator:8081/druid-ext/basic-security/authorization/db/basic/roles/analytics_reader/permissions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '[
    {
      "resourceAction": {
        "resource": { "name": "user_events", "type": "DATASOURCE" },
        "action": "READ"
      }
    },
    {
      "resourceAction": {
        "resource": { "name": "page_performance", "type": "DATASOURCE" },
        "action": "READ"
      }
    },
    {
      "resourceAction": {
        "resource": { "name": "STATE", "type": "STATE" },
        "action": "READ"
      }
    }
  ]'

# 3. Assign role to user
curl -u admin:password -X POST \
  http://coordinator:8081/druid-ext/basic-security/authorization/db/basic/users/alice/roles/analytics_reader

# Result: alice can query user_events and page_performance
# alice CANNOT query other datasources, submit tasks, or modify config

Common RBAC Role Templates

RoleDatasource AccessConfig AccessState AccessUse Case
adminREAD + WRITE on all (.*)READ + WRITEREAD + WRITEPlatform team — full cluster access
data_engineerREAD + WRITE on team datasourcesREADREAD + WRITECan ingest, query, monitor tasks
analyst_readerREAD on specific datasourcesNoneREADQuery only — no ingestion, no config
dashboard_serviceREAD on dashboard datasourcesNoneNoneService account for BI tool (Superset, Grafana)
tenant_XREAD on tenant_X_.*NoneNoneMulti-tenant isolation (Pattern 1)
⚠️ Common Pitfall Druid RBAC controls datasource-level access, not row-level. A user with READ on user_events can query ALL rows in that datasource — there's no built-in way to restrict "alice can only see country='US' data." For row-level security, you must implement it at the proxy layer (inject WHERE clause per user identity) as described in Section 22 (Multi-Tenancy).

24.4 TLS — Encryption in Transit

TLS encrypts all data in transit between clients and Druid, and between Druid services themselves. In production, both external and internal TLS should be enabled:

TLS Configuration — All Endpointsproperties
# ═══ Enable TLS on all Druid services ═══
druid.enableTlsPort=true
druid.server.https.keyStoreType=PKCS12
druid.server.https.keyStorePath=/etc/druid/tls/keystore.p12
druid.server.https.keyStorePassword=${TLS_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD}
druid.server.https.certAlias=druid

# ═══ Trust store for verifying other Druid services ═══
druid.server.https.trustStoreType=PKCS12
druid.server.https.trustStorePath=/etc/druid/tls/truststore.p12
druid.server.https.trustStorePassword=${TLS_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD}

# ═══ Disable plaintext HTTP (enforce HTTPS only) ═══
druid.enablePlaintextPort=false

# ═══ Internal mTLS (service-to-service) ═══
# Each Druid service presents a client cert when connecting to others
druid.client.https.protocol=TLSv1.2
druid.client.https.trustStoreType=PKCS12
druid.client.https.trustStorePath=/etc/druid/tls/truststore.p12
druid.client.https.keyStoreType=PKCS12
druid.client.https.keyStorePath=/etc/druid/tls/keystore.p12

Certificate management in Kubernetes:

  • Use cert-manager with a ClusterIssuer to automatically provision and rotate TLS certificates for all Druid pods
  • Mount certificates as K8s Secrets into the pod's /etc/druid/tls/ directory
  • Rotate certificates every 90 days — cert-manager handles this automatically
  • For internal mTLS: use a private CA (cert-manager self-signed or Vault PKI) — no need for public CAs for service-to-service
cert-manager Certificate for DruidYAML
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Certificate
metadata:
  name: druid-broker-tls
  namespace: druid
spec:
  secretName: druid-broker-tls-secret
  issuerRef:
    name: druid-ca-issuer           # Private CA for internal mTLS
    kind: ClusterIssuer
  commonName: druid-broker
  dnsNames:
    - druid-broker
    - druid-broker.druid.svc.cluster.local
    - "*.druid-broker.druid.svc.cluster.local"
  duration: 2160h                  # 90 days
  renewBefore: 720h                # Renew 30 days before expiry
  keystores:
    pkcs12:
      create: true                  # Druid needs PKCS12 format
      passwordSecretRef:
        name: druid-tls-password
        key: password

24.5 Encryption at Rest

Data LocationEncryption MethodConfiguration
Deep Storage (S3)SSE-KMS (AES-256)druid.storage.sse.type=kms + druid.storage.sse.kmsKeyId=arn:aws:kms:...
Deep Storage (GCS)CMEKConfigure CMEK on the GCS bucket; Druid uses it automatically
Historical local cache (EBS)EBS volume encryptionEnable encryption on the gp3 StorageClass in K8s; or EBS default encryption per region
MiddleManager local (EBS)EBS volume encryptionSame as Historical — encrypted PVCs
Metadata Store (RDS)RDS encryption at restEnable at RDS instance creation (cannot be added later)
HDFS (on-prem)TDE (Transparent Data Encryption)HDFS Encryption Zones; KMS for key management
⚙️ Engineering Insight S3 SSE-KMS encryption has zero performance impact on Druid — encryption/decryption happens transparently at the S3 layer. There is no reason NOT to enable it. The only cost is KMS API calls (~$0.03 per 10,000 requests), which is negligible for Druid's segment upload/download pattern. Enable SSE-KMS on all Druid S3 buckets.

24.6 Row-Level Security Patterns

Druid has no native row-level security (RLS). All users with READ access to a datasource can query all rows. To implement RLS, use one of these proxy patterns:

PatternHow It WorksSecurity LevelComplexity
Query proxy injectionProxy extracts user identity → injects WHERE tenant_id = 'X' into every query ASTMedium — depends on proxy correctnessMedium — requires SQL parser
Separate datasources per tenantEach tenant gets own datasources; RBAC restricts accessHigh — no shared dataLow — native Druid RBAC
BI tool-level filteringSuperset/Grafana enforces row filters before query reaches DruidLow — bypassed by direct API accessLow — BI config only
Column masking via viewsCreate Druid "virtual datasources" (views) that exclude sensitive columnsMedium — column-level only, not row-levelLow — Druid catalog API (Druid 28+)
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you implement row-level security in Druid?" — Druid doesn't support native RLS. Two approaches: (1) Proxy-based: Deploy an Envoy/custom proxy that validates JWT, extracts user identity, and injects WHERE user_org = 'X' into the SQL AST before forwarding to Druid. Must use a proper SQL parser (not string concat) to prevent injection. (2) Separate datasources: Each security domain gets its own datasource with RBAC restricting access. Simpler and more secure, but doesn't scale beyond ~50 domains. For a SaaS product, combine: proxy injection for tenant isolation + RBAC for datasource-level access.

24.7 Audit Logging

Druid logs every query with full details — essential for compliance (SOC 2, HIPAA), debugging, and capacity planning:

Audit Logging Configurationproperties
# ═══ Enable request logging ═══
druid.request.logging.type=file
druid.request.logging.dir=/var/log/druid/requests
druid.request.logging.filePattern=yyyy-MM-dd'.log'

# Each log entry contains:
# • queryId — unique per query (for end-to-end tracing)
# • sqlQuery — the full SQL text
# • identity — authenticated user name
# • query/time — total execution time (ms)
# • query/bytes — total bytes scanned
# • query/segment/count — segments touched
# • success — true/false
# • remoteAddress — client IP

# ═══ Ship to centralized logging ═══
# Mount /var/log/druid/requests as a volume
# Fluentd/Filebeat sidecar → Loki/Elasticsearch
# Or use emitter-based logging:
druid.request.logging.type=composing
druid.request.logging.loggerProviders=[{"type":"file","dir":"/var/log/druid/requests"}]

Audit queries you should be able to answer:

  • "Who queried the financial_events datasource in the last 24 hours?" → filter audit log by datasource + time
  • "What were the slowest queries last week?" → sort by query/time descending
  • "Is user X running unbounded GROUP BY queries?" → filter by identity + check for missing LIMIT
  • "How many queries per datasource per hour?" → aggregate for capacity planning

24.8 Security Checklist — Production Hardening

CategoryItemStatus
NetworkTLS enabled on all Druid endpoints (druid.enableTlsPort=true)☐
NetworkPlaintext HTTP disabled (druid.enablePlaintextPort=false)☐
NetworkInternal mTLS between all Druid services☐
NetworkSecurity groups restrict Druid ports to authorized networks only☐
AuthNAuthenticator chain configured (LDAP/OAuth/Basic)☐
AuthNDefault admin password changed from initial value☐
AuthNService accounts use mTLS or dedicated credentials (not shared)☐
AuthZRBAC roles defined with least-privilege principle☐
AuthZNo wildcard datasource grants for non-admin users☐
EncryptionS3 SSE-KMS enabled on all Druid buckets☐
EncryptionEBS encryption enabled on all Historical + MM PVCs☐
EncryptionRDS encryption at rest enabled☐
AuditRequest logging enabled and shipped to centralized log store☐
AuditQuery identity logged with every request☐
SecretsPasswords stored in K8s Secrets / Vault — not in plaintext config files☐
SecretsTLS certificates rotated automatically (cert-manager)☐
⚠️ Common Pitfall Leaving druid.enablePlaintextPort=true alongside TLS. If plaintext HTTP is enabled, any client can bypass TLS by connecting to the non-TLS port directly — all authentication and encryption is circumvented. Always disable plaintext when TLS is enabled. The only exception: during migration from plaintext → TLS, keep both temporarily while updating all client configurations, then disable plaintext.
⚙️ Engineering Insight Secrets management: Never put passwords in Druid's runtime.properties files. Use one of: (1) K8s Secrets mounted as environment variables (${TLS_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD} in properties), (2) HashiCorp Vault with the Vault Agent injector sidecar, (3) AWS Secrets Manager with a custom init container that fetches secrets at pod startup. Druid supports environment variable substitution in properties files: druid.metadata.storage.connector.password=${METADATA_PASSWORD}.

Section 24 — Key Takeaways

LayerWhat It ProtectsProduction Recommendation
TLS / mTLSData in transit (client→Druid, Druid→Druid)Enable on ALL endpoints. Disable plaintext. Use cert-manager for auto-rotation.
AuthenticationIdentity verificationLDAP or OAuth for humans. mTLS for services. Chain multiple authenticators.
Authorization (RBAC)Datasource-level access controlLeast-privilege roles. No wildcard grants for non-admin.
Row-level securityRow-level filtering per userNot native — implement via proxy injection or separate datasources.
Encryption at restData on disk and in object storageS3 SSE-KMS (zero perf impact). EBS encryption. RDS encryption.
Audit loggingCompliance and debuggingLog every query with identity, SQL, time, bytes. Ship to Loki/ES.
Part 9 — Section 25

Data Lifecycle and Retention Management

Every state a segment passes through — from ingestion to permanent deletion — and how to automate the entire lifecycle with rules and kill tasks.

25 — Data Lifecycle and Retention Management

25.1 Segment Lifecycle State Machine

Every Druid segment passes through a defined set of states from creation to permanent deletion. Understanding this state machine is essential for debugging "where is my data?" problems and designing retention policies:

Figure 25.1 — Segment lifecycle: ACTIVE → PUBLISHING → PUBLISHED → LOADING → SERVED → (compaction cycle) → OVERSHADOWED → UNUSED → KILLED

Key state transitions to understand:

  • SERVED → OVERSHADOWED: Happens when compaction or REPLACE INTO creates a new segment version for the same interval. The old version is automatically overshadowed — no manual action needed.
  • SERVED → UNUSED: Happens when retention rules (dropForever) determine data is past retention period. The Coordinator marks segments as unused — they're dropped from Historicals but still exist in deep storage.
  • UNUSED → KILLED: Only happens when a Kill Task explicitly deletes the segment from deep storage and removes its metadata. This is the only way to reclaim S3 space.
⚠️ Common Pitfall S3 bill grows despite retention rules. Retention rules only mark segments as UNUSED (dropped from Historicals). The segment files remain in S3 indefinitely. You MUST run kill tasks to permanently delete them from deep storage. Without kill tasks, your S3 bill grows forever — even for data you've "dropped" via retention rules.

25.2 Retention Rules — Tiered Data Management

Retention rules are the primary mechanism for managing data lifecycle. They're evaluated in order — the first matching rule wins. Rules are set per datasource via the Coordinator API:

Retention Rules — Hot / Warm / DropJSON
// POST /druid/coordinator/v1/rules/user_events
[
  {
    // Rule 1: Last 30 days → Hot tier, 2 replicas (fast SSD)
    "type": "loadByInterval",
    "interval": "P30D/now",
    "tieredReplicants": { "hot": 2 }
  },
  {
    // Rule 2: 30 days to 1 year → Warm tier, 1 replica (standard HDD)
    "type": "loadByInterval",
    "interval": "P1Y/now",
    "tieredReplicants": { "warm": 1 }
  },
  {
    // Rule 3: Everything older → DROP (mark unused, not queryable)
    "type": "dropForever"
  }
]

// Evaluation order matters:
// A segment for 2024-03-15 checks Rule 1 first.
//   - Is it within P30D/now? If yes → load on hot tier, 2 replicas. DONE.
//   - If no → check Rule 2. Within P1Y/now? → warm tier, 1 replica. DONE.
//   - If no → Rule 3 catches all remaining: drop.

Rule Types Reference

Rule TypeDescriptionExample
loadByIntervalLoad segments whose interval overlaps a relative time range"interval": "P30D/now" — last 30 days
loadByPeriodSame as loadByInterval but with period syntax"period": "P90D" — last 90 days
loadForeverAlways load (never drop) — use for small reference datasourcesLookup tables, config data
dropByIntervalDrop segments in a specific interval"interval": "2020-01-01/2021-01-01" — drop all of 2020
dropByPeriodDrop segments older than a period"period": "P2Y" — drop older than 2 years
dropForeverCatch-all: drop everything not matched by earlier rulesAlways the last rule in the chain
Figure 25.2 — Retention rules tier data by age: hot SSD for recent, warm HDD for historical, drop + kill for expired

25.3 Kill Tasks — Permanent Deletion from Deep Storage

The kill task is the only way to permanently delete segments from deep storage (S3/GCS) and remove their metadata entries. Without kill tasks, "unused" segments accumulate in S3 forever.

Manual Kill Task

Submit Kill Task via APIbash
# Kill all unused segments for user_events in the first half of 2023
curl -X POST "http://overlord:8090/druid/indexer/v1/task" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "type": "kill",
    "dataSource": "user_events",
    "interval": "2023-01-01/2023-07-01"
  }'

# IMPORTANT: Only kills segments that are already marked UNUSED.
# If retention rules haven't marked them unused yet, kill does nothing.
# Order: retention rule marks unused → kill task deletes from S3.

# Check task status:
curl "http://overlord:8090/druid/indexer/v1/task/{taskId}/status"

Automatic Kill — Coordinator-Managed

Auto Kill Configurationproperties
# ═══ Enable automatic kill of unused segments ═══
druid.coordinator.kill.on=true

# How long to retain unused segments before killing (safety buffer)
druid.coordinator.kill.durationToRetain=P90D  # 90 days after marked unused

# How often the Coordinator checks for segments to kill
druid.coordinator.kill.period=P1D               # Once per day
