# Maximum number of segments to kill per cycle (throttle)
druid.coordinator.kill.maxSegments=1000

# ═══ Explanation of the safety buffer ═══
# durationToRetain=P90D means:
# - Retention rule marks segment unused on Day 0
# - Kill task won't delete it until Day 90
# - This gives you 90 days to realize a mistake and re-enable the data
# - After kill: data is GONE from S3 — unrecoverable without S3 versioning
⚙️ Engineering Insight The durationToRetain safety buffer is your undo window. If someone accidentally sets a retention rule that drops last week's data, you have durationToRetain days to notice the mistake and re-enable the segments before they're permanently deleted. Set this to at least 30 days for production. For critical datasources, consider 90 days. Once killed, the only recovery is from S3 versioning (if enabled) or restoring the entire metadata store backup + S3 snapshot.

25.4 Segment Versioning and Overshadowing

When new segments are published for the same datasource + time interval (via compaction or REPLACE INTO), the old segments are overshadowed by the new version. This is Druid's mechanism for atomic data replacement:

ConceptMechanismImplication
Version stringEach segment has a version (timestamp string, e.g., 2024-03-15T10:30:00.000Z)Newer version for same interval = higher priority
OvershadowingCoordinator detects newer version → instructs Historicals to drop old versionAutomatic — no manual intervention needed
AtomicityOld version only dropped AFTER ALL partitions of new version are loadedQueries never see a mix of old and new versions for same interval
Unused transitionOvershadowed segments become UNUSED after Coordinator verifies new version is fully servedUNUSED segments still in S3 — kill task needed for cleanup
TombstonesEmpty segments that signal "no data for this interval" after REPLACE produces gapsEnsures old data is properly overshadowed even when replacement has no data
Check Segment Versions for an Intervalbash
# List all segment versions for a specific interval
curl -s "http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/datasources/user_events/intervals/2024-03-15_2024-03-16" \
  | jq '.[].version'

# Output:
# "2024-03-15T10:00:00.000Z"   ← old (from streaming ingestion)
# "2024-03-16T02:00:00.000Z"   ← newer (from compaction) — THIS is served

# Check if old versions are overshadowed:
curl -s "http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/datasources/user_events/segments?full" \
  | jq '[.[] | select(.interval == "2024-03-15T00:00:00.000Z/2024-03-16T00:00:00.000Z") | {id: .identifier, version: .version, used: .used}]'

25.5 Cost Impact of Retention Decisions

Retention rules directly control your infrastructure costs. Here's a worked example for a datasource ingesting 50GB/day after rollup:

Retention StrategyHistorical StorageS3 StorageMonthly Cost (est.)
Keep forever (no rules)50GB × 365 × 2 replicas = 36.5 TB on SSD50GB × 365 = 18.25 TB~$15,000+ (all SSD)
Hot 30d / Drop rest50GB × 30 × 2 = 3 TB on SSD50GB × 30 = 1.5 TB~$1,800
Hot 30d / Warm 1y / Drop3 TB SSD + 50GB × 335 × 1 = 16.75 TB HDD50GB × 365 = 18.25 TB~$3,500
Hot 30d / Warm 1y / Drop + Kill3 TB SSD + 16.75 TB HDD50GB × 365 = 18.25 TB (killed data freed)~$3,200
⚙️ Engineering Insight The biggest cost savings come from two decisions: (1) Reducing hot-tier replicas from 2 to 1 for warm data — cuts warm-tier storage cost in half. (2) Enabling kill tasks to actually reclaim S3 space for expired data. A typical cluster with "keep forever" defaults wastes 60–80% of its storage budget on data nobody queries. Run SELECT __time, COUNT(*) FROM sys.segments WHERE datasource='X' GROUP BY 1 to identify how much of each datasource's data is actually queried.

25.6 Practical Retention Configuration Patterns

Pattern A — Dashboard Datasource (high-frequency queries on recent data)JSON
[
  { "type": "loadByPeriod", "period": "P7D",
    "tieredReplicants": { "hot": 2 } },    // Last 7 days: hot SSD, 2 replicas
  { "type": "loadByPeriod", "period": "P90D",
    "tieredReplicants": { "warm": 1 } },    // 7–90 days: warm HDD, 1 replica
  { "type": "dropForever" }                  // Older: drop
]
Pattern B — Compliance Datasource (must retain 7 years)JSON
[
  { "type": "loadByPeriod", "period": "P30D",
    "tieredReplicants": { "hot": 2 } },
  { "type": "loadByPeriod", "period": "P1Y",
    "tieredReplicants": { "warm": 1 } },
  { "type": "loadByPeriod", "period": "P7Y",
    "tieredReplicants": { "cold": 1 } },   // 1–7 years: cold tier, cheapest storage
  { "type": "dropForever" }                  // After 7 years: drop (regulatory minimum met)
]
Pattern C — Lookup/Reference Datasource (tiny, always loaded)JSON
[
  { "type": "loadForever",
    "tieredReplicants": { "hot": 2 } }       // Always loaded, never dropped
]
// Use for: country code lookups, product catalogs, config tables
// These are small (< 1GB) — cost of keeping forever is negligible

25.7 Managing Retention Rules via API

Retention Rule API — CRUD Operationsbash
# ═══ GET current rules for a datasource ═══
curl -s http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/rules/user_events | jq .

# ═══ SET rules (replaces all existing rules) ═══
curl -X POST http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/rules/user_events \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '[
    {"type":"loadByPeriod","period":"P30D","tieredReplicants":{"hot":2}},
    {"type":"loadByPeriod","period":"P1Y","tieredReplicants":{"warm":1}},
    {"type":"dropForever"}
  ]'

# ═══ GET default cluster rules (applies to datasources without specific rules) ═══
curl -s http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/rules/_default | jq .

# ═══ SET default rules ═══
curl -X POST http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/rules/_default \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '[{"type":"loadForever","tieredReplicants":{"_default_tier":2}}]'

# ═══ Audit: who changed rules and when? ═══
curl -s http://coordinator:8081/druid/coordinator/v1/rules/user_events/history | jq .
⚠️ Common Pitfall The _default rules apply to ALL datasources that don't have datasource-specific rules. If the default is loadForever with 2 replicas, every new datasource you create will keep all data forever on the hot tier — silently consuming Historical storage. Set the default to something conservative (e.g., loadByPeriod P90D + dropForever) and override per datasource as needed.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Druid manage data retention and cost?" — Druid uses a rule-based retention system evaluated by the Coordinator. Rules are ordered: the first match wins. Common pattern: hot tier (30d, SSD, 2 replicas) → warm tier (1yr, HDD, 1 replica) → drop (mark unused). The drop rule only removes data from Historicals — not from S3. Kill tasks are required to permanently delete from deep storage and reclaim S3 costs. The durationToRetain safety buffer gives operators time to reverse accidental drops. Version-based overshadowing ensures atomic data replacement during compaction. The combination of tiering + retention + kill tasks can reduce storage costs by 70–90% compared to "keep everything on hot tier forever."

25.8 Monitoring Retention Health

What to MonitorHow to CheckHealthy StateAction if Unhealthy
Unused segment countDruid Console → DatasourcesDecreasing over time (kill tasks cleaning up)Enable auto-kill if not running
S3 storage sizeAWS Cost Explorer / S3 metricsProportional to retention period × daily ingestGrowing unbounded = kill tasks not running
Segment count per tiersegment/count metric by tier labelHot < warm < total by expected ratiosHot tier too large = rules not tiering correctly
Overshadowed segment countCoordinator API: segments with used=falseStable or decreasingGrowing = compaction producing versions faster than kill cleans
Rule audit logCoordinator API: /rules/{ds}/historyChanges tracked with author + timestampUnexpected changes = investigate who modified

Section 25 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaProduction Recommendation
Lifecycle statesACTIVE → PUBLISHED → SERVED → OVERSHADOWED → UNUSED → KILLEDUnderstand every state for debugging "where is my data?"
Retention rulesFirst-match-wins rule chain per datasourceHot (30d) → Warm (1yr) → Drop. Always end with dropForever.
Kill tasksOnly way to permanently delete from S3Enable auto-kill. Without it, S3 bill grows forever.
durationToRetainSafety buffer before kill30–90 days — your undo window for accidental drops.
OvershadowingNewer version replaces older atomicallyAutomatic via compaction/REPLACE. Old versions → unused → kill.
Cost impactTiering + retention + kill = 70–90% savingsAudit: is anyone querying data older than 30 days? If not, don't pay for it.
Part 10 — Section 26

Extension Architecture and Custom Development

Druid's modular plugin system — classloading internals, custom aggregators, input sources, emitters, and deep storage backends.

26 — Extension Architecture and Custom Development

26.1 Extension System Overview — Druid's Modular Core

Apache Druid is deliberately designed with a minimal core and a rich extension ecosystem. Almost every production feature — from Kafka ingestion to S3 deep storage to DataSketches approximations to security authentication — is loaded as an extension. This gives Druid several advantages: teams deploy only features they need (reducing attack surface and memory footprint), custom features can be added without forking core, and extensions can be developed and versioned independently.

The extension system is built on Google Guice (dependency injection) and Java's Service Provider Interface (SPI). Each extension is a JAR file placed in Druid's extensions directory. At startup, Druid discovers extensions, loads them in isolated classloaders, and binds their components into the Guice injector.

How Extensions Are Discovered and Loaded

  1. Configuration read: Druid reads druid.extensions.loadList from runtime.properties — an ordered JSON array of extension directory names.
  2. Directory scan: For each extension name, Druid looks in druid.extensions.directory (default: extensions/) for a subdirectory containing JAR files.
  3. Classloader creation: Each extension gets its own URLClassLoader with the extension's JARs on its classpath and Druid core classloader as parent. This isolation prevents dependency conflicts between extensions.
  4. SPI discovery: Druid uses ServiceLoader to find all implementations of DruidModule within each extension's classloader.
  5. Guice binding: Each DruidModule registers its components (aggregators, input sources, emitters, etc.) with the Guice injector via configure(Binder) and getJacksonModules().
  6. Initialization: Registered components are available throughout the Druid process — aggregators can be referenced in ingestion specs, input sources in ioConfig, etc.
Figure 26.1 — Extension loading pipeline: properties → directory scan → classloader isolation → SPI → Guice → runtime
Extension Configuration — runtime.propertiesproperties
# ═══ Core Extension Loading ═══
druid.extensions.loadList=[
  "druid-kafka-indexing-service",
  "druid-kinesis-indexing-service",
  "druid-s3-extensions",
  "druid-hdfs-storage",
  "druid-google-extensions",
  "druid-datasketches",
  "druid-bloom-filter",
  "druid-histogram",
  "druid-stats",
  "druid-basic-security",
  "druid-kerberos",
  "druid-parquet-extensions",
  "druid-orc-extensions",
  "druid-avro-extensions",
  "druid-protobuf-extensions",
  "druid-lookups-cached-global",
  "druid-lookups-cached-single",
  "prometheus-emitter"
]
 
# Directory where extension JARs are stored
druid.extensions.directory=extensions
 
# Directory for Hadoop-specific extensions
druid.extensions.hadoopDependenciesDir=hadoop-dependencies
 
# Allow loading community extensions
druid.extensions.searchCurrentClassloader=true
 
# ═══ Pull community extensions ═══
# java -classpath "lib/*" org.apache.druid.cli.Main tools pull-deps \
#   --no-default-hadoop -c org.apache.druid.extensions.contrib:druid-distinctcount
⚙️ Engineering Insight Extension load order matters when extensions depend on each other. If you encounter ClassNotFoundException at startup, verify dependent extensions are listed first. Also: every node in the cluster must have identical extension lists. A Broker that doesn't know about thetaSketch will fail to deserialize partial results from a Historical — the #1 cause of "unknown aggregator type" errors after upgrades.

26.2 Built-in Extension Catalog

CategoryExtensionProvidesKey Classes
Streamingdruid-kafka-indexing-serviceKafka Supervisor + tasksKafkaSupervisorSpec, KafkaIndexTask
druid-kinesis-indexing-serviceKinesis ingestionKinesisSupervisorSpec
Deep Storagedruid-s3-extensionsAmazon S3S3DataSegmentPusher, S3DataSegmentKiller
druid-google-extensionsGoogle Cloud StorageGoogleDataSegmentPusher
druid-azure-extensionsAzure Blob StorageAzureDataSegmentPusher
druid-hdfs-storageHDFSHdfsDataSegmentPusher
Sketchesdruid-datasketchesHLL, Theta, Quantiles, TupleHllSketchBuildAggregator, ThetaSketchAggregator
druid-bloom-filterBloom filter pruningBloomFilterAggregatorFactory
druid-histogramApproximate histogramsApproximateHistogramAggregatorFactory
druid-statsVariance, stddevVarianceAggregatorFactory
Formatsdruid-parquet-extensionsParquet readingParquetHadoopInputRowParser
druid-avro-extensionsAvro parsingAvroStreamInputFormat, SchemaRegistryDecoder
druid-protobuf-extensionsProtocol BuffersProtobufInputFormat
Securitydruid-basic-securityBasic auth + RBACBasicHTTPAuthenticator, BasicRBACAuthorizer
druid-kerberosKerberos (SPNEGO)KerberosAuthenticator
Lookupsdruid-lookups-cached-globalGlobal cached lookupsJdbcDataFetcher, KafkaLookupExtractorFactory
Monitoringprometheus-emitterPrometheus exportPrometheusEmitter
Communitydruid-distinctcountExact distinct countDistinctCountAggregator (expensive)
⚠️ Common Pitfall Loading unnecessary extensions wastes memory and slows startup. Each extension consumes 10–80 MB of classloader memory. A cluster using Kafka + S3 + DataSketches + Basic Security needs exactly 5 extensions — not the full 20+.

26.3 Writing a Custom Aggregator — Complete Production Guide

A complete custom aggregator requires five key interfaces:

  1. AggregatorFactory: Factory — defines name, field references, serialization, produces executor instances.
  2. Aggregator: Row-by-row on heap. Used for Scan queries.
  3. BufferAggregator: Off-heap buffer-based. Used by GroupBy for memory efficiency.
  4. VectorAggregator: Batch 512+ rows at once. Critical for performance (2–10× faster).
  5. PostAggregator (optional): Runs on Broker after merge — ratios, derived metrics.
Figure 26.2 — AggregatorFactory produces three executor variants; Broker uses CombiningFactory for merge

Complete Custom Aggregator: Weighted Average

WeightedAvgAggregatorFactory.java — The FactoryJAVA
@JsonTypeName("weightedAvg")
public class WeightedAvgAggregatorFactory extends AggregatorFactory {
 
    private final String name;
    private final String valueField;
    private final String weightField;
 
    @JsonCreator
    public WeightedAvgAggregatorFactory(
        @JsonProperty("name") String name,
        @JsonProperty("valueField") String valueField,
        @JsonProperty("weightField") String weightField
    ) {
        this.name = name;
        this.valueField = valueField;
        this.weightField = weightField;
    }
 
    @Override
    public Aggregator factorize(ColumnSelectorFactory factory) {
        return new WeightedAvgAggregator(
            factory.makeColumnValueSelector(valueField),
            factory.makeColumnValueSelector(weightField));
    }
 
    @Override
    public BufferAggregator factorizeBuffered(ColumnSelectorFactory factory) {
        return new WeightedAvgBufferAggregator(
            factory.makeColumnValueSelector(valueField),
            factory.makeColumnValueSelector(weightField));
    }
 
    @Override
    public VectorAggregator factorizeVector(VectorColumnSelectorFactory f) {
        return new WeightedAvgVectorAggregator(
            f.makeValueSelector(valueField),
            f.makeValueSelector(weightField));
    }
 
    @Override public boolean canVectorize(ColumnInspector ci) { return true; }
 
    @Override
    public AggregatorFactory getCombiningFactory() {
        return new WeightedAvgCombiningFactory(name);
    }
 
    @Override
    public Object finalizeComputation(Object intermediate) {
        if (intermediate instanceof WeightedAvgState s) {
            return s.totalWeight == 0 ? 0.0 : s.weightedSum / s.totalWeight;
        }
        return 0.0;
    }
 
    @Override public String getName() { return name; }
    @Override public int getMaxIntermediateSize() { return 16; }
}
WeightedAvgBufferAggregator.java — Off-Heap GroupByJAVA
public class WeightedAvgBufferAggregator implements BufferAggregator {
    private final BaseDoubleColumnValueSelector valSel;
    private final BaseDoubleColumnValueSelector wgtSel;
 
    @Override public void init(ByteBuffer buf, int pos) {
        buf.putDouble(pos, 0.0);        // weightedSum
        buf.putDouble(pos + 8, 0.0);    // totalWeight
    }
 
    @Override public void aggregate(ByteBuffer buf, int pos) {
        if (valSel.isNull() || wgtSel.isNull()) return;
        double v = valSel.getDouble(), w = wgtSel.getDouble();
        buf.putDouble(pos, buf.getDouble(pos) + v * w);
        buf.putDouble(pos + 8, buf.getDouble(pos + 8) + w);
    }
 
    @Override public Object get(ByteBuffer buf, int pos) {
        return new WeightedAvgState(buf.getDouble(pos), buf.getDouble(pos+8));
    }
 
    @Override public double getDouble(ByteBuffer buf, int pos) {
        double w = buf.getDouble(pos + 8);
        return w == 0 ? 0.0 : buf.getDouble(pos) / w;
    }
    @Override public void close() {}
}
WeightedAvgVectorAggregator.java — Vectorized (2–10× faster)JAVA
public class WeightedAvgVectorAggregator implements VectorAggregator {
    private final VectorValueSelector valSel, wgtSel;
 
    @Override public void init(ByteBuffer buf, int pos) {
        buf.putDouble(pos, 0.0); buf.putDouble(pos+8, 0.0);
    }
 
    @Override public void aggregate(
        ByteBuffer buf, int pos, int[] rows, int start, int end
    ) {
        double[] vals = valSel.getDoubleVector();
        double[] wgts = wgtSel.getDoubleVector();
        double sWV = 0, sW = 0;
 
        // Tight loop — JIT auto-vectorizes with SIMD
        if (rows != null) {
            for (int i = start; i < end; i++) {
                int r = rows[i];
                sWV += vals[r] * wgts[r]; sW += wgts[r];
            }
        } else {
            for (int i = start; i < end; i++) {
                sWV += vals[i] * wgts[i]; sW += wgts[i];
            }
        }
        buf.putDouble(pos, buf.getDouble(pos) + sWV);
        buf.putDouble(pos+8, buf.getDouble(pos+8) + sW);
    }
 
    @Override public Object get(ByteBuffer buf, int pos) {
        return new WeightedAvgState(buf.getDouble(pos), buf.getDouble(pos+8));
    }
    @Override public void close() {}
}
WeightedAvgDruidModule.java — Guice Registration + SPIJAVA
public class WeightedAvgDruidModule implements DruidModule {
    @Override
    public List<? extends Module> getJacksonModules() {
        return List.of(
            new SimpleModule("WeightedAvgModule")
                .registerSubtypes(new NamedType(
                    WeightedAvgAggregatorFactory.class, "weightedAvg"))
        );
    }
    @Override public void configure(Binder binder) {}
}
// SPI File: META-INF/services/org.apache.druid.initialization.DruidModule
// com.example.druid.aggregator.WeightedAvgDruidModule
Using Custom Aggregator in Ingestion SpecJSON
{
  "metricsSpec": [
    { "type": "count", "name": "row_count" },
    { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "revenue_sum", "fieldName": "revenue" },
    {
      "type": "weightedAvg",       // Our custom aggregator!
      "name": "weighted_price",
      "valueField": "unit_price",
      "weightField": "quantity"
    }
  ]
}
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you add a custom metric type to Druid?" — Write an extension with AggregatorFactory. Implement three variants: Aggregator (heap), BufferAggregator (off-heap), VectorAggregator (batch). Register via DruidModule + SPI. Key: always implement VectorAggregator — without it, 2–10× slower.

26.4 Writing a Custom Input Source

Custom REST API InputSourceJAVA
@JsonTypeName("customApi")
public class CustomApiInputSource
    implements SplittableInputSource<List<String>> {
 
    private final String apiBaseUrl;
    private final List<String> endpoints;
    private final Map<String, String> headers;
 
    @Override public boolean isSplittable() { return true; }
 
    @Override
    public Stream<InputSplit<List<String>>> createSplits(
        InputFormat fmt, SplitHintSpec hint
    ) {
        // Each endpoint = one parallel sub-task
        return endpoints.stream()
            .map(ep -> new InputSplit<>(List.of(ep)));
    }
 
    @Override
    public InputSourceReader reader(
        InputRowSchema schema, InputFormat fmt, File tempDir
    ) {
        return new CustomApiReader(apiBaseUrl, endpoints, headers, schema, fmt);
    }
}

26.5 Custom Deep Storage Backend

InterfaceResponsibilityCalled By
DataSegmentPusherUpload segment files to deep storagePeon after segment build
DataSegmentKillerPermanently delete segments (kill tasks)Coordinator kill task
DataSegmentMoverMove/rename segments (tier changes)Coordinator
URIDataPullerDownload segments to Historical cacheHistorical on load
Figure 26.3 — Four deep storage interfaces: Pusher (upload), Puller (download), Killer (delete), Mover (rename)

26.6 Extension Performance Impact Analysis

ExtensionStartupMemoryQuery ImpactNotes
druid-datasketches+200 ms+30 MBSketch ops ~2× slower than sumEssential; always load
druid-kafka-indexing-service+100 ms+20 MBNone (ingestion only)Skip if batch-only
druid-s3-extensions+300 ms+50 MBNone (storage only)AWS SDK is heavy
druid-bloom-filter+50 ms+5 MB+1 ms per segment pruneOnly if using bloom pruning
druid-basic-security+150 ms+15 MB+0.5 ms per queryRequired for security
druid-parquet-extensions+400 ms+80 MBNone (ingestion only)Very heavy; MM only
prometheus-emitter+50 ms+10 MB+0.1 ms overheadEssential for monitoring
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Selective Loading per Node Type Different node types can load different extensions. Maintain separate runtime.properties per node type:
  • Broker: datasketches, bloom-filter, basic-security, prometheus-emitter
  • Historical: datasketches, bloom-filter, basic-security, prometheus-emitter, s3-extensions
  • MiddleManager: kafka-indexing, s3-extensions, parquet, datasketches, basic-security, prometheus-emitter
  • Coordinator/Overlord: s3-extensions, basic-security, prometheus-emitter
This saves 100–200 MB per node and reduces startup time.

26.7 Deployment Checklist

StepActionVerification
1Build JAR: mvn package< 500 KB for aggregators
2Copy to extensions/custom-weighted-avg/ls extensions/custom-weighted-avg/*.jar
3Add to druid.extensions.loadListVerify on every node type
4Rolling restart all Druid processesLogs: "Loading extension custom-weighted-avg"
5Test ingestion with custom aggregatorSegments created with custom metric
6Test query referencing aggregatorResults match expected values
7Monitor for OOM, GC, latencyGrafana: JVM heap, query/time, GC
⚠️ Common Pitfall Version mismatch. Extension compiled against Druid 28.x but cluster runs 29.x causes NoSuchMethodError at runtime. Always compile against the exact Druid version deployed. Pin in pom.xml and update with every upgrade.

Section 26 — Key Takeaways

TopicKey Point
ArchitectureClassloader isolation per extension + Guice DI + Java SPI discovery
Custom AggregatorAggregatorFactory + BufferAggregator + VectorAggregator (always implement vectorized!)
Custom InputSourceInputSource + SplittableInputSource for parallel ingestion
Deep Storage4 interfaces: Pusher, Puller, Killer, Mover
ProductionLoad only needed extensions per node type. Compile against exact Druid version.
Part 10 — Section 27

Lakehouse Integration — Iceberg, Delta, Spark, and Trino

Druid as the real-time serving layer on top of the modern lakehouse — ingestion from data lakes, query federation, CDC pipelines, and BI tool connectivity.

27 — Lakehouse Integration

27.1 Druid's Role in the Modern Lakehouse Architecture

The modern lakehouse unifies the data warehouse and data lake on open table formats (Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake, Apache Hudi) running on S3/GCS. Druid is not a replacement for the lakehouse — it is the real-time serving layer on top of it. The lakehouse handles ETL, ad-hoc analytics, and data science. Druid handles sub-second dashboard queries, high-concurrency API serving, and real-time alerting.

Three data flow patterns:

  1. Real-time: Source → Kafka → Druid (streaming ingestion, seconds latency)
  2. Batch: Lake (Iceberg/Delta) → Spark export → Druid REPLACE INTO (backfills, corrections)
  3. Federation: Trino queries Druid connector + Iceberg connector in a single SQL
Figure 27.1 — Three data flow patterns: real-time (Kafka), batch (lake reindex), federation (Trino)
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Why Not Just Use the Lakehouse? Trino on Iceberg delivers 2–10s query latency. But when you need sub-second P99, 100+ concurrent dashboards, real-time freshness, and predictable latency — Druid's pre-aggregated bitmap-indexed memory-mapped architecture delivers what the lakehouse cannot. The lakehouse is the source of truth; Druid is the serving layer.

27.2 Ingesting from Apache Iceberg Tables

Spark: Export Iceberg to Parquet for DruidPython
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql import functions as F
 
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("iceberg-to-druid-export") \
    .config("spark.sql.catalog.lakehouse",
            "org.apache.iceberg.spark.SparkCatalog") \
    .config("spark.sql.catalog.lakehouse.type", "glue") \
    .getOrCreate()
 
# Read with snapshot isolation (time travel)
df = spark.read.format("iceberg") \
    .option("snapshot-id", "7654321098765432100") \
    .load("lakehouse.analytics.user_events")
 
# Filter + pre-aggregate for Druid rollup
df.filter((df.event_time >= "2024-06-01") & (df.event_time < "2024-07-01")) \
  .groupBy("event_date", "country", "platform", "event_type") \
  .agg(F.count("*").alias("event_count"), F.sum("revenue").alias("revenue_sum")) \
  .write.mode("overwrite") \
  .parquet("s3://druid-staging/iceberg-export/user_events/2024-06/")
Druid REPLACE INTO from Iceberg-Exported ParquetSQL
REPLACE INTO "user_events"
  OVERWRITE WHERE __time >= TIMESTAMP '2024-06-01'
                 AND __time < TIMESTAMP '2024-07-01'
SELECT
    TIME_PARSE("event_date") AS __time,
    "country", "platform", "event_type",
    SUM("event_count") AS "event_count",
    SUM("revenue_sum") AS "revenue_sum"
FROM TABLE(EXTERN(
    '{"type":"s3","prefixes":["s3://druid-staging/iceberg-export/user_events/2024-06/"]}',
    '{"type":"parquet"}'))
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
PARTITIONED BY DAY CLUSTERED BY "country"
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Direct Iceberg File Reading Never point Druid at Iceberg's internal Parquet files directly. Iceberg uses a metadata layer (manifests, snapshots) to track current files. Pointing at raw S3 will ingest deleted data, partial writes, and orphaned files. Always export via Spark with snapshot isolation.

27.3 Ingesting from Delta Lake Tables

Delta Lake Export + CDF Streaming to KafkaPython
# Batch export with time travel
spark.read.format("delta") \
    .option("timestampAsOf", "2024-06-30T23:59:59Z") \
    .load("s3://data-lake-prod/delta/user_events") \
    .filter("event_date >= '2024-06-01' AND event_date < '2024-07-01'") \
    .write.parquet("s3://druid-staging/delta-export/2024-06/")
 
# ── Alternative: Delta CDF → Kafka → Druid ──
cdf_df = spark.readStream.format("delta") \
    .option("readChangeFeed", "true") \
    .option("startingVersion", 1) \
    .load("s3://data-lake-prod/delta/user_events")
 
cdf_df.writeStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "kafka:9092") \
    .option("topic", "druid-user-events-cdf") \
    .start()

27.4 Trino/Presto Federation — Querying Druid from the Lakehouse

Trino Druid Connectorproperties
# trino/etc/catalog/druid.properties
connector.name=druid
druid.broker-url=http://druid-broker:8082
druid.schema=druid
druid.compute-pushdown-enabled=true
Trino Federated Query — Druid + Iceberg JOINSQL
WITH current_rev AS (
    SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
    FROM druid.druid."user_events"       -- Druid catalog
    WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
    GROUP BY country
),
historical_rev AS (
    SELECT country, SUM(revenue) AS rev
    FROM lakehouse.analytics."user_events"  -- Iceberg catalog
    WHERE event_date >= DATE '2023-03-24'
      AND event_date < DATE '2023-03-31'
    GROUP BY country
)
SELECT c.country, c.rev AS current_rev, h.rev AS last_year_rev,
    ROUND(100.0 * (c.rev - h.rev) / NULLIF(h.rev, 0), 2) AS yoy_pct
FROM current_rev c JOIN historical_rev h ON c.country = h.country
ORDER BY yoy_pct DESC
Figure 27.2 — Trino federates Druid (real-time) and Iceberg (historical) with filter pushdown
🎯 Interview Tip "Design analytics with both real-time dashboards and ad-hoc historical analysis." — Lakehouse (Iceberg on S3 + Spark) as source of truth. Kafka as event bus. Druid as serving layer. Dashboards → Druid (sub-second). Ad-hoc → Trino federating Druid + Iceberg. Corrections → lake → Druid REPLACE. Separates serving from processing from storage.

27.5 CDC Pipelines — Debezium → Kafka → Druid

Figure 27.3 — Two CDC patterns: append-all with query-time dedup vs periodic materialization
Pattern A — Query-Time Dedup for CDC DataSQL
SELECT
    order_id,
    LATEST(order_status, __time)  AS current_status,
    LATEST(total_amount, __time)  AS current_amount,
    MIN(__time) AS created_at,
    MAX(__time) AS last_updated,
    COUNT(*) AS change_count
FROM "orders_cdc"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY
  AND cdc_operation != 'd'
GROUP BY order_id
Kafka Supervisor for CDC (Debezium Format)JSON
{
  "type": "kafka",
  "spec": {
    "dataSchema": {
      "dataSource": "orders_cdc",
      "timestampSpec": { "column": "ts_ms", "format": "millis" },
      "dimensionsSpec": {
        "dimensions": ["order_id", "customer_id", "order_status", "cdc_operation"]
      },
      "metricsSpec": [
        { "type": "count", "name": "change_count" },
        { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "total_amount", "fieldName": "after_total_amount" }
      ],
      "transformSpec": {
        "transforms": [
          { "type": "expression", "name": "cdc_operation",
            "expression": "json_value(payload, '$.op')" },
          { "type": "expression", "name": "order_id",
            "expression": "json_value(payload, '$.after.order_id')" },
          { "type": "expression", "name": "order_status",
            "expression": "json_value(payload, '$.after.status')" }
        ]
      },
      "granularitySpec": {
        "segmentGranularity": "HOUR",
        "queryGranularity": "NONE",
        "rollup": false    // Keep every CDC event
      }
    },
    "ioConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "consumerProperties": { "bootstrap.servers": "kafka:9092" },
      "topic": "dbserver1.public.orders",
      "taskCount": 4,
      "taskDuration": "PT1H"
    }
  }
}

27.6 BI Tool Integration — Superset and Grafana

Apache Superset ConnectionPython
# pip install pydruid sqlalchemy-druid
SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI = "druid://druid-broker:8082/druid/v2/sql/"
 
# With HTTPS + Basic Auth:
SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI = (
    "druid+https://druid-broker.prod.company.com:8282/druid/v2/sql/"
    "?header_Authorization=Basic%20dXNlcjpwYXNz"
)
Grafana Druid Datasource ProvisioningYAML
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Druid Production
    type: grafadruid-druid-datasource
    access: proxy
    url: http://druid-router:8888
    jsonData:
      connection: { url: http://druid-router:8888 }
      query: { defaultQueryType: sql }
    isDefault: true

27.7 Airflow Orchestration for Druid Reindex

Airflow DAG — Daily Druid ReindexPython
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import requests, time
 
dag = DAG("druid_daily_reindex",
    default_args={"owner": "data-eng", "retries": 2},
    schedule_interval="0 3 * * *",
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1), catchup=False)
 
def trigger_druid_reindex(**kwargs):
    ds = kwargs["ds"]
    sql = f"""
    REPLACE INTO "user_events"
    OVERWRITE WHERE __time >= TIMESTAMP '{ds}'
                 AND __time < TIMESTAMP '{ds}' + INTERVAL '1' DAY
    SELECT TIME_PARSE("event_time") AS __time,
           "country", "platform", "event_type",
           COUNT(*) AS "event_count", SUM("revenue") AS "revenue_sum"
    FROM TABLE(EXTERN(
        '{{"type":"s3","prefixes":["s3://staging/export/{ds}/"]}}}',
        '{{"type":"parquet"}}'))
    GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
    PARTITIONED BY DAY CLUSTERED BY "country"
    """
 
    resp = requests.post(
        "http://druid-router:8888/druid/v2/sql/task",
        json={"query": sql, "context": {"maxNumTasks": 8}})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    task_id = resp.json()["taskId"]
 
    while True:
        st = requests.get(
            f"http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/task/{task_id}/status"
        ).json()["status"]["statusCode"]
        if st == "SUCCESS": return task_id
        elif st == "FAILED": raise Exception(f"Task {task_id} failed")
        time.sleep(30)
 
PythonOperator(task_id="reindex", python_callable=trigger_druid_reindex, dag=dag)
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Orchestration Patterns
  • Daily reindex: Airflow: Spark reads Iceberg → Parquet → REPLACE INTO → monitor → Slack alert
  • Compaction monitor: Dagster sensor checks segment count hourly; triggers compaction if threshold exceeded
  • Data quality: Post-reindex, validate row counts against lakehouse source; alert if diff > 1%

Section 27 — Key Takeaways

PatternPathLatencyUse Case
① Real-timeKafka → DruidSecondsLive dashboards, real-time alerts
② Batch reindexIceberg/Delta → Spark → Druid REPLACEHoursBackfills, data corrections, curated metrics
③ FederationTrino → Druid + IcebergSeconds–MinutesAd-hoc analytics joining real-time + historical
CDCDebezium → Kafka → DruidSecondsOperational DB analytics, event sourcing
BI ToolsSuperset / Grafana → Druid SQLSub-secondDashboard visualization layer
Part 11 — Section 28

Advanced Monitoring, Alerting, and Query Audit

Production observability for Druid — Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards, SLO-based alerting, query tracing, slow-query debugging, and operational failure runbooks.

28 — Advanced Monitoring, Alerting, and Query Audit

28.1 Monitoring Stack Architecture

A production Druid cluster generates thousands of metrics per second across multiple process types. Building a comprehensive monitoring stack requires three pillars: metrics collection (Prometheus), visualization (Grafana), and alerting (Alertmanager → PagerDuty/Slack). Additionally, log aggregation (Loki/Elasticsearch) captures query-level audit trails and debugging information that metrics alone cannot provide.

Figure 28.1 — Production monitoring: Prometheus (metrics) + Loki (logs) + Grafana (dashboards) + Alertmanager (alerts)

28.2 Prometheus Emitter Configuration

Prometheus Emitter — runtime.propertiesproperties
# ═══ Enable Prometheus emitter on ALL Druid nodes ═══
druid.emitter=prometheus
druid.emitter.prometheus.port=9090
druid.emitter.prometheus.namespace=druid
 
# Dimension mapping — adds labels to Prometheus metrics
druid.emitter.prometheus.dimensionMapPath=/opt/druid/conf/prometheus_dimensions.json
 
# Strategy: exporter (Prometheus scrapes) vs pushgateway
druid.emitter.prometheus.strategy=exporter
 
# ═══ Composing emitter: Prometheus + logging simultaneously ═══
# druid.emitter=composing
# druid.emitter.composing.emitters=["prometheus","logging"]
Prometheus Scrape ConfigYAML
# prometheus.yml — scrape all Druid services
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: druid-broker
    scrape_interval: 15s
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - druid-broker-0:9090
          - druid-broker-1:9090
          - druid-broker-2:9090
    metrics_path: /metrics
 
  - job_name: druid-historical
    scrape_interval: 15s
    kubernetes_sd_configs:          # K8s service discovery
      - role: pod
    relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: [__meta_kubernetes_pod_label_app]
        regex: druid-historical
        action: keep
 
  - job_name: druid-middlemanager
    scrape_interval: 15s
    static_configs:
      - targets: [druid-mm-0:9090, druid-mm-1:9090, druid-mm-2:9090, druid-mm-3:9090]
 
  - job_name: druid-coordinator
    scrape_interval: 30s
    static_configs:
      - targets: [druid-coordinator-0:9090, druid-coordinator-1:9090]
⚙️ Engineering Insight In Kubernetes, use ServiceMonitor CRDs (from Prometheus Operator) instead of static scrape configs. Define one ServiceMonitor per Druid component label — Prometheus auto-discovers pods as they scale. This eliminates manual target maintenance and works seamlessly with HPA.

28.3 Critical Metrics Reference — What to Monitor

Query Performance Metrics

MetricComponentWhat It Tells YouAlert Threshold
druid_query_timeBrokerEnd-to-end query latency (ms)P99 > 5s → warning; P99 > 15s → critical
druid_query_segment_timeHistoricalPer-segment scan timeP99 > 2s → investigate GC or cold segments
druid_query_segment_and_cache_timeHistoricalScan + cache lookup timeCompare with segment_time to measure cache benefit
druid_query_countBrokerQueries per second (QPS)Track trend; spike = possible query storm
druid_query_failed_countBrokerFailed queries (timeout, OOM, error)Any sustained > 0 → investigate
druid_query_interrupted_countBrokerQueries cancelled (client disconnect, timeout)> 5% of total → timeout tuning needed

Ingestion Metrics

MetricComponentWhat It Tells YouAlert Threshold
druid_ingest_kafka_lagMiddleManagerKafka consumer lag (messages behind)Growing for > 10 min → scale up taskCount
druid_ingest_events_processedMiddleManagerEvents ingested per secondDrop > 50% from baseline → task failure
druid_ingest_events_unparseableMiddleManagerParse errors (bad JSON, schema mismatch)Any sustained > 0 → check data quality
druid_ingest_events_thrown_awayMiddleManagerEvents rejected (outside window, filter)> 1% of processed → check time window config
druid_ingest_handoff_countMiddleManagerSegments handed off to deep storage0 for > 2× taskDuration → handoff stuck
druid_ingest_rows_outputMiddleManagerRows written to segmentsCompare with events_processed for rollup ratio

Segment and Cluster Health Metrics

MetricComponentWhat It Tells YouAlert Threshold
druid_segment_loadqueue_countCoordinatorSegments waiting to be loaded on Historicals> 500 sustained → Historicals overloaded
druid_segment_dropqueue_countCoordinatorSegments queued for unloadGrowing → Coordinator cycle too slow
druid_segment_used_bytesHistoricalTotal bytes of loaded segments> 85% of maxSize → add capacity
druid_segment_countCoordinatorTotal segment count per datasource> 10K per datasource → compaction needed
druid_coordinator_timeCoordinatorCoordinator run cycle duration> 120s → too many segments or rules
druid_task_success_countOverlordSuccessful task completionsDrop to 0 → all tasks failing
druid_task_failed_countOverlordFailed task countAny sustained > 0 → check task logs

JVM and Resource Metrics

MetricComponentWhat It Tells YouAlert Threshold
druid_jvm_mem_usedAllJVM heap usage> 85% sustained → GC pressure, risk OOM
druid_jvm_gc_timeAllTime spent in GC (ms/min)> 10% of wall time → switch to ZGC or reduce heap
druid_jvm_gc_countAllGC frequencyTrack trend; sudden spike = memory pressure
druid_jetty_numOpenConnectionsBrokerActive HTTP connections> 80% of maxThreads → scale Brokers
druid_cache_hitRateBroker/HistoricalCache hit ratio< 20% → cache too small or workload not cache-friendly

28.4 Grafana Dashboard Blueprints

Build these five Grafana dashboards for complete Druid observability:

DashboardKey PanelsPromQL Examples
1. Query Performance P50/P95/P99 latency trend, QPS, failed queries, cache hit rate histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(druid_query_time_bucket[5m]))
2. Ingestion Health Kafka lag per partition, events/sec, parse errors, handoff rate sum(rate(druid_ingest_events_processed[5m])) by (dataSource)
3. Segment Health Load queue, segment count per DS, compaction status, used bytes druid_segment_loadqueue_count
4. JVM Resources Heap usage, GC time, GC count, direct memory, thread count druid_jvm_gc_time / 60000 (GC % of wall time)
5. Task Management Running tasks, pending tasks, success/failure rates, task duration rate(druid_task_failed_count[5m])
Grafana Dashboard JSON — Query Performance Panel (P99 Latency)JSON
{
  "title": "Query P99 Latency by DataSource",
  "type": "timeseries",
  "targets": [{
    "expr": "histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(druid_query_time_bucket{job=\"druid-broker\"}[5m])) by (le, dataSource))",
    "legendFormat": "{{dataSource}} P99"
  }],
  "fieldConfig": {
    "defaults": {
      "unit": "ms",
      "thresholds": {
        "steps": [
          { "color": "green", "value": null },
          { "color": "yellow", "value": 5000 },     // 5s warning
          { "color": "red", "value": 15000 }       // 15s critical
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

28.5 SLO-Based Alerting Rules

Define alerts based on Service Level Objectives (SLOs), not arbitrary thresholds. Example SLOs for a Druid cluster:

  • Query SLO: 99.5% of queries complete in < 5 seconds
  • Ingestion SLO: Kafka lag never exceeds 60 seconds of data
  • Availability SLO: All datasources queryable with < 1 minute of data loss
Prometheus Alerting Rules — druid-alerts.ymlYAML
groups:
  - name: druid-query-alerts
    rules:
      # ── Query latency SLO violation ──
      - alert: DruidQueryP99High
        expr: >
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum(rate(druid_query_time_bucket{job="druid-broker"}[10m]))
            by (le)) > 5000
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Druid P99 query latency > 5s for 5 minutes"
          runbook: "Check slow query log. Look for missing time filters or high-cardinality GROUP BY."
 
      # ── Query failure rate ──
      - alert: DruidQueryFailureRate
        expr: >
          rate(druid_query_failed_count[5m])
            / rate(druid_query_count[5m]) > 0.05
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Druid query failure rate > 5%"
 
  - name: druid-ingestion-alerts
    rules:
      # ── Kafka lag growing ──
      - alert: DruidKafkaLagGrowing
        expr: >
          increase(druid_ingest_kafka_lag[10m]) > 0
            and druid_ingest_kafka_lag > 100000
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Kafka lag growing for 10 min — ingestion falling behind"
          runbook: "Scale up taskCount. Check MiddleManager resource usage. Verify Kafka broker health."
 
      # ── Parse errors ──
      - alert: DruidIngestionParseErrors
        expr: rate(druid_ingest_events_unparseable[5m]) > 0
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Druid ingestion parse errors detected — check data quality"
 
  - name: druid-cluster-alerts
    rules:
      # ── Segment load queue backlog ──
      - alert: DruidSegmentLoadQueueHigh
        expr: druid_segment_loadqueue_count > 1000
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "1000+ segments queued for loading — Historicals may be overloaded"
 
      # ── Historical disk full ──
      - alert: DruidHistoricalDiskFull
        expr: druid_segment_used_bytes / druid_server_maxSize > 0.90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Historical at 90%+ segment cache capacity"
 
      # ── GC pressure ──
      - alert: DruidGCPressure
        expr: rate(druid_jvm_gc_time[5m]) / 60000 > 0.10
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "JVM spending > 10% of time in GC — consider ZGC or heap tuning"
🎯 Interview Tip "What causes P99 query latency spikes in Druid?" — Top 5 causes: (1) GC pauses on Historical (200ms G1GC stop-the-world → switch to ZGC). (2) Cold segments not in OS page cache (disk read latency). (3) Missing time filter scanning all segments. (4) High-cardinality GROUP BY blowing merge buffer. (5) Straggler Historical node — one slow node blocks entire scatter-gather. Monitor each cause with different metrics: GC time, segment cache hit rate, query/segment count, merge buffer usage, per-node latency.

28.6 Query Audit Logging and End-to-End Tracing

Every Druid query gets a unique queryId. By enabling request logging, you capture the full audit trail: who ran what query, when, how long it took, and how many bytes were scanned. This is essential for compliance (SOC 2, HIPAA), debugging, and capacity planning.

Request Logging Configurationproperties
# ═══ File-based request logging (on Broker) ═══
druid.request.logging.type=composing
druid.request.logging.loggerProviders=[{"type":"file","dir":"/var/log/druid/requests"}]
 
# Each log entry (JSON) contains:
# {
#   "queryId": "abc-123-def-456",
#   "sqlQuery": "SELECT country, SUM(revenue) FROM ...",
#   "identity": "alice@company.com",
#   "remoteAddress": "10.0.5.23",
#   "query/time": 342,
#   "query/bytes": 15728640,
#   "query/segment/count": 7,
#   "success": true
# }
 
# ═══ Audit logging for config changes ═══
druid.audit.manager.type=sql
# Logs supervisor submissions, rule changes, datasource config changes
# Stored in metadata store — queryable via Coordinator API

Useful Audit Queries

Query Audit Analysis with Loki/ElasticsearchLogQL
# ── Who queried financial_events in the last 24h? ──
{job="druid-broker"} |= "financial_events" | json | identity != ""
 
# ── Top 10 slowest queries last week ──
{job="druid-broker"} | json | query_time > 10000
  | sort by (query_time) desc | limit 10
 
# ── Queries without time filter (full table scan) ──
{job="druid-broker"} | json | query_segment_count > 1000
 
# ── Query volume per datasource per hour ──
sum by (dataSource)(count_over_time(
  {job="druid-broker"} | json [1h]))
⚙️ Engineering Insight — End-to-End Query Tracing Every Druid query carries a queryId through all components: Broker logs, Historical logs, and metrics. To trace a slow query end-to-end: (1) find the queryId in Broker request logs, (2) grep for that queryId in Historical logs to find per-segment scan times, (3) check if any Historical had a GC pause during that query window. In Kubernetes, use kubectl logs --all-containers with a queryId grep across all pods. For structured tracing, integrate with OpenTelemetry — Druid doesn't natively support it, but you can instrument the Broker's HTTP handler to create spans that propagate to downstream Historical calls.

28.7 Debugging Slow Queries — Systematic Approach

Figure 28.2 — Systematic slow query debugging: segment count → per-node latency → GC vs cardinality
EXPLAIN PLAN — Diagnose Query ExecutionSQL
-- Step 1: Check what native query Druid will execute
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum)
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY 2 DESC
LIMIT 10
 
-- Look for in the output:
-- • queryType: "topN" (fast) vs "groupBy" (slower)
--   → single dim + ORDER BY + LIMIT = Druid should choose topN
-- • intervals: how many time intervals are queried?
-- • filter: is the WHERE clause pushed down as bitmap filter?
-- • columns: are only needed columns being read?
 
-- Step 2: Check actual segment count for the time range
-- GET http://broker:8082/druid/v2/datasources/user_events
-- ?interval=2024-03-24/2024-03-31

28.8 Production Failure Patterns and Runbooks

FailureSymptomsRoot CauseRunbook
Ingestion lag spike Kafka lag growing; new data not visible in queries MiddleManagers overloaded or task failures 1. Check task logs for errors
2. Scale up taskCount
3. Verify Kafka broker health
4. Check MM CPU/memory
Query timeout Queries return 504/timeout; dashboards show errors Straggler Historical, GC pause, or oversized query 1. Check per-Historical latency
2. Check GC metrics on slow node
3. Add time filter if missing
4. Increase query.timeout
Segment load backlog New segments not appearing; loadqueue_count > 1000 Historicals at capacity or slow deep storage 1. Check Historical disk usage
2. Check S3 bandwidth
3. Add Historical nodes
4. Review load rules
OOM on Broker Broker JVM crashes during large GroupBy Unbounded GroupBy on high-cardinality dimension 1. Add maxMergingDictionarySize
2. Add LIMIT clause
3. Use approximate queries
4. Increase Broker heap
Metadata lock contention Supervisor restarts slow; task submission hangs Too many concurrent tasks competing for metadata locks 1. Tune metadata store pool size
2. Reduce concurrent compaction
3. Check RDS CPU/connections
Compaction stall Segment count growing; query latency increasing Compaction tasks starved by ingestion tasks 1. Dedicated compaction MMs
2. Lower compaction priority
3. Increase worker capacity
4. Check skipOffsetFromLatest
ZooKeeper timeout Coordinator/Overlord leader election churn; "session expired" in logs ZK overloaded, network partition, or GC on ZK nodes 1. Check ZK pod health
2. Increase tickTime
3. Consider K8s extensions
4. Separate ZK onto dedicated nodes
⚠️ Common Pitfall Monitoring Druid without monitoring its dependencies. Druid depends on ZooKeeper, the metadata store (MySQL/PostgreSQL), Kafka, and deep storage (S3). A "Druid problem" is often actually a dependency failure: ZK session timeouts cause leader election churn, RDS connection exhaustion blocks task submission, S3 throttling delays segment downloads. Always monitor all dependencies alongside Druid itself.

28.9 Health Check Endpoints

Druid Health Check API Endpointsbash
# ── Broker health ──
curl -s http://broker:8082/status/health
# Returns: true (200) or error (503)
 
# ── Historical health ──
curl -s http://historical:8083/status/health
 
# ── Coordinator leader check ──
curl -s http://coordinator:8081/status/leader
# Returns the hostname of current leader
 
# ── Overlord leader check ──
curl -s http://overlord:8090/status/leader
 
# ── Self-discovery — is this node registered in cluster? ──
curl -s http://broker:8082/status/selfDiscovered
# Returns: {"selfDiscovered": true}
 
# ── Kubernetes readiness probe config ──
# Use /status/selfDiscovered for readiness
# Use /status/health for liveness
Kubernetes Probe ConfigurationYAML
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /status/health
    port: 8082
  initialDelaySeconds: 60
  periodSeconds: 30
  failureThreshold: 3
 
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /status/selfDiscovered
    port: 8082
  initialDelaySeconds: 30
  periodSeconds: 15
  failureThreshold: 5
 
startupProbe:                  # Historical needs time to load segments
  httpGet:
    path: /status/health
    port: 8083
  initialDelaySeconds: 120
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 30         # 5 min for segment download
⚙️ Engineering Insight For Historicals, use startupProbe with a generous timeout (5+ minutes). New Historical pods need to download GBs of segments from S3 before they can serve queries. If your readiness probe fails before segment loading completes, Kubernetes will restart the pod in a crash loop. The startupProbe (K8s 1.18+) disables liveness/readiness checks during initial startup.

Section 28 — Key Takeaways

AreaKey Recommendation
MetricsPrometheus emitter on all nodes. Scrape every 15s. K8s: use ServiceMonitor CRDs.
Dashboards5 Grafana dashboards: query perf, ingestion, segments, JVM, tasks.
AlertingSLO-based alerts: P99 latency, failure rate, Kafka lag, segment load queue, GC pressure.
AuditRequest logging with queryId, identity, SQL, time, bytes. Ship to Loki/ES.
Slow queriesEXPLAIN PLAN → check segment count → per-node latency → GC vs cardinality.
Health checks/status/health (liveness), /status/selfDiscovered (readiness). Startup probe for Historicals.
DependenciesMonitor ZK, RDS, Kafka, S3 alongside Druid — dependency failures masquerade as Druid issues.
Part 11 — Section 29

Disaster Recovery and Backup

Recovery strategies, metadata backup, multi-DC deployments, RPO/RTO planning, rolling upgrades, and incident response playbooks for Druid clusters.

29 — Disaster Recovery and Backup

29.1 Disaster Recovery Foundations — What Can Go Wrong

Druid's distributed architecture has three categories of state that must be protected separately. Understanding what each stores determines your backup strategy, recovery time, and data loss window:

  • Deep Storage (S3/GCS/HDFS): All segment files. This is the source of truth for all ingested data. If deep storage survives, data is recoverable.
  • Metadata Store (MySQL/PostgreSQL): Segment metadata, task state, supervisor offsets, rules, audit logs. This is the catalog — without it, Druid cannot discover which segments exist in deep storage.
  • Local State (Historical segment cache, ZooKeeper): Ephemeral — can always be reconstructed from deep storage + metadata store. Loss means temporary query unavailability during reconstruction, not permanent data loss.
Figure 29.1 — Three state categories: Deep Storage (durable), Metadata (critical backup), Local (ephemeral, auto-reconstruct)
⚠️ Common Pitfall The metadata store is the single most critical backup target. Without it, Druid cannot discover which segments exist in deep storage. Losing the metadata store without a backup requires manually reconstructing segment metadata from S3 file listings — a multi-day operation for large clusters. Always use managed RDS with automated daily snapshots and point-in-time recovery enabled.

29.2 Metadata Store Backup Strategies

StrategyRPORTOConfigurationCost
RDS Automated Snapshots24h (daily snapshot)10–30 minEnable automated backups; retention 7–35 daysLow (included in RDS)
RDS Point-in-Time Recovery~5 min (WAL replay)15–45 minEnabled with automated backups; retains WALLow (WAL storage cost)
RDS Multi-AZ0 (synchronous replica)1–2 min (auto failover)MultiAZ=true in RDS config2× RDS cost
Cross-Region Read ReplicaSeconds (async replication)5–10 min (promote replica)Create read replica in DR region1.5× RDS cost
pg_dump / mysqldump (manual)At dump time5–30 min (restore)Cron job: pg_dump → S3Minimal
Automated pg_dump to S3 (supplement to RDS snapshots)bash
#!/bin/bash — druid-metadata-backup.sh
# Run via cron every 6 hours as a defense-in-depth layer
 
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
DB_HOST="druid-metadata.cluster-xxxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com"
DB_NAME="druid"
S3_BUCKET="s3://druid-backups/metadata"
 
# Dump metadata store (typically 1–50 GB, completes in seconds to minutes)
pg_dump \
  -h ${DB_HOST} \
  -U druid_admin \
  -d ${DB_NAME} \
  --format=custom \
  --compress=9 \
  -f /tmp/druid_metadata_${TIMESTAMP}.dump
 
# Upload to S3 with server-side encryption
aws s3 cp /tmp/druid_metadata_${TIMESTAMP}.dump \
  ${S3_BUCKET}/druid_metadata_${TIMESTAMP}.dump \
  --sse aws:kms \
  --storage-class STANDARD_IA
 
# Clean up local file
rm -f /tmp/druid_metadata_${TIMESTAMP}.dump
 
# Retain last 30 backups in S3
aws s3 ls ${S3_BUCKET}/ | sort -r | tail -n +31 | \
  awk '{print $4}' | xargs -I{} aws s3 rm ${S3_BUCKET}/{}
 
echo "Backup complete: druid_metadata_${TIMESTAMP}.dump"
Metadata Restore from pg_dumpbash
# ═══ Emergency metadata restore procedure ═══
 
# 1. Stop all Druid processes (Coordinator, Overlord first)
kubectl scale statefulset druid-coordinator --replicas=0
kubectl scale statefulset druid-overlord --replicas=0
 
# 2. Download latest backup from S3
aws s3 cp s3://druid-backups/metadata/druid_metadata_latest.dump /tmp/
 
# 3. Restore to RDS (or new RDS instance)
pg_restore \
  -h druid-metadata-new.xxxxx.rds.amazonaws.com \
  -U druid_admin \
  -d druid \
  --clean --if-exists \
  /tmp/druid_metadata_latest.dump
 
# 4. Update Druid config to point to new metadata store (if different host)
# druid.metadata.storage.connector.connectURI=jdbc:postgresql://NEW_HOST:5432/druid
 
# 5. Restart Coordinator and Overlord
kubectl scale statefulset druid-coordinator --replicas=2
kubectl scale statefulset druid-overlord --replicas=2
 
# 6. Coordinator will read segment metadata → assign to Historicals
# 7. Historicals download segments from S3 → cluster recovers
# 8. Monitor: segment loadqueue_count should decrease to 0
⚙️ Engineering Insight RDS Multi-AZ is the minimum production requirement. It provides synchronous replication to a standby in another Availability Zone with automatic failover in 1–2 minutes. This handles the most common failure mode (single-AZ outage) with zero RPO and near-zero RTO. The pg_dump backup is a defense-in-depth layer for catastrophic scenarios (accidental table drop, RDS corruption, cross-region DR).

29.3 Full Cluster Recovery Procedure

If an entire Druid cluster is lost (region outage, catastrophic failure), recovery follows this sequence:

Figure 29.2 — Full DR recovery: metadata restore (5 min) → deploy (15 min) → segment download (hours)
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you design DR for a Druid cluster?" — The key insight: Druid's data is already durable in S3 (11 nines). The only critical backup is the metadata store — use RDS Multi-AZ + automated snapshots + pg_dump to S3. Recovery: restore metadata → deploy new cluster (same Helm chart) → Coordinator auto-assigns segments → Historicals download from S3. RPO = metadata snapshot age (typically 5 min with PITR). RTO = infrastructure deploy time + segment download time (proportional to data volume, typically hours for TB-scale).

29.4 Multi-DC and Multi-Region Deployments

Druid does not natively support multi-DC replication — each cluster is independent. Three patterns for multi-region deployments:

PatternArchitectureRPORTOCostBest For
Active-Passive Primary cluster serves all queries. Standby cluster in DR region ingests from same Kafka + same S3. Seconds (Kafka offset) Minutes (DNS failover) 2× compute Standard DR requirement
Active-Active Two independent clusters in different regions, both ingesting from Kafka. DNS routes to nearest. 0 (both active) 0 (both active) 2× everything Global low-latency
Shared Deep Storage One cluster ingests; standby is read-only from same S3 with cross-region replication. Minutes (S3 replication lag) Minutes (promote standby) 1.5× compute Cost-optimized DR
Figure 29.3 — Active-Active: both regions serve queries. Failover via DNS routing + RDS replica promotion.

29.5 Rolling Upgrades with Zero Downtime

Druid supports rolling upgrades — upgrade one node type at a time without cluster downtime. The order matters because of backward compatibility requirements:

OrderComponentWhy This OrderRollback Risk
1HistoricalsMust handle both old and new segment formats. Safe to upgrade first.Low — just rollback binary
2MiddleManagersNew ingestion features depend on Historical compatibility already in place.Low — restart tasks from offset
3BrokersMust understand response format from new Historicals + old simultaneously.Low — stateless
4CoordinatorNew segment management features; Historicals already on new version.Medium — segment assignment state
5OverlordLast — new task management; all workers already upgraded.Medium — task state
Rolling Upgrade Script — Historical StatefulSetbash
#!/bin/bash — rolling-upgrade-historical.sh
# Upgrade Historicals one pod at a time
 
NAMESPACE="druid"
STATEFULSET="druid-historical"
REPLICAS=$(kubectl get sts $STATEFULSET -n $NAMESPACE -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}')
 
for i in $(seq 0 $(($REPLICAS - 1))); do
    POD="${STATEFULSET}-${i}"
    echo "Upgrading $POD..."
 
    # 1. Drain: Coordinator stops assigning new segments to this Historical
    # (Queries to existing segments still served by replicas on other nodes)
 
    # 2. Delete pod — K8s recreates with updated image
    kubectl delete pod $POD -n $NAMESPACE
 
    # 3. Wait for pod to be ready (segment download + health check)
    kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod/$POD -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=600s
 
    # 4. Verify: check segment count is recovering
    until [ $(curl -s http://$POD:8083/druid/historical/v1/loadstatus | jq '.loaded') -gt 0 ]; do
        sleep 10
    done
 
    echo "$POD upgraded and serving segments."
    sleep 30  # Grace period before next pod
done
 
echo "All Historicals upgraded."
⚠️ Common Pitfall Upgrading all Historicals simultaneously. If you do kubectl rollout restart on the Historical StatefulSet with maxUnavailable > 1, multiple Historicals go down at once. With replication=2, losing 2 Historicals that share a segment replica means that segment becomes temporarily unavailable → query failures. Always use maxUnavailable: 1 and wait for full segment reload between pods.

29.6 RPO / RTO Planning Matrix

Failure ScenarioRPO (Data Loss)RTO (Downtime)Recovery Action
Single Historical crash00 (if replication ≥ 2)Auto: Broker routes to replica. Coordinator re-replicates.
Single Broker crash00Auto: Load balancer routes to other Brokers.
MiddleManager crashUncommitted Kafka window~30s (Supervisor restarts task)Auto: Task restarts from last committed Kafka offset.
ZooKeeper leader loss05–30s (re-election)Auto: ZK quorum re-elects. Druid reconnects.
Metadata store crash0 (if Multi-AZ)1–2 min (RDS failover)Auto: RDS promotes standby. Druid reconnects.
Full AZ outage0 (if Multi-AZ)5–15 minSemi-auto: RDS fails over. K8s reschedules pods to other AZ.
Full region outageSeconds–Minutes30 min – 2 hoursManual: DNS failover → DR cluster. Promote RDS replica.
Accidental datasource drop0 (segments in S3)10–60 minManual: Restore metadata. Coordinator reloads from S3.
Metadata corruptionPITR restore age15–45 minManual: RDS PITR to pre-corruption point.
🎯 Interview Tip "What's the RPO for a Druid cluster?" — For query data, RPO is effectively 0 because all segments are in S3 (11 nines durability). The real RPO question is about the metadata store: with RDS Multi-AZ, RPO is 0 (synchronous replication). With only automated snapshots, RPO is up to 24 hours. With PITR enabled, RPO is ~5 minutes. The RTO depends on data volume: infrastructure recovery takes 15–30 minutes, but Historical segment download is proportional to total data size — 5 TB at 1 Gbps = ~1 hour per Historical.

29.7 Disaster Recovery Testing

A DR plan that hasn't been tested is not a plan — it's a hope. Schedule quarterly DR drills:

DrillFrequencyProcedureSuccess Criteria
Metadata restore test Monthly Restore latest pg_dump to a test RDS instance. Connect a test Druid cluster. Coordinator discovers all segments. Test query succeeds.
Historical failure test Monthly Kill 1 Historical pod. Verify queries still succeed via replica. Zero query failures during pod restart + segment reload.
Rolling upgrade rehearsal Before each upgrade Upgrade staging cluster first. Run full regression test suite. All queries return same results. No error rate increase.
Full region failover Quarterly Simulate primary region outage. Failover DNS to DR region. Validate. DR cluster serves queries within RTO. Kafka supervisors resume.
Chaos engineering Monthly Use Chaos Mesh / Litmus to inject: pod kills, network partitions, disk pressure. Cluster self-heals within defined RTO for each failure type.
Chaos Mesh — Historical Pod Kill ExperimentYAML
apiVersion: chaos-mesh.org/v1alpha1
kind: PodChaos
metadata:
  name: druid-historical-kill
  namespace: druid
spec:
  action: pod-kill
  mode: one                      # Kill one random Historical
  selector:
    labelSelectors:
      "app": "druid-historical"
  scheduler:
    cron: "0 2 * * 3"           # Every Wednesday at 2 AM
  duration: "60s"                # Pod stays dead for 60s
⚙️ Engineering Insight The most common DR failure in practice is not infrastructure — it's stale config. Your DR Helm chart hasn't been updated in 6 months, the new extensions aren't included, the metadata URI points to a deleted RDS instance, and the S3 bucket policy was changed. Treat your DR infrastructure as code and test it quarterly. Every Druid config change should be mirrored to the DR environment via the same CI/CD pipeline.

29.8 Kafka Offset Recovery and Data Gap Handling

After a DR event, Kafka supervisors resume from their last committed offset. But if Kafka retention has expired for uncommitted offsets, there's a data gap — events between the last committed offset and the earliest available Kafka offset are lost from Druid's perspective.

Handling Data Gaps After DR Recoverybash
# 1. Check supervisor status — what offset did it resume from?
curl -s http://overlord:8090/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor/user_events/status | jq .
 
# Look for:
# "latestOffsets": {"0": 15234567, "1": 15234890}
# "minimumLag": {"0": 0, "1": 0}   ← 0 lag = caught up
 
# 2. Check for data gaps — compare Druid data with Kafka offsets
# If Kafka retention was 7 days but DR took 2 days:
# → Offsets committed before retention window are gone
# → Druid has a gap for those offsets
 
# 3. Backfill the gap from the data lake
# If the same data flows through both Kafka → Druid AND Kafka → Lake:
# Use REPLACE INTO to backfill from the lake for the gap interval
 
# Example: gap is 2024-03-28 to 2024-03-30
curl -X POST http://router:8888/druid/v2/sql/task \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "query": "REPLACE INTO \"user_events\" OVERWRITE WHERE __time >= TIMESTAMP '\''2024-03-28'\'' AND __time < TIMESTAMP '\''2024-03-30'\'' SELECT ... FROM TABLE(EXTERN(...)) ...",
    "context": {"maxNumTasks": 8}
  }'
⚠️ Common Pitfall Kafka retention too short for DR scenarios. If your Kafka retention is 7 days but a full region DR recovery takes 2 days, you only have a 5-day safety margin. If the DR event lasts longer than Kafka retention, uncommitted data is permanently lost from Kafka (though it may exist in the data lake). Set Kafka retention to at least 3× your worst-case DR RTO to ensure supervisors can always resume without data gaps.

Section 29 — Key Takeaways

AreaKey Recommendation
Metadata backupRDS Multi-AZ (RPO=0, RTO=2min). Plus: PITR enabled + pg_dump to S3 every 6h.
Deep storageS3 is already 11-nines durable. Enable CRR for multi-region. No extra backup needed.
Full cluster recoveryRestore metadata → deploy cluster → Coordinator auto-assigns → Historicals download.
Multi-regionActive-Passive (cheapest), Active-Active (best RPO/RTO), Shared S3 (cost-optimized).
Rolling upgradesOrder: Historical → MM → Broker → Coordinator → Overlord. maxUnavailable=1.
DR testingMonthly: metadata restore + Historical kill. Quarterly: full region failover + chaos.
Kafka gapsSet retention ≥ 3× worst-case RTO. Backfill gaps from data lake via REPLACE INTO.
Part 11 — Section 30

Cost-Based Query Optimization and EXPLAIN PLAN

Calcite SQL planner internals, native query type selection, EXPLAIN PLAN analysis, query rewrite patterns, and a systematic optimization playbook for sub-second analytics.

30 — Cost-Based Query Optimization and EXPLAIN PLAN

30.1 How Druid Translates SQL — The Calcite Pipeline

Every Druid SQL query passes through Apache Calcite, a rule-based query planner. Unlike traditional databases that use cost-based optimization with table statistics, Druid's planner applies a fixed set of rewrite rules to convert SQL into the most efficient native query type. Understanding this pipeline is the key to writing fast Druid queries — the same logical query can execute as a sub-second TopN or a multi-second GroupBy depending on how you write it.

Figure 30.1 — Calcite translates SQL through parse → validate → rule-based optimize → native query type selection

30.2 Query Type Selection Rules — When Does Druid Choose What?

SQL PatternNative TypeTypical LatencyDetection Rule
SELECT SUM(x) WHERE __time ...
No GROUP BY or only TIME_FLOOR(__time)		 Timeseries 10–50 ms No non-time dimensions in GROUP BY. Pure time-bucketed aggregation.
SELECT dim, SUM(x) GROUP BY dim ORDER BY SUM(x) DESC LIMIT N
Exactly 1 dimension + ORDER BY metric + LIMIT		 TopN 50–200 ms Single dimension groupBy + ordering by a metric + threshold (LIMIT). Approximate by default.
SELECT dim1, dim2, SUM(x) GROUP BY dim1, dim2
2+ dimensions or no ORDER BY or no LIMIT		 GroupBy v2 100–2000 ms Multi-dimension groupBy. Most flexible but uses merge buffers (off-heap).
SELECT * FROM ds WHERE ... LIMIT 1000
No aggregation		 Scan Variable No aggregation functions → raw row retrieval. LIMIT strongly recommended.
SELECT ... (window functions, complex subqueries, large JOINs) MSQ (multi-stage) 5–60 seconds Triggers when scatter-gather cannot handle query (window funcs, JOINs).
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Forcing TopN over GroupBy Druid's Calcite planner automatically converts GROUP BY dim ORDER BY metric DESC LIMIT N on a single dimension to a native TopN query (much faster than GroupBy). But if you GROUP BY two dimensions (e.g., country, platform), Druid falls back to GroupBy even with LIMIT. Workaround: concatenate dimensions at ingestion time ("country_platform" as a single dimension), or use nested subqueries to TopN on the outer dimension first, then filter the inner dimension.

30.3 EXPLAIN PLAN — Reading and Interpreting Output

EXPLAIN PLAN FOR shows the native query JSON that Druid will execute for any SQL query. This is the single most important debugging tool for query performance — it reveals the native query type, filter pushdown, segment pruning, and column projection.

EXPLAIN PLAN — Timeseries DetectionSQL
-- ✅ This query triggers TIMESERIES (fastest)
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
SELECT
    TIME_FLOOR(__time, 'PT1H') AS hour,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_revenue,
    SUM(event_count) AS total_events
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '24' HOUR
GROUP BY 1
 
-- Output excerpt (simplified):
-- {
--   "queryType": "timeseries",        ← FASTEST type
--   "dataSource": "user_events",
--   "intervals": ["2024-03-30T.../2024-03-31T..."],
--   "granularity": {"type": "period", "period": "PT1H"},
--   "aggregations": [
--     {"type": "longSum", "name": "total_revenue", "fieldName": "revenue_sum"},
--     {"type": "longSum", "name": "total_events", "fieldName": "event_count"}
--   ]
-- }
EXPLAIN PLAN — TopN DetectionSQL
-- ✅ This query triggers TOPN (fast: 1 dim + ORDER BY + LIMIT)
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
SELECT
    country,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_revenue
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 20
 
-- Output excerpt:
-- {
--   "queryType": "topN",               ← Fast single-dim ranked query
--   "dimension": "country",
--   "metric": {"type": "numeric", "metric": "total_revenue"},
--   "threshold": 20,
--   "filter": null                     ← No additional filter
-- }
EXPLAIN PLAN — GroupBy Fallback (slower)SQL
-- ⚠ This triggers GROUPBY (slower: 2 dimensions)
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
SELECT
    country,
    platform,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_revenue
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country, platform
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 50
 
-- Output excerpt:
-- {
--   "queryType": "groupBy",            ← Falls back to GroupBy
--   "dimensions": ["country", "platform"],
--   "limitSpec": {"limit": 50, "columns": [...]},
--   "context": {"groupByStrategy": "v2"}
-- }

EXPLAIN PLAN Checklist — What to Look For

FieldWhat to CheckAction if Wrong
queryTypeIs it topN or timeseries when expected?If groupBy unexpectedly: reduce to 1 dim, add LIMIT+ORDER BY
intervalsIs the time range tight?If -146136543-..../146136543-... (infinite): add WHERE __time filter
filterAre WHERE clauses pushed down as bitmap filters?If filter is null but WHERE exists: check for non-pushable expressions
dimensionsOnly needed dims? No extra columns?Remove unnecessary GROUP BY columns
aggregationsUsing sketch approximations where possible?Replace COUNT(DISTINCT x) with APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT
context.vectorizeIs vectorized execution enabled?If false: check for non-vectorizable expressions

30.4 Query Rewrite Patterns — Slow → Fast

Pattern 1: Missing Time Filter → Full Scan

BAD → GOOD: Always add __time filterSQL
-- ❌ BAD: Scans ALL segments in datasource (could be years of data)
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum)
FROM "user_events"
GROUP BY country;
-- Scans: 10,000 segments | Time: 15+ seconds | Timeout risk: HIGH
 
-- ✅ GOOD: Prunes 99%+ of segments immediately
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum)
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country;
-- Scans: 7 segments (daily granularity) | Time: 80 ms

Pattern 2: Exact Distinct → Approximate

BAD → GOOD: Use sketch approximationSQL
-- ❌ BAD: Exact COUNT DISTINCT — requires dedup across all rows
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS unique_users
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY;
-- Forces exact dedup: materializes all user_ids in memory. OOM risk on 100M+ users.
 
-- ✅ GOOD: Approximate with HLL sketch (~2% error, constant memory)
SELECT APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users_sketch) AS unique_users
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' DAY;
-- Uses pre-built HLL sketch: O(1) memory, sub-second. Ideal for dashboards.

Pattern 3: GroupBy → TopN Conversion

BAD → GOOD: Trigger TopN instead of GroupBySQL
-- ⚠ SLOW: GroupBy on single dimension WITHOUT ORDER BY + LIMIT
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country;
-- → Druid uses GroupBy (slower, needs merge buffers)
 
-- ✅ FAST: Same query but Druid uses TopN (3-5× faster)
SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY rev DESC
LIMIT 1000;
-- → Druid uses TopN (heap-based top-K, no merge buffers needed)
-- Trick: even LIMIT 1000 triggers TopN if there's only 1 GROUP BY dim

Pattern 4: Subquery → Filtered Aggregation

BAD → GOOD: Replace subqueries with filtered aggregationSQL
-- ❌ BAD: Two separate queries merged via subquery (multi-stage)
SELECT
    a.country,
    a.mobile_rev,
    b.desktop_rev
FROM
    (SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS mobile_rev
     FROM "user_events" WHERE platform='mobile' GROUP BY country) a
JOIN
    (SELECT country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS desktop_rev
     FROM "user_events" WHERE platform='desktop' GROUP BY country) b
ON a.country = b.country;
-- → Triggers MSQ multi-stage query. Slow (5–30 seconds).
 
-- ✅ GOOD: Single GroupBy with filtered aggregations (scatter-gather)
SELECT
    country,
    SUM(revenue_sum) FILTER (WHERE platform = 'mobile')  AS mobile_rev,
    SUM(revenue_sum) FILTER (WHERE platform = 'desktop') AS desktop_rev
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country;
-- → Single GroupBy with filtered aggregators. 100–500 ms. One pass.

Pattern 5: HAVING on Sketch → Post-Aggregator

Efficient HAVING with SketchesSQL
-- Find countries with > 10K unique users in the last week
SELECT
    country,
    APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users_sketch) AS dau
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7' DAY
GROUP BY country
HAVING APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(unique_users_sketch) > 10000
ORDER BY dau DESC
-- HAVING is applied on Broker AFTER merge — efficient because sketch
-- estimation is O(1) per group. No extra scan needed.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Using CASE WHEN instead of FILTER. Both SUM(CASE WHEN platform='mobile' THEN revenue END) and SUM(revenue) FILTER (WHERE platform='mobile') produce the same result, but Druid optimizes the FILTER syntax into a native filtered aggregator that uses bitmap indexes for row selection — significantly faster than the CASE WHEN approach which evaluates an expression per row.

30.5 Query Context Parameters for Performance

ParameterDefaultRecommendedEffect
timeout300000 (5 min)10000–30000Kill queries that exceed this — prevents runaway queries from consuming resources
priority0-1 (batch), 0 (normal), 1 (critical)Higher priority queries get scheduled first in query lanes
useCachetruetrueCheck Broker segment-level cache before scanning
useResultLevelCachefalsetrue (for dashboards)Cache the complete query result on Broker — instant for repeated queries
vectorize"true""true"Enable vectorized engine. Use "force" to error on non-vectorizable queries.
vectorSize512512–1024Rows per vectorized batch. 1024 can help on very wide aggregations.
maxScatterGatherBytes~150 MBTune per workloadMax bytes Broker accepts from scatter-gather — prevents OOM on wide queries
groupByStrategy"v2""v2"Never use "v1" — deprecated and slower.
lanenull"interactive" / "batch"Assign query to a resource lane for isolation (see Section 10A).
sqlTimeZone"UTC"Set per userTIME_FLOOR bucketing uses this timezone. Affects dashboard aggregations.
Optimal Context for Dashboard QueriesJSON
{
  "query": "SELECT ...",
  "context": {
    "timeout": 10000,                   // 10s max — kill slow queries fast
    "priority": 1,                      // High priority for dashboards
    "lane": "interactive",              // Dedicated resource lane
    "useCache": true,                   // Check segment cache
    "useResultLevelCache": true,        // Cache full result
    "populateCache": true,              // Write to cache for next refresh
    "populateResultLevelCache": true,
    "vectorize": "true",               // Vectorized execution
    "skipEmptyBuckets": true            // Skip time buckets with no data
  }
}

30.6 Query Optimization Decision Tree

Figure 30.2 — Decision tree: time filter → aggregation check → dimension count → native query type selection
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you optimize a slow Druid query?" — Systematic approach: (1) EXPLAIN PLAN FOR — check native query type. (2) Ensure __time filter is present and tight. (3) If GroupBy: can it be rewritten as TopN (single dim + ORDER BY + LIMIT)? (4) Replace COUNT(DISTINCT) with APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT. (5) Replace subquery JOINs with filtered aggregations. (6) Check segment count — if >100, time filter too wide or granularity too fine. (7) Check per-Historical latency — straggler node or GC issue? (8) Context parameters: enable result-level cache, set timeout, assign query lane.

30.7 Calcite Rule Internals — What Happens Under the Hood

Calcite RuleWhat It DoesPerformance Impact
DruidQueryRuleConverts LogicalAggregate + LogicalFilter → DruidQuery with native aggregationEnables push-down of entire aggregation to Druid nodes
TopN RecognitionDetects 1-dim GROUP BY + ORDER BY metric + LIMIT → converts to TopN3–5× faster than GroupBy for single-dimension ranked queries
Timeseries RecognitionDetects GROUP BY TIME_FLOOR only → converts to TimeseriesFastest query type — no hash table, no merge buffers
Filter PushdownMoves WHERE clauses from Calcite plan into Druid native filterEnables bitmap index evaluation — zero row scanning
Projection PruningReads only columns referenced in SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BYReduces I/O — skip unreferenced column files in segments
Sort EliminationRemoves unnecessary ORDER BY when result ordering is irrelevantEliminates sort step on Broker merge
Subquery FlatteningMerges simple nested subqueries into a single query stageAvoids multi-stage MSQ execution for simple patterns
Join PushdownPushes small-table JOINs to broadcast hash join on HistoricalsAvoids MSQ shuffle for small dimension table JOINs
⚙️ Engineering Insight — When Calcite Gets It Wrong Calcite's rule-based optimizer doesn't always choose the optimal plan because it lacks table statistics (row counts, cardinality estimates). Common misfires:
  • TopN not triggered: Adding HAVING to a single-dim query prevents TopN recognition — Druid falls back to GroupBy. Workaround: use a subquery (SELECT * FROM (topN query) WHERE condition).
