  • Unnecessary MSQ: Complex CASE expressions or certain function combinations trigger MSQ when scatter-gather would suffice. Simplify expressions or use virtual columns at ingestion.
  • Join broadcast failure: Druid tries to broadcast the right side of a JOIN. If the right side is large (>100 MB), it OOMs the Broker. Always put the smaller table on the right side of the JOIN.
When in doubt, use EXPLAIN PLAN FOR and compare the native query type with your expectation. If Druid chose GroupBy when you expected TopN, restructure the SQL.

30.8 Performance Anti-Patterns — What Never to Do

Anti-PatternWhy It's SlowFix
SELECT * FROM ds (no LIMIT) Returns every row from every segment — unbounded scan Always add LIMIT. Use Scan query only for debugging.
WHERE dim LIKE '%substring%' Leading wildcard can't use bitmap index — full dictionary scan Use WHERE dim LIKE 'prefix%' (uses dictionary range). Or add a derived dimension for the pattern.
GROUP BY high_cardinality_id Creates millions of groups → blows merge buffer → spills to disk Use sketches for counting. Hash/bucket the ID. Don't GROUP BY UUIDs.
JOINing two large Druid datasources Triggers MSQ shuffle — minutes not seconds Denormalize at ingestion. Use Theta Sketch set operations instead.
Window functions on dashboard queries Triggers MSQ — 5–30 seconds vs 100–500ms scatter-gather Pre-compute running totals at ingestion or use post-processing in the app.
COUNT(DISTINCT x) on high-cardinality Exact dedup requires materializing all values in memory Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT with HLL sketch (~2% error, O(1) memory).
Missing __time filter Scans all segments — years of data Every query must have WHERE __time >= .... No exceptions.
ORDER BY without LIMIT Broker must sort entire result set in memory — no TopN optimization Always pair ORDER BY with LIMIT. Even LIMIT 10000 enables TopN.

Section 30 — Key Takeaways

TopicKey Point
Calcite pipelineSQL → Parse → Validate → Rule-based optimize → Native query type selection (no cost-based)
Query type priorityTimeseries > TopN > GroupBy > Scan > MSQ. Write SQL to trigger the fastest type.
EXPLAIN PLANCheck: queryType, intervals, filter pushdown, dimension count, vectorize flag.
Top rewritesAdd __time filter. Add ORDER BY + LIMIT for TopN. Use FILTER() not subquery JOIN. Use sketches.
Context paramstimeout=10s, priority=1, useResultLevelCache=true, lane="interactive" for dashboards.
Anti-patternsNever: SELECT *, LIKE '%x%', GROUP BY UUID, JOIN large tables, window funcs on dashboards.
Part 12 — Section 31

How to Setup a Druid Cluster

Complete step-by-step guide — from local single-node quickstart to production-grade Kubernetes deployment with Helm, including bare-metal, Docker Compose, and cloud-native patterns.

31 — How to Setup a Druid Cluster

31.1 Deployment Options Overview

Druid can be deployed in four progressively complex modes. Choose based on your stage: development → staging → production.

ModeUse CaseNodesHATime to Setup
1. Local QuickstartLearning, PoC, local dev1 (all-in-one JVM)None5 minutes
2. Docker ComposeLocal multi-service, CI/CD testing1 host, 7+ containersNone10 minutes
3. Bare-Metal / VMOn-prem production, dedicated hardware10–100+ VMsFull1–3 days
4. Kubernetes (Helm)Cloud-native productionK8s clusterFull2–4 hours
Figure 31.1 — Four deployment modes from learning to cloud production

31.2 Prerequisites — What You Need Before Starting

DependencyMinimumProductionPurpose
JavaJDK 11JDK 17 or 21 (for ZGC)All Druid services are JVM processes
ZooKeeper3.5+3.8+ (or K8s extensions)Service discovery, leader election, coordination
Metadata StoreDerby (dev only)PostgreSQL 13+ or MySQL 8+Segment metadata, task state, rules
Deep StorageLocal filesystemS3 / GCS / Azure Blob / HDFSDurable segment storage (source of truth)
Kafka2.8+ (optional)3.0+ recommendedReal-time streaming ingestion
OSAny (Java-based)Linux (Ubuntu 22.04 / Amazon Linux 2023)Memory-mapped I/O, page cache tuning
RAM8 GB (quickstart)64–256 GB per HistoricalJVM heap + page cache for segments

31.3 Option 1 — Local Quickstart (Single Machine, 5 Minutes)

Download and Start Druid — Nano Quickstartbash
# ═══ Step 1: Download latest Druid release ═══
curl -O https://dlcdn.apache.org/druid/30.0.1/apache-druid-30.0.1-bin.tar.gz
tar -xzf apache-druid-30.0.1-bin.tar.gz
cd apache-druid-30.0.1
 
# ═══ Step 2: Verify Java ═══
java -version
# Must be JDK 11 or higher
 
# ═══ Step 3: Start all services (single JVM, embedded ZK + Derby) ═══
./bin/start-druid
 
# Output:
# Starting ZooKeeper...
# Starting Coordinator-Overlord...
# Starting Broker...
# Starting Historical...
# Starting MiddleManager...
# Starting Router...
 
# ═══ Step 4: Open Druid Console ═══
# Navigate to: http://localhost:8888
# Console shows: Datasources, Segments, Ingestion, Query tabs
 
# ═══ Step 5: Load sample data ═══
# Console → "Load data" → "Example data" → "Wikipedia edits"
# Or via API:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8888/druid/indexer/v1/task \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @quickstart/tutorial/wikipedia-index.json
 
# ═══ Step 6: Query it ═══
curl -X POST http://localhost:8888/druid/v2/sql \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"SELECT channel, COUNT(*) AS edits FROM wikipedia GROUP BY channel ORDER BY edits DESC LIMIT 10"}'
 
# ═══ Stop ═══
./bin/stop-druid
⚠️ Common Pitfall The quickstart uses Derby (embedded DB) as the metadata store and local filesystem as deep storage. These are not production-safe — Derby doesn't support concurrent connections, local storage doesn't survive disk failure. Never use this in production. It's strictly for learning and local development.

31.4 Option 2 — Docker Compose (Multi-Service, Local)

docker-compose.yml — Full Druid StackYAML
version: "3.8"
 
services:
 
  # ── External Dependencies ──
  zookeeper:
    image: zookeeper:3.8
    ports: ["2181:2181"]
    environment:
      ZOO_MY_ID: 1
 
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: druid
      POSTGRES_USER: druid
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: druid_password
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
 
  # ── Druid Master Services ──
  coordinator:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: coordinator
    ports: ["8081:8081"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres]
    volumes:
      - ./conf/druid/coordinator:/opt/druid/conf/druid/coordinator
      - druid_shared:/opt/shared
    environment: &druid-env
      DRUID_LOG4J: '<?xml version="1.0"?><Configuration></Configuration>'
      druid_metadata_storage_type: postgresql
      druid_metadata_storage_connector_connectURI: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/druid
      druid_metadata_storage_connector_user: druid
      druid_metadata_storage_connector_password: druid_password
      druid_zk_service_host: zookeeper:2181
      druid_storage_type: local
      druid_storage_storageDirectory: /opt/shared/segments
      druid_indexer_logs_type: file
      druid_indexer_logs_directory: /opt/shared/indexing-logs
 
  overlord:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: overlord
    ports: ["8090:8090"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres]
    volumes: [druid_shared:/opt/shared]
    environment: *druid-env
 
  # ── Druid Data Services ──
  historical:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: historical
    ports: ["8083:8083"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres, coordinator]
    volumes:
      - druid_shared:/opt/shared
      - historical_cache:/opt/druid/var/druid/segment-cache
    environment: *druid-env
 
  middlemanager:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: middleManager
    ports: ["8091:8091", "8100-8105:8100-8105"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres, overlord]
    volumes: [druid_shared:/opt/shared]
    environment: *druid-env
 
  # ── Druid Query Services ──
  broker:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: broker
    ports: ["8082:8082"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres, historical]
    environment: *druid-env
 
  router:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: router
    ports: ["8888:8888"]        # ← Console UI
    depends_on: [broker]
    environment: *druid-env
 
volumes:
  postgres_data:
  druid_shared:
  historical_cache:
Start and Validate Docker Compose Clusterbash
# Start all services
docker compose up -d
 
# Watch logs (wait for all services to be ready)
docker compose logs -f coordinator | grep "Started"
 
# Verify all services healthy
curl -s http://localhost:8888/status/health     # Router
curl -s http://localhost:8082/status/health     # Broker
curl -s http://localhost:8081/status/health     # Coordinator
 
# Open console: http://localhost:8888
 
# Stop
docker compose down
⚙️ Engineering Insight Docker Compose is ideal for CI/CD integration testing. Run your ingestion spec → query assertions → teardown in a GitHub Actions or Jenkins pipeline. The entire Druid stack starts in ~60 seconds. Use health check polling before running tests.

31.5 Option 3 — Bare-Metal / VM Production Cluster

Recommended Node Layout

RoleCountInstance Type (AWS)Key Config
Master (Coordinator + Overlord)2m5.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 32 GB)asOverlord.enabled=true, 8 GB heap
Query (Broker + Router)3m5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 64 GB)25 GB heap, 12 GB direct, behind ALB
Data (Historical)10+r5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 128 GB)25 GB heap, 12 GB direct, 500 GB SSD cache
Ingestion (MiddleManager)5+m5.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 32 GB)4 Peon slots, 2 GB heap per Peon
ZooKeeper3t3.medium (2 vCPU, 4 GB)Dedicated nodes, SSD storage
Metadata (RDS)1 (Multi-AZ)db.r5.largePostgreSQL 15, encrypted, automated backups
common.runtime.properties — Shared Across All Nodesproperties
# ═══ Extensions ═══
druid.extensions.loadList=[
  "druid-kafka-indexing-service",
  "druid-s3-extensions",
  "druid-datasketches",
  "druid-basic-security",
  "prometheus-emitter"
]
 
# ═══ ZooKeeper ═══
druid.zk.service.host=zk-1:2181,zk-2:2181,zk-3:2181
druid.zk.paths.base=/druid
 
# ═══ Metadata Store (PostgreSQL on RDS) ═══
druid.metadata.storage.type=postgresql
druid.metadata.storage.connector.connectURI=jdbc:postgresql://druid-meta.xxxxx.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/druid
druid.metadata.storage.connector.user=druid_admin
druid.metadata.storage.connector.password=${METADATA_PASSWORD}
 
# ═══ Deep Storage (S3) ═══
druid.storage.type=s3
druid.storage.bucket=my-druid-segments
druid.storage.baseKey=druid/segments
druid.s3.accessKey=${AWS_ACCESS_KEY}
druid.s3.secretKey=${AWS_SECRET_KEY}
 
# ═══ Indexer Logs (S3) ═══
druid.indexer.logs.type=s3
druid.indexer.logs.s3Bucket=my-druid-segments
druid.indexer.logs.s3Prefix=druid/indexing-logs
 
# ═══ Monitoring ═══
druid.emitter=prometheus
druid.emitter.prometheus.port=9090
historical/runtime.properties — Per-Node Configproperties
druid.service=druid/historical
druid.plaintextPort=8083
 
# ═══ JVM (set in jvm.config) ═══
# -Xms25g -Xmx25g
# -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g
# -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational
 
# ═══ Processing ═══
druid.processing.buffer.sizeBytes=524288000
druid.processing.numThreads=15
druid.processing.numMergeBuffers=4
 
# ═══ Segment Cache (SSD) ═══
druid.segmentCache.locations=[{"path":"/mnt/ssd/druid/segments","maxSize":500000000000}]
druid.server.maxSize=500000000000
 
# ═══ Cache ═══
druid.historical.cache.useCache=true
druid.historical.cache.populateCache=true
druid.cache.type=caffeine
druid.cache.sizeInBytes=2147483648
historical/jvm.configconfig
-server
-Xms25g
-Xmx25g
-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g
-XX:+UseZGC
-XX:+ZGenerational
-Duser.timezone=UTC
-Dfile.encoding=UTF-8
-Djava.io.tmpdir=var/tmp
-Djava.util.logging.manager=org.apache.logging.log4j.jul.LogManager

31.6 Option 4 — Kubernetes with Helm (Production Recommended)

Figure 31.2 — K8s architecture: query/data/master namespaces + external RDS, S3, Kafka
Helm Install — Step by Stepbash
# ═══ Step 1: Add Druid Helm repo ═══
helm repo add druid https://apache.github.io/druid
helm repo update
 
# ═══ Step 2: Create namespace ═══
kubectl create namespace druid
 
# ═══ Step 3: Create secrets ═══
kubectl create secret generic druid-metadata \
  --namespace druid \
  --from-literal=password='your_rds_password'
 
kubectl create secret generic druid-s3 \
  --namespace druid \
  --from-literal=accessKey='AKIA...' \
  --from-literal=secretKey='xxxxx'
 
# ═══ Step 4: Install with custom values ═══
helm install druid-prod druid/druid \
  --namespace druid \
  --values values-production.yaml \
  --timeout 15m
 
# ═══ Step 5: Verify ═══
kubectl get pods -n druid -w
# Wait for all pods to be Running/Ready
 
kubectl port-forward svc/druid-prod-router 8888:8888 -n druid
# Console: http://localhost:8888
values-production.yaml — Helm ValuesYAML
# ═══ ZooKeeper ═══
zookeeper:
  enabled: true
  replicaCount: 3
  persistence:
    size: 20Gi
 
# ═══ Broker ═══
broker:
  replicaCount: 3
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "4", memory: "32Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "8", memory: "32Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms20g -Xmx20g -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=6g -XX:+UseZGC"
 
# ═══ Historical ═══
historical:
  replicaCount: 10
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "8", memory: "128Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "16", memory: "128Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms25g -Xmx25g -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational"
  persistence:
    enabled: true
    size: 500Gi
    storageClass: gp3-ssd
  env:
    - name: druid_processing_buffer_sizeBytes
      value: "524288000"
    - name: druid_processing_numThreads
      value: "15"
    - name: druid_processing_numMergeBuffers
      value: "4"
 
# ═══ MiddleManager ═══
middleManager:
  replicaCount: 5
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "4", memory: "32Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "8", memory: "32Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms1g -Xmx1g"
 
# ═══ Coordinator (with Overlord co-located) ═══
coordinator:
  replicaCount: 2
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "2", memory: "16Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms8g -Xmx8g"
  env:
    - name: druid_coordinator_asOverlord_enabled
      value: "true"
 
# ═══ Router ═══
router:
  replicaCount: 2
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "1", memory: "4Gi" }
 
# ═══ Common Config (all services) ═══
configVars:
  druid_metadata_storage_type: postgresql
  druid_metadata_storage_connector_connectURI: jdbc:postgresql://druid-meta.xxxxx.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/druid
  druid_storage_type: s3
  druid_storage_bucket: my-druid-segments
  druid_storage_baseKey: druid/segments
  druid_emitter: prometheus
  druid_emitter_prometheus_port: "9090"
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Historical Startup Probes New Historical pods need to download GBs of segments from S3 before serving queries. If your readiness probe fails before loading completes, K8s restarts the pod in a crash loop. Always use startupProbe with generous timeout (5+ minutes):

startupProbe: httpGet: /status/health, port: 8083, initialDelaySeconds: 120, periodSeconds: 10, failureThreshold: 30

31.7 Post-Setup Validation Checklist

Cluster Health Validation Scriptbash
#!/bin/bash — validate-druid-cluster.sh
 
ROUTER="http://druid-router:8888"
 
echo "=== 1. Service Health ==="
for svc in broker historical coordinator overlord router; do
  status=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" $ROUTER/proxy/$svc/status/health)
  echo "  $svc: $status"
done
 
echo "=== 2. Coordinator Leader ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/leader | jq .
 
echo "=== 3. Overlord Leader ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/indexer/v1/leader | jq .
 
echo "=== 4. Historical Nodes ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/servers?simple | jq '.[].host'
 
echo "=== 5. Test Ingestion (Wikipedia sample) ==="
curl -s -X POST $ROUTER/druid/indexer/v1/task \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @quickstart/tutorial/wikipedia-index.json | jq .task
 
echo "=== 6. Wait for ingestion (60s) ==="
sleep 60
 
echo "=== 7. Test Query ==="
curl -s -X POST $ROUTER/druid/v2/sql \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM wikipedia"}' | jq .
 
echo "=== 8. Segment Count ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/datasources/wikipedia | jq .segments.count
 
echo "=== ✅ Cluster Validation Complete ==="

Validation Checklist

CheckCommand / URLExpected
All services healthy/status/health on each servicetrue (HTTP 200)
Coordinator leader elected/druid/coordinator/v1/leaderReturns hostname
Overlord leader elected/druid/indexer/v1/leaderReturns hostname
Historicals registered/druid/coordinator/v1/servers?simpleLists all Historical hosts
Sample ingestion succeedsSubmit wikipedia-index taskTask completes with SUCCESS
Query returns dataSELECT COUNT(*) FROM wikipediaReturns row count > 0
Segments loaded/druid/coordinator/v1/loadqueueEmpty (all segments loaded)
Prometheus metrics:9090/metrics on any nodeReturns Prometheus text format
Console accessiblehttp://router:8888Druid Console UI loads

31.8 Setting Up Your First Kafka Ingestion

Submit Kafka Supervisor — Real-Time Streamingbash
# Submit a Kafka supervisor to start real-time ingestion
curl -X POST http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
  "type": "kafka",
  "spec": {
    "dataSchema": {
      "dataSource": "user_events",
      "timestampSpec": { "column": "event_time", "format": "iso" },
      "dimensionsSpec": {
        "dimensions": ["user_id", "event_type", "platform", "country"]
      },
      "metricsSpec": [
        { "type": "count", "name": "event_count" },
        { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "revenue_sum", "fieldName": "revenue" }
      ],
      "granularitySpec": {
        "segmentGranularity": "HOUR",
        "queryGranularity": "MINUTE",
        "rollup": true
      }
    },
    "ioConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "consumerProperties": {
        "bootstrap.servers": "kafka-broker-1:9092,kafka-broker-2:9092"
      },
      "topic": "user_events_prod",
      "taskCount": 4,
      "replicas": 2,
      "taskDuration": "PT1H"
    },
    "tuningConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "maxRowsPerSegment": 5000000,
      "maxRowsInMemory": 1000000
    }
  }
}'
 
# Check supervisor status
curl -s http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor/user_events/status | jq .
 
# Check Kafka lag
curl -s http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor/user_events/stats | jq .

31.9 OS-Level Tuning for Production

Linux Kernel Tuning for Druid Historicalsbash
# ═══ Maximize memory-mapped file performance ═══
# Druid Historicals rely heavily on mmap for segment files
 
# Increase max memory map areas (default 65536 is too low for large clusters)
echo "vm.max_map_count=262144" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
 
# Reduce swappiness — keep segment pages in RAM
echo "vm.swappiness=1" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
 
# Increase file descriptor limits (Druid opens many segment files)
echo "* soft nofile 65536" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
echo "* hard nofile 131072" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
 
# Increase max processes
echo "* soft nproc 65536" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
 
# Apply
sysctl -p
 
# ═══ Disable Transparent Huge Pages (THP) ═══
# THP causes unpredictable latency spikes with mmap-heavy workloads
echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag
 
# ═══ Verify ═══
sysctl vm.max_map_count
sysctl vm.swappiness
cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max
cat /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through setting up a production Druid cluster." — Key steps: (1) Provision external deps: RDS PostgreSQL (Multi-AZ), S3 bucket (SSE-KMS), ZooKeeper (3-node). (2) Deploy via Helm on K8s with separate node pools per component type. (3) Configure: extensions list, metadata URI, S3 creds, processing threads/buffers per node type. (4) OS tuning: max_map_count=262144, swappiness=1, disable THP, high file descriptor limits. (5) Validate: health checks, sample ingestion, query test. (6) Set up monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana), alerting, and Kafka supervisor for real-time data. The most common mistake: undersizing Historical page cache by over-allocating JVM heap.

Section 31 — Key Takeaways

TopicKey Point
Local dev./bin/start-druid — all-in-one JVM, 5 min. Derby + local storage. Never for production.
Docker Compose7 containers (ZK, Postgres, Coord, Overlord, Historical, MM, Broker, Router). Good for CI/CD.
Bare-metalSeparate nodes per role. common.runtime.properties shared. Per-node JVM config for heap/direct.
KubernetesHelm chart. StatefulSets for Historical/MM/ZK. Deployments for Broker/Router/Coordinator.
External depsRDS Multi-AZ (metadata), S3 SSE-KMS (deep storage), MSK/Kafka (ingestion). Never run MySQL on K8s.
OS tuningmax_map_count=262144, swappiness=1, disable THP, file descriptors 65536+.
ValidationHealth check all services → sample ingest → query → check segments loaded → Prometheus metrics.
Part 12 — Section 31

How to Setup a Druid Cluster

Complete step-by-step guide — from local single-node quickstart to production-grade Kubernetes deployment with Helm, including bare-metal, Docker Compose, and cloud-native patterns.

31 — How to Setup a Druid Cluster

31.1 Deployment Options Overview

Druid can be deployed in four progressively complex modes. Choose based on your stage: development → staging → production.

ModeUse CaseNodesHATime to Setup
1. Local QuickstartLearning, PoC, local dev1 (all-in-one JVM)None5 minutes
2. Docker ComposeLocal multi-service, CI/CD testing1 host, 7+ containersNone10 minutes
3. Bare-Metal / VMOn-prem production, dedicated hardware10–100+ VMsFull1–3 days
4. Kubernetes (Helm)Cloud-native productionK8s clusterFull2–4 hours
Figure 31.1 — Four deployment modes from learning to cloud production

31.2 Prerequisites — What You Need Before Starting

DependencyMinimumProductionPurpose
JavaJDK 11JDK 17 or 21 (for ZGC)All Druid services are JVM processes
ZooKeeper3.5+3.8+ (or K8s extensions)Service discovery, leader election, coordination
Metadata StoreDerby (dev only)PostgreSQL 13+ or MySQL 8+Segment metadata, task state, rules
Deep StorageLocal filesystemS3 / GCS / Azure Blob / HDFSDurable segment storage (source of truth)
Kafka2.8+ (optional)3.0+ recommendedReal-time streaming ingestion
OSAny (Java-based)Linux (Ubuntu 22.04 / Amazon Linux 2023)Memory-mapped I/O, page cache tuning
RAM8 GB (quickstart)64–256 GB per HistoricalJVM heap + page cache for segments

31.3 Option 1 — Local Quickstart (Single Machine, 5 Minutes)

Download and Start Druid — Nano Quickstartbash
# ═══ Step 1: Download latest Druid release ═══
curl -O https://dlcdn.apache.org/druid/30.0.1/apache-druid-30.0.1-bin.tar.gz
tar -xzf apache-druid-30.0.1-bin.tar.gz
cd apache-druid-30.0.1
 
# ═══ Step 2: Verify Java ═══
java -version
# Must be JDK 11 or higher
 
# ═══ Step 3: Start all services (single JVM, embedded ZK + Derby) ═══
./bin/start-druid
 
# Output:
# Starting ZooKeeper...
# Starting Coordinator-Overlord...
# Starting Broker...
# Starting Historical...
# Starting MiddleManager...
# Starting Router...
 
# ═══ Step 4: Open Druid Console ═══
# Navigate to: http://localhost:8888
# Console shows: Datasources, Segments, Ingestion, Query tabs
 
# ═══ Step 5: Load sample data ═══
# Console → "Load data" → "Example data" → "Wikipedia edits"
# Or via API:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8888/druid/indexer/v1/task \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @quickstart/tutorial/wikipedia-index.json
 
# ═══ Step 6: Query it ═══
curl -X POST http://localhost:8888/druid/v2/sql \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"SELECT channel, COUNT(*) AS edits FROM wikipedia GROUP BY channel ORDER BY edits DESC LIMIT 10"}'
 
# ═══ Stop ═══
./bin/stop-druid
⚠️ Common Pitfall The quickstart uses Derby (embedded DB) as the metadata store and local filesystem as deep storage. These are not production-safe — Derby doesn't support concurrent connections, local storage doesn't survive disk failure. Never use this in production. It's strictly for learning and local development.

31.4 Option 2 — Docker Compose (Multi-Service, Local)

docker-compose.yml — Full Druid StackYAML
version: "3.8"
 
services:
 
  # ── External Dependencies ──
  zookeeper:
    image: zookeeper:3.8
    ports: ["2181:2181"]
    environment:
      ZOO_MY_ID: 1
 
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: druid
      POSTGRES_USER: druid
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: druid_password
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
 
  # ── Druid Master Services ──
  coordinator:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: coordinator
    ports: ["8081:8081"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres]
    volumes:
      - ./conf/druid/coordinator:/opt/druid/conf/druid/coordinator
      - druid_shared:/opt/shared
    environment: &druid-env
      DRUID_LOG4J: '<?xml version="1.0"?><Configuration></Configuration>'
      druid_metadata_storage_type: postgresql
      druid_metadata_storage_connector_connectURI: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/druid
      druid_metadata_storage_connector_user: druid
      druid_metadata_storage_connector_password: druid_password
      druid_zk_service_host: zookeeper:2181
      druid_storage_type: local
      druid_storage_storageDirectory: /opt/shared/segments
      druid_indexer_logs_type: file
      druid_indexer_logs_directory: /opt/shared/indexing-logs
 
  overlord:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: overlord
    ports: ["8090:8090"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres]
    volumes: [druid_shared:/opt/shared]
    environment: *druid-env
 
  # ── Druid Data Services ──
  historical:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: historical
    ports: ["8083:8083"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres, coordinator]
    volumes:
      - druid_shared:/opt/shared
      - historical_cache:/opt/druid/var/druid/segment-cache
    environment: *druid-env
 
  middlemanager:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: middleManager
    ports: ["8091:8091", "8100-8105:8100-8105"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres, overlord]
    volumes: [druid_shared:/opt/shared]
    environment: *druid-env
 
  # ── Druid Query Services ──
  broker:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: broker
    ports: ["8082:8082"]
    depends_on: [zookeeper, postgres, historical]
    environment: *druid-env
 
  router:
    image: apache/druid:30.0.1
    command: router
    ports: ["8888:8888"]        # ← Console UI
    depends_on: [broker]
    environment: *druid-env
 
volumes:
  postgres_data:
  druid_shared:
  historical_cache:
Start and Validate Docker Compose Clusterbash
# Start all services
docker compose up -d
 
# Watch logs (wait for all services to be ready)
docker compose logs -f coordinator | grep "Started"
 
# Verify all services healthy
curl -s http://localhost:8888/status/health     # Router
curl -s http://localhost:8082/status/health     # Broker
curl -s http://localhost:8081/status/health     # Coordinator
 
# Open console: http://localhost:8888
 
# Stop
docker compose down
⚙️ Engineering Insight Docker Compose is ideal for CI/CD integration testing. Run your ingestion spec → query assertions → teardown in a GitHub Actions or Jenkins pipeline. The entire Druid stack starts in ~60 seconds. Use health check polling before running tests.

31.5 Option 3 — Bare-Metal / VM Production Cluster

Recommended Node Layout

RoleCountInstance Type (AWS)Key Config
Master (Coordinator + Overlord)2m5.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 32 GB)asOverlord.enabled=true, 8 GB heap
Query (Broker + Router)3m5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 64 GB)25 GB heap, 12 GB direct, behind ALB
Data (Historical)10+r5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 128 GB)25 GB heap, 12 GB direct, 500 GB SSD cache
Ingestion (MiddleManager)5+m5.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 32 GB)4 Peon slots, 2 GB heap per Peon
ZooKeeper3t3.medium (2 vCPU, 4 GB)Dedicated nodes, SSD storage
Metadata (RDS)1 (Multi-AZ)db.r5.largePostgreSQL 15, encrypted, automated backups
common.runtime.properties — Shared Across All Nodesproperties
# ═══ Extensions ═══
druid.extensions.loadList=[
  "druid-kafka-indexing-service",
  "druid-s3-extensions",
  "druid-datasketches",
  "druid-basic-security",
  "prometheus-emitter"
]
 
# ═══ ZooKeeper ═══
druid.zk.service.host=zk-1:2181,zk-2:2181,zk-3:2181
druid.zk.paths.base=/druid
 
# ═══ Metadata Store (PostgreSQL on RDS) ═══
druid.metadata.storage.type=postgresql
druid.metadata.storage.connector.connectURI=jdbc:postgresql://druid-meta.xxxxx.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/druid
druid.metadata.storage.connector.user=druid_admin
druid.metadata.storage.connector.password=${METADATA_PASSWORD}
 
# ═══ Deep Storage (S3) ═══
druid.storage.type=s3
druid.storage.bucket=my-druid-segments
druid.storage.baseKey=druid/segments
druid.s3.accessKey=${AWS_ACCESS_KEY}
druid.s3.secretKey=${AWS_SECRET_KEY}
 
# ═══ Indexer Logs (S3) ═══
druid.indexer.logs.type=s3
druid.indexer.logs.s3Bucket=my-druid-segments
druid.indexer.logs.s3Prefix=druid/indexing-logs
 
# ═══ Monitoring ═══
druid.emitter=prometheus
druid.emitter.prometheus.port=9090
historical/runtime.properties — Per-Node Configproperties
druid.service=druid/historical
druid.plaintextPort=8083
 
# ═══ JVM (set in jvm.config) ═══
# -Xms25g -Xmx25g
# -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g
# -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational
 
# ═══ Processing ═══
druid.processing.buffer.sizeBytes=524288000
druid.processing.numThreads=15
druid.processing.numMergeBuffers=4
 
# ═══ Segment Cache (SSD) ═══
druid.segmentCache.locations=[{"path":"/mnt/ssd/druid/segments","maxSize":500000000000}]
druid.server.maxSize=500000000000
 
# ═══ Cache ═══
druid.historical.cache.useCache=true
druid.historical.cache.populateCache=true
druid.cache.type=caffeine
druid.cache.sizeInBytes=2147483648
historical/jvm.configconfig
-server
-Xms25g
-Xmx25g
-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g
-XX:+UseZGC
-XX:+ZGenerational
-Duser.timezone=UTC
-Dfile.encoding=UTF-8
-Djava.io.tmpdir=var/tmp
-Djava.util.logging.manager=org.apache.logging.log4j.jul.LogManager

31.6 Option 4 — Kubernetes with Helm (Production Recommended)

Figure 31.2 — K8s architecture: query/data/master namespaces + external RDS, S3, Kafka
Helm Install — Step by Stepbash
# ═══ Step 1: Add Druid Helm repo ═══
helm repo add druid https://apache.github.io/druid
helm repo update
 
# ═══ Step 2: Create namespace ═══
kubectl create namespace druid
 
# ═══ Step 3: Create secrets ═══
kubectl create secret generic druid-metadata \
  --namespace druid \
  --from-literal=password='your_rds_password'
 
kubectl create secret generic druid-s3 \
  --namespace druid \
  --from-literal=accessKey='AKIA...' \
  --from-literal=secretKey='xxxxx'
 
# ═══ Step 4: Install with custom values ═══
helm install druid-prod druid/druid \
  --namespace druid \
  --values values-production.yaml \
  --timeout 15m
 
# ═══ Step 5: Verify ═══
kubectl get pods -n druid -w
# Wait for all pods to be Running/Ready
 
kubectl port-forward svc/druid-prod-router 8888:8888 -n druid
# Console: http://localhost:8888
values-production.yaml — Helm ValuesYAML
# ═══ ZooKeeper ═══
zookeeper:
  enabled: true
  replicaCount: 3
  persistence:
    size: 20Gi
 
# ═══ Broker ═══
broker:
  replicaCount: 3
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "4", memory: "32Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "8", memory: "32Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms20g -Xmx20g -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=6g -XX:+UseZGC"
 
# ═══ Historical ═══
historical:
  replicaCount: 10
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "8", memory: "128Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "16", memory: "128Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms25g -Xmx25g -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational"
  persistence:
    enabled: true
    size: 500Gi
    storageClass: gp3-ssd
  env:
    - name: druid_processing_buffer_sizeBytes
      value: "524288000"
    - name: druid_processing_numThreads
      value: "15"
    - name: druid_processing_numMergeBuffers
      value: "4"
 
# ═══ MiddleManager ═══
middleManager:
  replicaCount: 5
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "4", memory: "32Gi" }
    limits:   { cpu: "8", memory: "32Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms1g -Xmx1g"
 
# ═══ Coordinator (with Overlord co-located) ═══
coordinator:
  replicaCount: 2
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "2", memory: "16Gi" }
  javaOpts: "-Xms8g -Xmx8g"
  env:
    - name: druid_coordinator_asOverlord_enabled
      value: "true"
 
# ═══ Router ═══
router:
  replicaCount: 2
  resources:
    requests: { cpu: "1", memory: "4Gi" }
 
# ═══ Common Config (all services) ═══
configVars:
  druid_metadata_storage_type: postgresql
  druid_metadata_storage_connector_connectURI: jdbc:postgresql://druid-meta.xxxxx.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/druid
  druid_storage_type: s3
  druid_storage_bucket: my-druid-segments
  druid_storage_baseKey: druid/segments
  druid_emitter: prometheus
  druid_emitter_prometheus_port: "9090"
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Historical Startup Probes New Historical pods need to download GBs of segments from S3 before serving queries. If your readiness probe fails before loading completes, K8s restarts the pod in a crash loop. Always use startupProbe with generous timeout (5+ minutes):

startupProbe: httpGet: /status/health, port: 8083, initialDelaySeconds: 120, periodSeconds: 10, failureThreshold: 30

31.7 Post-Setup Validation Checklist

Cluster Health Validation Scriptbash
#!/bin/bash — validate-druid-cluster.sh
 
ROUTER="http://druid-router:8888"
 
echo "=== 1. Service Health ==="
for svc in broker historical coordinator overlord router; do
  status=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" $ROUTER/proxy/$svc/status/health)
  echo "  $svc: $status"
done
 
echo "=== 2. Coordinator Leader ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/leader | jq .
 
echo "=== 3. Overlord Leader ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/indexer/v1/leader | jq .
 
echo "=== 4. Historical Nodes ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/servers?simple | jq '.[].host'
 
echo "=== 5. Test Ingestion (Wikipedia sample) ==="
curl -s -X POST $ROUTER/druid/indexer/v1/task \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @quickstart/tutorial/wikipedia-index.json | jq .task
 
echo "=== 6. Wait for ingestion (60s) ==="
sleep 60
 
echo "=== 7. Test Query ==="
curl -s -X POST $ROUTER/druid/v2/sql \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM wikipedia"}' | jq .
 
echo "=== 8. Segment Count ==="
curl -s $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/datasources/wikipedia | jq .segments.count
 
echo "=== ✅ Cluster Validation Complete ==="

Validation Checklist

CheckCommand / URLExpected
All services healthy/status/health on each servicetrue (HTTP 200)
Coordinator leader elected/druid/coordinator/v1/leaderReturns hostname
Overlord leader elected/druid/indexer/v1/leaderReturns hostname
Historicals registered/druid/coordinator/v1/servers?simpleLists all Historical hosts
Sample ingestion succeedsSubmit wikipedia-index taskTask completes with SUCCESS
Query returns dataSELECT COUNT(*) FROM wikipediaReturns row count > 0
Segments loaded/druid/coordinator/v1/loadqueueEmpty (all segments loaded)
Prometheus metrics:9090/metrics on any nodeReturns Prometheus text format
Console accessiblehttp://router:8888Druid Console UI loads

31.8 Setting Up Your First Kafka Ingestion

Submit Kafka Supervisor — Real-Time Streamingbash
# Submit a Kafka supervisor to start real-time ingestion
curl -X POST http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
  "type": "kafka",
  "spec": {
    "dataSchema": {
      "dataSource": "user_events",
      "timestampSpec": { "column": "event_time", "format": "iso" },
      "dimensionsSpec": {
        "dimensions": ["user_id", "event_type", "platform", "country"]
      },
      "metricsSpec": [
        { "type": "count", "name": "event_count" },
        { "type": "doubleSum", "name": "revenue_sum", "fieldName": "revenue" }
      ],
      "granularitySpec": {
        "segmentGranularity": "HOUR",
        "queryGranularity": "MINUTE",
        "rollup": true
      }
    },
    "ioConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "consumerProperties": {
        "bootstrap.servers": "kafka-broker-1:9092,kafka-broker-2:9092"
      },
      "topic": "user_events_prod",
      "taskCount": 4,
      "replicas": 2,
      "taskDuration": "PT1H"
    },
    "tuningConfig": {
      "type": "kafka",
      "maxRowsPerSegment": 5000000,
      "maxRowsInMemory": 1000000
    }
  }
}'
 
# Check supervisor status
curl -s http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor/user_events/status | jq .
 
# Check Kafka lag
curl -s http://druid-router:8888/druid/indexer/v1/supervisor/user_events/stats | jq .

31.9 OS-Level Tuning for Production

Linux Kernel Tuning for Druid Historicalsbash
# ═══ Maximize memory-mapped file performance ═══
# Druid Historicals rely heavily on mmap for segment files
 
# Increase max memory map areas (default 65536 is too low for large clusters)
echo "vm.max_map_count=262144" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
 
# Reduce swappiness — keep segment pages in RAM
echo "vm.swappiness=1" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
 
# Increase file descriptor limits (Druid opens many segment files)
echo "* soft nofile 65536" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
echo "* hard nofile 131072" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
 
# Increase max processes
echo "* soft nproc 65536" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
 
# Apply
sysctl -p
 
# ═══ Disable Transparent Huge Pages (THP) ═══
# THP causes unpredictable latency spikes with mmap-heavy workloads
echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag
 
# ═══ Verify ═══
sysctl vm.max_map_count
sysctl vm.swappiness
cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max
cat /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through setting up a production Druid cluster." — Key steps: (1) Provision external deps: RDS PostgreSQL (Multi-AZ), S3 bucket (SSE-KMS), ZooKeeper (3-node). (2) Deploy via Helm on K8s with separate node pools per component type. (3) Configure: extensions list, metadata URI, S3 creds, processing threads/buffers per node type. (4) OS tuning: max_map_count=262144, swappiness=1, disable THP, high file descriptor limits. (5) Validate: health checks, sample ingestion, query test. (6) Set up monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana), alerting, and Kafka supervisor for real-time data. The most common mistake: undersizing Historical page cache by over-allocating JVM heap.

Section 31 — Key Takeaways

TopicKey Point
Local dev./bin/start-druid — all-in-one JVM, 5 min. Derby + local storage. Never for production.
Docker Compose7 containers (ZK, Postgres, Coord, Overlord, Historical, MM, Broker, Router). Good for CI/CD.
Bare-metalSeparate nodes per role. common.runtime.properties shared. Per-node JVM config for heap/direct.
KubernetesHelm chart. StatefulSets for Historical/MM/ZK. Deployments for Broker/Router/Coordinator.
External depsRDS Multi-AZ (metadata), S3 SSE-KMS (deep storage), MSK/Kafka (ingestion). Never run MySQL on K8s.
OS tuningmax_map_count=262144, swappiness=1, disable THP, file descriptors 65536+.
ValidationHealth check all services → sample ingest → query → check segments loaded → Prometheus metrics.
Part 12 — Section 32

Multi-VM Druid Cluster — Step-by-Step Deployment

Complete walkthrough deploying Druid across 10+ VMs — IP planning, per-node installation, configuration files for every role, startup order, networking, firewall rules, and end-to-end validation.

32 — Multi-VM Druid Cluster Setup

32.1 Reference Cluster Topology — 13-Node Layout

This section walks through deploying a production-grade 13-node Druid cluster across dedicated VMs (AWS EC2, GCP Compute, Azure VMs, or bare-metal). Every IP address, every config file, and every startup command is provided.

HostnamePrivate IPRoleSpec (AWS)Disk
zk-110.0.1.11ZooKeeper node 1t3.medium (2 vCPU, 4 GB)50 GB SSD
zk-210.0.1.12ZooKeeper node 2t3.medium50 GB SSD
zk-310.0.1.13ZooKeeper node 3t3.medium50 GB SSD
master-110.0.2.11Coordinator + Overlord (leader)m5.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 32 GB)100 GB SSD
master-210.0.2.12Coordinator + Overlord (standby)m5.2xlarge100 GB SSD
broker-110.0.3.11Broker + Routerm5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 64 GB)50 GB SSD
broker-210.0.3.12Broker + Routerm5.4xlarge50 GB SSD
data-110.0.4.11Historical (hot tier)r5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 128 GB)500 GB NVMe SSD
data-210.0.4.12Historical (hot tier)r5.4xlarge500 GB NVMe SSD
data-310.0.4.13Historical (hot tier)r5.4xlarge500 GB NVMe SSD
ingest-110.0.5.11MiddleManagerm5.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 32 GB)200 GB SSD
ingest-210.0.5.12MiddleManagerm5.2xlarge200 GB SSD
ingest-310.0.5.13MiddleManagerm5.2xlarge200 GB SSD

External managed services (not deployed on these VMs):

  • Metadata Store: Amazon RDS PostgreSQL 15 — Multi-AZ, db.r5.large, encrypted, endpoint: druid-meta.xxxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432
  • Deep Storage: S3 bucket company-druid-segments with SSE-KMS enabled
  • Kafka: Amazon MSK or self-managed Kafka cluster (separate VMs)
Figure 32.1 — 13-node multi-VM topology: separate subnets per role, ALB for query traffic, external RDS + S3

32.2 Firewall Rules / Security Groups

SourceDestinationPortPurpose
ALB (public)broker-1, broker-28888Router/Console traffic from users
broker-*data-*, ingest-*8083, 8091Broker → Historical (query), MM (real-time query)
master-*data-*8083Coordinator → Historical (load/drop instructions)
master-*ingest-*8091, 8100–8199Overlord → MM (task assignment), Peon ports
All Druid nodeszk-1, zk-2, zk-32181ZooKeeper client connections
zk-*zk-*2888, 3888ZK inter-node (leader election, replication)
All Druid nodesRDS endpoint5432Metadata store (PostgreSQL)
data-*, ingest-*S3 (VPC endpoint)443Segment push/pull via HTTPS
ingest-*Kafka brokers9092Kafka consumer connections
PrometheusAll Druid nodes9090Metrics scrape
⚠️ Common Pitfall Forgetting to open Peon ports 8100–8199 from Overlord/Broker to MiddleManagers. Each Peon JVM binds to a unique port in this range. If these are blocked, Overlord can't monitor tasks and Broker can't query real-time data on MiddleManagers. Always open a port range, not just 8091.

32.3 Step 1 — Install Druid on All 10 Druid Nodes

Run on EVERY Druid VM (master-*, broker-*, data-*, ingest-*)bash
#!/bin/bash — install-druid.sh (run on each VM)
 
# ═══ 1. Install JDK 17 ═══
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y openjdk-17-jdk-headless
java -version  # Verify: openjdk 17.x
 
# ═══ 2. Create druid user ═══
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash druid
sudo mkdir -p /opt/druid /mnt/ssd/druid/segments /var/log/druid
sudo chown -R druid:druid /opt/druid /mnt/ssd/druid /var/log/druid
 
# ═══ 3. Download and extract Druid ═══
DRUID_VERSION=30.0.1
cd /opt/druid
curl -O https://dlcdn.apache.org/druid/${DRUID_VERSION}/apache-druid-${DRUID_VERSION}-bin.tar.gz
tar -xzf apache-druid-${DRUID_VERSION}-bin.tar.gz --strip-components=1
rm apache-druid-${DRUID_VERSION}-bin.tar.gz
 
# ═══ 4. OS tuning (all nodes) ═══
sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf <<EOF
vm.max_map_count=262144
vm.swappiness=1
EOF
sudo sysctl -p
 
sudo tee -a /etc/security/limits.conf <<EOF
druid soft nofile 65536
druid hard nofile 131072
druid soft nproc 65536
EOF
 
# Disable THP
echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag
 
# ═══ 5. Create config directories ═══
mkdir -p /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/_common
mkdir -p /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/master
mkdir -p /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/query
mkdir -p /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/data/historical
mkdir -p /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/data/middleManager
 
chown -R druid:druid /opt/druid

32.4 Step 2 — Setup ZooKeeper Ensemble (zk-1, zk-2, zk-3)

Run on zk-1, zk-2, zk-3bash
# ═══ Install ZooKeeper ═══
ZK_VERSION=3.8.4
cd /opt
curl -O https://dlcdn.apache.org/zookeeper/zookeeper-${ZK_VERSION}/apache-zookeeper-${ZK_VERSION}-bin.tar.gz
tar -xzf apache-zookeeper-${ZK_VERSION}-bin.tar.gz
ln -s apache-zookeeper-${ZK_VERSION}-bin zookeeper
 
# ═══ Create data directory ═══
mkdir -p /var/lib/zookeeper/data
 
# ═══ Set myid (UNIQUE per node) ═══
# On zk-1:
echo 1 > /var/lib/zookeeper/data/myid
# On zk-2:
echo 2 > /var/lib/zookeeper/data/myid
# On zk-3:
echo 3 > /var/lib/zookeeper/data/myid
/opt/zookeeper/conf/zoo.cfg — Same on all 3 ZK nodesconfig
tickTime=2000
initLimit=10
syncLimit=5
dataDir=/var/lib/zookeeper/data
clientPort=2181
maxClientCnxns=100
autopurge.snapRetainCount=5
autopurge.purgeInterval=1
 
# Ensemble members (same on all 3)
server.1=10.0.1.11:2888:3888
server.2=10.0.1.12:2888:3888
server.3=10.0.1.13:2888:3888
Start ZooKeeper on all 3 nodes + Verifybash
# Start (run on each zk node)
/opt/zookeeper/bin/zkServer.sh start
 
# Verify ensemble formed
/opt/zookeeper/bin/zkServer.sh status
# Should show: Mode: leader (on one) and Mode: follower (on others)
 
# Test connectivity from any Druid node
echo ruok | nc 10.0.1.11 2181
# Should return: imok

32.5 Step 3 — Common Configuration (All 10 Druid Nodes)

conf/druid/cluster/_common/common.runtime.propertiesproperties
# ═══ Extensions ═══
druid.extensions.loadList=["druid-kafka-indexing-service","druid-s3-extensions","druid-datasketches","druid-basic-security","prometheus-emitter"]
 
# ═══ ZooKeeper ═══
druid.zk.service.host=10.0.1.11:2181,10.0.1.12:2181,10.0.1.13:2181
druid.zk.paths.base=/druid
 
# ═══ Metadata Store ═══
druid.metadata.storage.type=postgresql
druid.metadata.storage.connector.connectURI=jdbc:postgresql://druid-meta.xxxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/druid
druid.metadata.storage.connector.user=druid_admin
druid.metadata.storage.connector.password=${DRUID_METADATA_PASSWORD}
 
# ═══ Deep Storage (S3) ═══
druid.storage.type=s3
druid.storage.bucket=company-druid-segments
druid.storage.baseKey=druid/segments
 
# ═══ Indexer Logs (S3) ═══
druid.indexer.logs.type=s3
druid.indexer.logs.s3Bucket=company-druid-segments
druid.indexer.logs.s3Prefix=druid/indexing-logs
 
# ═══ Monitoring ═══
druid.emitter=prometheus
druid.emitter.prometheus.port=9090
 
# ═══ Request Logging ═══
druid.request.logging.type=file
druid.request.logging.dir=/var/log/druid/requests
⚙️ Engineering Insight Copy this _common/common.runtime.properties file identically to all 10 Druid VMs. Use scp, Ansible, or a shared NFS mount. The per-role differences are in role-specific config files, not this common file. Any mismatch causes "unknown type" deserialization errors.

32.6 Step 4 — Master Nodes (master-1, master-2)

conf/druid/cluster/master/runtime.propertiesproperties
druid.service=druid/coordinator
druid.plaintextPort=8081
 
# ═══ Co-locate Overlord in same JVM ═══
druid.coordinator.asOverlord.enabled=true
druid.coordinator.asOverlord.overlordService=druid/overlord
 
# ═══ Segment management ═══
druid.coordinator.period=PT60S
druid.coordinator.startDelay=PT30S
druid.coordinator.merge.on=false
conf/druid/cluster/master/jvm.configconfig
-server
-Xms8g
-Xmx8g
-XX:+UseG1GC
-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=2g
-Duser.timezone=UTC
-Dfile.encoding=UTF-8
-Djava.io.tmpdir=/opt/druid/var/tmp

32.7 Step 5 — Query Nodes (broker-1, broker-2)

conf/druid/cluster/query/broker/runtime.propertiesproperties
druid.service=druid/broker
druid.plaintextPort=8082
 
# ═══ Processing ═══
druid.processing.buffer.sizeBytes=524288000
druid.processing.numThreads=11
druid.processing.numMergeBuffers=4
 
# ═══ HTTP ═══
druid.server.http.numThreads=25
druid.broker.http.numConnections=20
 
# ═══ Caching ═══
druid.broker.cache.useCache=true
druid.broker.cache.populateCache=true
druid.broker.cache.useResultLevelCache=true
druid.broker.cache.populateResultLevelCache=true
druid.cache.type=caffeine
druid.cache.sizeInBytes=2147483648
conf/druid/cluster/query/broker/jvm.configconfig
-server
-Xms20g
-Xmx20g
-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=10g
-XX:+UseZGC
-Duser.timezone=UTC
conf/druid/cluster/query/router/runtime.propertiesproperties
druid.service=druid/router
druid.plaintextPort=8888
 
druid.router.defaultBrokerServiceName=druid/broker
druid.router.coordinatorServiceName=druid/coordinator
druid.router.managementProxy.enabled=true

32.8 Step 6 — Data Nodes (data-1, data-2, data-3)

conf/druid/cluster/data/historical/runtime.propertiesproperties
druid.service=druid/historical
druid.plaintextPort=8083
 
# ═══ Processing ═══
druid.processing.buffer.sizeBytes=524288000
druid.processing.numThreads=15
druid.processing.numMergeBuffers=4
 
# ═══ Segment Cache (NVMe SSD) ═══
druid.segmentCache.locations=[{"path":"/mnt/ssd/druid/segments","maxSize":450000000000}]
druid.server.maxSize=450000000000
 
# ═══ Query Result Cache ═══
druid.historical.cache.useCache=true
druid.historical.cache.populateCache=true
druid.cache.type=caffeine
druid.cache.sizeInBytes=2147483648
 
# ═══ Tier (for tiered storage rules) ═══
druid.server.tier=hot
conf/druid/cluster/data/historical/jvm.configconfig
-server
-Xms25g
-Xmx25g
-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=12g
-XX:+UseZGC
-XX:+ZGenerational
-Duser.timezone=UTC

32.9 Step 7 — Ingestion Nodes (ingest-1, ingest-2, ingest-3)

conf/druid/cluster/data/middleManager/runtime.propertiesproperties
druid.service=druid/middleManager
druid.plaintextPort=8091
 
# ═══ Worker capacity ═══
druid.worker.capacity=4          # 4 Peon slots per MM
 
# ═══ Peon JVM config ═══
druid.indexer.runner.javaOptsArray=["-server","-Xms2g","-Xmx2g","-XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=1g","-XX:+UseG1GC","-Duser.timezone=UTC"]
 
# ═══ Peon port range ═══
druid.indexer.runner.startPort=8100
druid.indexer.runner.endPort=8199
 
# ═══ Task storage ═══
druid.indexer.task.baseTaskDir=/mnt/ssd/druid/task-storage
conf/druid/cluster/data/middleManager/jvm.configconfig
-server
-Xms1g
-Xmx1g
-XX:+UseG1GC
-Duser.timezone=UTC

32.10 Step 8 — Startup Order (Critical!)

Druid processes must start in a specific order. Starting a Broker before ZooKeeper or Coordinator results in connection failures and missing segment topology.

OrderNodesCommand (as druid user)Wait For
1zk-1, zk-2, zk-3/opt/zookeeper/bin/zkServer.sh startAll 3 form quorum (Mode: leader/follower)
2master-1, master-2bin/run-druid coordinator conf/druid/clusterLeader elected (/status/leader returns hostname)
3data-1, data-2, data-3bin/run-druid historical conf/druid/clusterRegistered in ZK (/druid/announcements)
4ingest-1, ingest-2, ingest-3bin/run-druid middleManager conf/druid/clusterWorkers visible in Overlord API
5broker-1, broker-2bin/run-druid broker conf/druid/cluster/status/health returns true
6broker-1, broker-2bin/run-druid router conf/druid/clusterConsole at :8888 accessible
Systemd Service File (run as daemon)systemd
# /etc/systemd/system/druid-historical.service
# (change ExecStart for each role)
[Unit]
Description=Apache Druid Historical
After=network.target
 
[Service]
Type=simple
User=druid
Group=druid
WorkingDirectory=/opt/druid
ExecStart=/opt/druid/bin/run-druid historical /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=30
LimitNOFILE=65536
Environment="DRUID_METADATA_PASSWORD=your_password_here"
 
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
Enable and Start Servicesbash
# On each Historical node (data-1, data-2, data-3):
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable druid-historical
sudo systemctl start druid-historical
sudo systemctl status druid-historical
 
# Check logs
journalctl -u druid-historical -f
⚠️ Common Pitfall Starting Broker before Historicals register in ZK. If Broker starts and ZK has no segment announcements yet, the Broker's segment timeline is empty — all queries return empty results until Historicals announce. Always wait for Historicals to fully start (check /druid/coordinator/v1/servers) before starting Brokers.

32.11 Step 9 — Full Cluster Validation

Validate Every Component — Run from any machinebash
ROUTER="http://10.0.3.11:8888"  # or ALB endpoint
 
echo "=== ZooKeeper ==="
for zk in 10.0.1.11 10.0.1.12 10.0.1.13; do
  echo "  $zk: $(echo ruok | nc $zk 2181)"
done
 
echo "=== Service Health ==="
curl -sf $ROUTER/status/health && echo " Router OK"
curl -sf http://10.0.3.11:8082/status/health && echo " Broker-1 OK"
curl -sf http://10.0.3.12:8082/status/health && echo " Broker-2 OK"
curl -sf http://10.0.2.11:8081/status/health && echo " Master-1 OK"
curl -sf http://10.0.2.12:8081/status/health && echo " Master-2 OK"
 
echo "=== Leaders ==="
echo "  Coordinator: $(curl -sf $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/leader)"
echo "  Overlord:    $(curl -sf $ROUTER/druid/indexer/v1/leader)"
 
echo "=== Historical Nodes ==="
curl -sf $ROUTER/druid/coordinator/v1/servers?simple | python3 -m json.tool
 
echo "=== Worker Nodes (MM) ==="
curl -sf $ROUTER/druid/indexer/v1/workers | python3 -m json.tool
 
echo "=== Test Query ==="
curl -sf -X POST $ROUTER/druid/v2/sql \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"SELECT 1 + 1 AS result"}'
 
echo "=== Prometheus Metrics ==="
curl -sf http://10.0.4.11:9090/metrics | head -5
 
echo "=== ✅ All checks complete ==="

32.12 Automation with Ansible (Optional)

Ansible Inventory — hosts.ymlYAML
all:
  children:
    zookeeper:
      hosts:
        zk-1: { ansible_host: 10.0.1.11, zk_id: 1 }
        zk-2: { ansible_host: 10.0.1.12, zk_id: 2 }
        zk-3: { ansible_host: 10.0.1.13, zk_id: 3 }
    druid_master:
      hosts:
        master-1: { ansible_host: 10.0.2.11 }
        master-2: { ansible_host: 10.0.2.12 }
    druid_broker:
      hosts:
        broker-1: { ansible_host: 10.0.3.11 }
        broker-2: { ansible_host: 10.0.3.12 }
    druid_historical:
      hosts:
        data-1: { ansible_host: 10.0.4.11 }
        data-2: { ansible_host: 10.0.4.12 }
        data-3: { ansible_host: 10.0.4.13 }
    druid_middlemanager:
      hosts:
        ingest-1: { ansible_host: 10.0.5.11 }
        ingest-2: { ansible_host: 10.0.5.12 }
        ingest-3: { ansible_host: 10.0.5.13 }
    druid_all:
      children:
        druid_master:
        druid_broker:
        druid_historical:
        druid_middlemanager:
Ansible Playbook — deploy-druid.yml (simplified)YAML
---
- name: Install Druid on all nodes
  hosts: druid_all
  become: yes
  tasks:
    - name: Install JDK 17
      apt: { name: openjdk-17-jdk-headless, state: present }
 
    - name: Create druid user
      user: { name: druid, shell: /bin/bash }
 
    - name: Download and extract Druid
      unarchive:
        src: https://dlcdn.apache.org/druid/30.0.1/apache-druid-30.0.1-bin.tar.gz
        dest: /opt/druid
        remote_src: yes
        extra_opts: ["--strip-components=1"]
 
    - name: Deploy common config
      template:
        src: templates/common.runtime.properties.j2
        dest: /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/_common/common.runtime.properties
 
    - name: Apply OS tuning
      sysctl: { name: "{{ item.key }}", value: "{{ item.value }}", sysctl_set: yes }
      loop:
        - { key: vm.max_map_count, value: "262144" }
        - { key: vm.swappiness, value: "1" }
 
- name: Deploy Historical config
  hosts: druid_historical
  become: yes
  tasks:
    - name: Deploy Historical runtime.properties
      template:
        src: templates/historical.runtime.properties.j2
        dest: /opt/druid/conf/druid/cluster/data/historical/runtime.properties
    - name: Enable systemd service
      systemd: { name: druid-historical, enabled: yes, state: started }
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you deploy Druid across multiple VMs?" — Key decisions: (1) Separate subnets per role with security group rules allowing only required ports. (2) External managed deps: RDS for metadata (Multi-AZ), S3 for deep storage — never run MySQL on the same VMs as Druid. (3) Startup order matters: ZK → Master → Historical → MM → Broker → Router. (4) Identical common config on all nodes; role-specific config per node type. (5) systemd for process management with auto-restart. (6) Use Ansible/Terraform for reproducibility — never hand-configure production nodes.

Section 32 — Key Takeaways

TopicKey Point
Topology13 nodes: 3 ZK + 2 master + 2 broker + 3 historical + 3 MM. External RDS + S3.
NetworkingSeparate subnets per role. Open ZK:2181, Coord:8081, Broker:8082, Hist:8083, MM:8091+8100–8199.
Common configOne common.runtime.properties copied identically to all 10 Druid VMs.
Per-role configSeparate runtime.properties + jvm.config per role (master/broker/historical/MM).
Startup orderZK → Master (Coord+Overlord) → Historical → MM → Broker → Router. Always this order.
SystemdRun each Druid service as systemd unit for auto-restart and proper log management.
AutomationAnsible inventory + playbook for reproducible multi-node deployment.
Appendix A

Druid vs the Broader OLAP Ecosystem

Head-to-head comparison matrices — Druid vs ClickHouse, Pinot, Elasticsearch, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Spark — plus a system selection decision tree.

Appendix A — OLAP Ecosystem Comparison Tables

A.1 Druid vs ClickHouse vs Pinot vs Elasticsearch

DimensionApache DruidClickHouseApache PinotElasticsearch
OriginMetamarkets (2011)Yandex (2016)LinkedIn (2013)Elastic (2010)
Primary UseReal-time OLAP dashboardsGeneral OLAP analyticsUser-facing analyticsFull-text search + logs
ArchitectureDistributed, many service typesSharded, fewer servicesDistributed, Helix-managedDistributed, Lucene-based
Query LatencySub-second P99Sub-second to secondsSub-second P99Seconds for aggregations
Concurrency100+ concurrent~50–100100K QPS (user-facing)Good for search, limited agg
IngestionNative Kafka supervisorKafka engine, HTTPReal-time + offline tablesLogstash, Beats, API
RollupFirst-class, ingestion-timeAggregatingMergeTreeStar-tree index (unique)No native rollup
JOIN supportLimited (broadcast, lookups)Full SQL JOINsV2 MSE multi-stageVery limited
UpsertNo (append-only)ALTER TABLE UPDATEYes (full/partial)Full document update
Full-text searchLimitedLimitedLucene-based text indexExcellent (core feature)
Ops ComplexityHigh (many services, ZK)MediumHigh (Helix, ZK)Medium
Approx. analyticsHLL, Theta, Quantilesuniq, quantileTDigestHLL, Theta, percentilecardinality, percentiles
Best forTime-series, unified RT+historicalGeneral analytics, wide SQLUser-facing at 100K QPSLog search, documents

A.2 Druid vs Cloud Data Warehouses

DimensionApache DruidSnowflakeBigQueryRedshift Serverless
DeploymentSelf-managed / ImplyFully managed SaaSFully managed SaaSFully managed SaaS
Query LatencySub-second P992–30 seconds2–60 secondsSub-second to seconds
Concurrency100+~50 per warehouse~100~100
Real-time FreshnessSeconds (Kafka)Minutes (Snowpipe)MinutesMinutes
SQL CompletenessLimitedFull ANSI SQLFull ANSI SQLFull ANSI SQL
Data MutationsAppend-onlyFull DMLFull DMLFull DML
Cost ModelInfra (compute+storage)Per-second warehousePer-query (bytes)RPU-based
Best forRT dashboards, high concurrencyAd-hoc, data scienceAd-hoc at scaleAWS-native analytics

A.3 Druid vs Apache Spark

DimensionApache DruidApache Spark SQL
Primary UseSub-second interactive OLAPBatch ETL, ML, transformations
Query Latency50–500 ms P995s – 30 min
Concurrency100+ simultaneousLimited (job-level resources)
Real-timeNative Kafka supervisorStructured Streaming (micro-batch)
JOINsLimitedFull (SMJ, BHJ, SHJ)
StorageOwn columnar segmentsParquet/Delta/Iceberg
RelationshipComplementary: Spark writes to lake → Druid serves from Kafka + lake

A.4 System Selection Decision Tree

Figure A.1 — Decision tree: sub-second → Druid/Pinot | Full SQL → Snowflake/ClickHouse | Search → Elasticsearch
🎯 Interview Tip "When would you choose Druid over ClickHouse?" — Druid when: (1) native Kafka streaming with exactly-once, (2) 100+ concurrent dashboards, (3) unified real-time + historical. ClickHouse when: (1) full SQL JOINs needed, (2) in-place mutations, (3) operational simplicity matters more.
Appendix B

The Complete Query Story — Dashboard Click to Result

End-to-end journey of a single Druid query — every phase timed, every component traced, from Grafana refresh to chart render in ~510 ms.

Appendix B — The Complete Query Story

B.1 End-to-End Query Gantt Timeline

The query: SELECT TIME_FLOOR(__time,'PT1H') AS hour, country, SUM(revenue_sum) AS rev FROM user_events WHERE __time >= NOW()-7d AND platform='mobile' GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY rev DESC LIMIT 100

Figure B.1 — Parallel Historical scans dominate. Straggler node sets final latency.

B.2 Phase-by-Phase Breakdown

PhaseComponentDurationWhat Happens
1Client → Broker<1 msGrafana POSTs SQL to Broker
2Broker: Parse3–5 msCalcite parses → validates → translates to native GroupBy
3Broker: Cache<1 msL2 result cache check → HIT? Return. MISS? Continue.
4Broker: Prune1–2 msZK timeline: 7 daily segments → 3 Historicals
5Broker: Fan-out<1 msDispatch sub-queries to 3 Historicals (async HTTP)
6aHistorical: Bitmap<1 msLookup platform_mobile bitmap → matching row indices
6bHistorical: Scan50–400 msRead __time, country, revenue_sum for matched rows only
7Network Return5–20 msPartial results (Smile binary) → HTTP back to Broker
8Broker: Merge5–50 msMerge 3 hash tables → SUM sums → ORDER BY → LIMIT
9Broker → Client5–20 msJSON serialize → HTTP 200 → Grafana renders chart
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Where Time Actually Goes Phase 6b (Historical column scan) = 70–90% of total query time. Everything else is noise at <50ms. Two levers: (1) Better rollup = fewer rows. (2) Warm OS page cache = segments in RAM, no disk I/O. This is why Historical memory sizing (80%+ as page cache) is the #1 tuning knob.

B.3 The 30-Second Mental Model

When a dashboard fires a query, the Broker receives SQL, Calcite translates to native GroupBy, checks L2 cache. On miss, Broker prunes to relevant segments via ZK timeline, fans out sub-queries to Historicals in parallel. Each Historical evaluates WHERE via Roaring Bitmap AND (nanoseconds), reads only required columns, builds partial hash table, ships back. Broker merges partials, sorts, limits, caches, serializes JSON. Chart renders in under a second on terabytes of data.

B.4 Failure Modes

FailureUser SeesInternal CauseFix
Straggler8s instead of 500msGC pause or cold segmentsZGC. Warm cache. Add replicas.
Segment unavailablePartial/errorHistorical down, no replicaReplication ≥ 2
Handoff gapMissing last 30–60sMM committed, Historical loadingNormal — wait for handoff
Timeout504Too many segments / OOMAdd __time filter. Add LIMIT.
Broker OOM503 / crashUnbounded GroupBymaxMergingDictionarySize. LIMIT.
Appendix C

Capacity Planning and Cost Optimization

Sizing formulas for every Druid component, cost distribution breakdown, and spot instance strategies.

Appendix C — Capacity Planning and Cost Optimization

C.1 Sizing Formulas

ComponentFormulaExample
Historical count(daily_data × retention_days × replication) / cache_per_node(50GB × 365 × 2) / 500GB = 73
Historical RAMcache + heap + direct + os_reserve500GB cache → 25GB heap + 12GB direct + 90GB page cache = 128GB node
MiddleManager count(kafka_partitions / tasks_per_mm) × replicas(32 / 4) × 2 = 16
MiddleManager RAM(peon_slots × peon_heap) + mm_overhead4 × 2GB + 1GB = ~9GB
Broker countpeak_concurrent / queries_per_broker300 / 75 = 4
Direct memorybuffer × (threads + mergeBuffers + 1)500MB × (15+4+1) = 10GB → MaxDirect=12g

C.2 Cost Distribution

Component% CostOptimization
Historical compute~50%Better rollup, tiered storage, spot for cold tier
Deep Storage (S3)~15%Higher rollup, Zstd compression, S3 lifecycle IA
MiddleManager~15%Right-size peon heap, use Indexer mode
Broker~8%Result-level cache, spot instances
Metadata (RDS)~5%Smallest instance meeting IOPS, reserved
ZK / Network~4%K8s extensions eliminate ZK
Monitoring~3%Local retention 15d, downsample old metrics

C.3 Spot Instance Strategy

ComponentSpot-Safe?Rationale
Broker✅ ExcellentStateless. LB handles failover.
Historical (cold/warm)✅ GoodData in S3. Loss = re-download only. Replication=2.
Historical (hot)⚠️ CautionRe-download hot segments = latency spike.
MM (batch tasks)✅ GoodBatch tasks idempotent.
MM (streaming)❌ AvoidKafka task loss = offset rollback.
Coordinator/Overlord❌ AvoidLeader election churn destabilizes.
Appendix D

Joins, Lookups, and Denormalization Patterns

Join strategy decision matrix, lookup configuration, and denormalization best practices for Druid data modeling.

Appendix D — Joins, Lookups, and Denormalization

D.1 Join Strategy Decision Matrix

ScenarioBest StrategyLatencyComplexity
Enrich with small dim (<100K rows)Lookup (in-memory key→value)Sub-msLow
Enrich with medium dim (<10M rows)Broadcast hash JOIN100–500 msMedium
JOIN two large Druid datasourcesAvoid — denormalize at ingestionN/ALow (ETL side)
Set operations (A ∩ B, A \ B)Theta Sketch set opsSub-secondMedium
Complex multi-table analytical JOINTrino federationSeconds–minMedium
One-time backfill report JOINMSQ multi-stage5–60 sLow

D.2 Lookup Configuration

Register a JDBC LookupJSON
// POST /druid/coordinator/v1/lookups/config/__default/country_names
{
  "version": "2024-03-31",
  "lookupExtractorFactory": {
    "type": "cachedNamespace",
    "extractionNamespace": {
      "type": "jdbc",
      "connectorConfig": {
        "connectURI": "jdbc:postgresql://dim-db:5432/dimensions",
        "user": "druid_lookup",
        "password": "${LOOKUP_DB_PASSWORD}"
      },
      "table": "countries",
      "keyColumn": "country_code",
      "valueColumn": "country_name",
      "pollPeriod": "PT1H"
    }
  }
}
Using Lookups in SQLSQL
SELECT
    LOOKUP(country_code, 'country_names') AS country_name,
    SUM(revenue_sum) AS total_revenue
FROM "user_events"
WHERE __time >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC

D.3 Denormalization Checklist

  • Always denormalize dimension attributes into the fact table at ingestion (via Kafka Streams, Flink, or Druid transformSpec).
  • Use lookups only for dimensions that change frequently (e.g., product price, user tier) — lookups are refreshed on a poll interval.
  • Never JOIN two large Druid datasources in dashboard queries — denormalize or use Theta Sketch set operations.
  • Theta Sketch for set algebra: "users who did X AND Y" = intersection of two sketches — no JOIN needed.
Appendix F

Performance Tuning — Impact Hierarchy

5-layer tuning pyramid (highest impact at bottom) and critical configuration reference for production clusters.

Appendix F — Performance Tuning Quick Reference

F.1 Tuning Pyramid — Highest Impact First

Layer (bottom = highest)What to TuneSpeedup
1. Data ModelingRollup ratio, queryGranularity, dimension cardinality, sketch choiceUp to 100×
2. Segment DesignOptimal size (300–700 MB), compaction, hash/range partitioning10–50×
3. Ingestion TuningtaskCount, maxRowsInMemory, taskDuration, intermediatePersist2–5× throughput
4. Query OptimizationTime filter, TopN trigger, sketches, filtered aggs, EXPLAIN PLAN2–10× per query
5. Cluster ResourcesHistorical memory, processing threads, broker cache, ZGC1.5–3× concurrency

F.2 Critical Configuration Reference

ConfigComponentDefaultProduction
druid.processing.numThreadsHistorical1num_cores - 1
druid.processing.buffer.sizeBytesHistorical1 GB500 MB
druid.processing.numMergeBuffersHistorical24 (high concurrency)
druid.server.maxSizeHistorical0Match segmentCache total
druid.broker.cache.useResultLevelCacheBrokerfalsetrue
druid.cache.sizeInBytesHistorical02 GB (L1 cache)
maxRowsPerSegmentIngestion5M5M (300–700 MB target)
taskDurationKafka SupervisorPT1HPT2H (better rollup)
skipOffsetFromLatestCompactionP1DP1D–P14D
druid.coordinator.periodCoordinatorPT60SPT60S
